### cobol file sort

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2004-01-01T06:20:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0401010620.427dda41@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I am trying to produce a sorted report using the report writer.
I am using the Fujitsu compiler.

I am trying to read a file, sort it and then make a report from the
sorted file.
I need to exclude certain records from the file (say: numbers less
than 100..)
I want to use input & output procedures for my sort rather than using
& giving.


For the "OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS sort-output.", would it be better to have
this calling "REPORT-PARAGRAPH." or somehow
produce the report from the output procedure?  This is what I ideally
want to do.
I have tried a couple of ways but I can't get it right.
 
I'm not sure where i'm going wrong.  
I have done sorts before with 'USING' and 'GIVING' but not with imput
& output procedures.
If anyone could help me understand the differences between USING &
GIVING - and input &
output procedures that would be great.

Any help would be much aprechiated,

Thanks again,
Ritchie.
 
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~CODE START~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
 000120	INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000130
000140	FILE-CONTROL.
000150		SELECT detail-file ASSIGN TO "detail.dat"
000160			ORGANIZATION IS Indexed
000170		        ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
			...
	*sort work file
000210          SELECT sort-file ASSIGN TO DUMMY. 
	*sorted file
000211		SELECT sorted-file ASSIGN TO "sorted.dat".
	*report file
000220		SELECT report-file ASSIGN TO "report.dat"	.
.........

001502***********SORT****************************
001503	SORT-PARA.
001504	   SORT sort-file ON ASCENDING
001505		sort-surname, sort-number
001506		INPUT PROCEDURE IS INPUT-PROC
001507		OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS OUTPUT-PROC  >>>>*can this be the
REPORT-PARAGRAPH?
001508
001510	INPUT-PROC.
001511	   OPEN INPUT detail-file
001512		SET not-end-of-file-flag TO TRUE

001515		PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL end-of-file-flag
001516			READ detail-file NEXT
001517			AT END 
001518				SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
001519			NOT AT END
001521				IF amount < 100
	*move (FD) - surname & number to sort work file...
001522					MOVE surname TO sort-surname
001523					MOVE number TO sort-number

001525				        RELEASE sort-record
001527				END-IF
001528			END-READ
001529		END-PERFORM.
001530		CLOSE detail-file.
001567*~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
001568	OUTPUT-PROC.
	* report file
001569		OPEN OUTPUT report-file.
	*sorted-file
001570	   	OPEN OUTPUT sorted-file
                INITIATE report
001573		PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL end-of-file-flag
001574		      OPEN OUTPUT sorted-file
001575			RETURN sort-file 
001576				AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
001577				NOT AT END	
001578					MOVE sort-surname TO sorted-surname
001579					MOVE sort-number TO sorted-number
001583				PERFORM REPORT-PARAGRAPH
001585			END-RETURN
001587		END-PERFORM
001588		TERMINATE cust-report 
001592
001593*****************END SORTS **************************
002740	REPORT-PARAGRAPH.
003060             GENERATE report1
003061 			   READ sorted-file NEXT RECORD 
003062			         AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
003066                     END-READ.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~CODE END~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol file sort

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-01T15:07:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gJWIb.16694$aw2.9942099@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401010620.427dda41@posting.google.com>`

```
"ritchie" <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:3bee6ba6.0401010620.427dda41@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
> I am trying to produce a sorted report using the report writer.


Your code is fine, except for

> 002740 REPORT-PARAGRAPH.
> 003060             GENERATE report1
> 003061    READ sorted-file NEXT RECORD
> 003062          AT END SET end-of-file-flag TO TRUE
> 003066                     END-READ.

No need to READ Sorted-File in this paragraph. After you generate the report
line, just let the paragraph exit, and the next RETURN will get you the next
line of detail to report.

i.e., when you RETURN a line,  your NOT AT END should look something like...

   RETURN sort-record
     NOT AT END
      PERFORM   REPORT-PARAGRAPH

...

REPORT-PARAGRAPH.

     IF lines-this page GREATER  THAN  MAX-LINES-PER-PAGE
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADER-ROUTINE
    END-IF
    IF  CONTROL-BREAK-CONDITION     (new name?)
         PERFORM  CONTROL-BREAK-ROUTINE
    END-IF
    MOVE  (fields of sort record) TO (fields of report-line)
    WRITE REPORT-LINE
    ADD    1  TO Lines-this-page

   CONTINUE.

OTHER-PARAGRAHS.


MCM
```

## Thread: COBOL Standards Issue — 7 this month / 54 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

_Thread started [2003-12](2003-12.md#t-e6413d5517)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T09:35:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<btc795$15gv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8545dc5cd4c4c4c250ae794390d78c@news.teranews.com> <20031227130705.29482.00001123@mb-m02.aol.com> <vusdf9770dqrd1@corp.supernews.com> <bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9732ebbe850931ee13efeb5da7f2dc8@news.teranews.com>`

```
Still true, to a degree.

For Unisys MCP/AS COBOL74, fixed-point 77-level numerics of any USAGE are
regularly coerced into on-stack USAGE BINARY items, unless the compiler
figures out a reason to disallow this optimization.  Moreover, elementary
01-level numerics are first optimized into 77-level items if the compiler
doesn't see a reason to prohibit the optimization.  And in both cases, if
the item is used as a source but not a destination and in addition has a
VALUE clause, it is turned into a compile-time constant; if it is never
referenced, it can be optimized out of existence.

MCP/AS COBOL85 treats 77-level BINARY, BINARY EXTENDED, BINARY TRUNCATED,
REAL and DOUBLE items as on-stack items, but does not second-guess the
programmer's USAGE specification for COMP and DISPLAY numerics at Level 77,
and does not treat 01-level elementary items of any USAGE into 77's.  This
is in part because COBOL85 strives to reduce its dependence on the D2 stack,
"pooling" most working-storage.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:b9732ebbe850931ee13efeb5da7f2dc8@news.teranews.com...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> >
> > In most shops lately people cluster similar working storage fields under
an 01,
> > and few use 77 levels.   I don't know if the efficiency issues have been
> > overcome, or whether they just don't matter anymore.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> could speed the others up, but if 77's are no longer stack variables,
there
> would be no difference anymore. :-)
> -- 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2004-01-01T21:17:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20040101161708.22085.00001396@mb-m19.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

 "Howard Brazee" howard@brazee.net 
Date: 12/29/03 9:14 AM EST
Message-id: <bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>

enters an interrogatory rhetorically ...

<<

Speaking of interpreting - I was in a shop where the standard was only one 01
level in working storage and no 77 levels.   It seems that they didn't like the
way CoBOL moved data to word boundaries.

In most shops lately people cluster similar working storage fields under an 01,
and few use 77 levels.   I don't know if the efficiency issues have been
overcome, or whether they just don't matter anymore.

>>

Several things seem to be going on in the mainframe shops. One is that there
are very few programmers on site relative to demands nowadays, so bit fiddling
for any purpose is a fading art.  Boundary alignment can make a big difference,
as can comprehension of the machines internal cache line allignments (greatly
model dependent in any case).  But only if your own application code is the
issue.

In the mainframe shops the DB2, IMS, IDMS, CICS, and VSAM for that matter
actually determine the CPU piggery of your application (no matter how those
mips are accounted). This is independent of the I/O time itself.  SQL in
particular sells iron because it is unbelievable inefficient if there are no
direct indexes to which to bind.  But also things like IMS and CICS are real
Santa Clause packages that offer a little for everyone, and it takes eons of
CPU time to get past all the what-ifs that ain't-if.

The unit of work, resource contention management facilities, and synchpoint
processing capabilities of modern packages chew mightily into the CPU as does
most notably the every constant march of the more robust OSes. There is little
you can do with your own code optimizations in this context.

The best bet for performance is to design your program and the databases in
such a way as to minimize the need for services.

Boundary alignment and data conversion in an application program still mean a
lot but just in an ever  decreasing portion of the whole situation presented to
the CPU.


Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-02T09:57:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FBE502.09574102012004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20040101161708.22085.00001396@mb-m19.aol.com>`

```
In article <20040101161708.22085.00001396@mb-m19.aol.com>,
 rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

>  "Howard Brazee" howard@brazee.net 
> Date: 12/29/03 9:14 AM EST
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> issue.


One of my favorites is the standard that says:

   01 WS-BEGIN-MARKER PIC X(80)
      VALUE 'SO-AND-SO STORAGE BEGINS HERE>'.


   ...REST OF WORKING STORAGE...


   01 WS-END-MARKER PIC X(80)
      VALUE 'SO-AND-SO STORAGE ENDS HERE<'.

When looked at in a memory dump, what one sees is:

SO-AND-SO STORAGE BEGINSHERE>bbb...bbb
bbbSO-AND-SO STORAGE ENDS HERE<bbb.bbb

followed by the remainder of working storage.

I even made a nice effort to explain to the standards people how some 
versions of the IBM Cobol compiler will reorder untouched working 
storage fields into a collective bunch of static literals.  And how 
being an 01 level means never knowing where you are located.

Fell on deaf ears though...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-03T10:49:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A0C0BE.10490303012004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20040101161708.22085.00001396@mb-m19.aol.com> <joe_zitzelberger-FBE502.09574102012004@corp.supernews.com> <bt47fe$280$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bt47fe$280$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On  2-Jan-2004, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> boundaries as well.

I don't disagree that it was useful -- but it stopped being useful at 
least a decade ago (possibly more) when optimize is turned on.  
01-Levels can be moved in any way the compiler likes.

Now if you put an 02-Level at the beginning and end of each 01, that 
might help dump reading.  But I've never seen that standard -- it is 
always extra 01's.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2004-01-03T18:25:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bt6tef$pad$1@services.kq.no>`
- **References:** `<bspcsn$lu0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20040101161708.22085.00001396@mb-m19.aol.com> <joe_zitzelberger-FBE502.09574102012004@corp.supernews.com> <bt47fe$280$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-A0C0BE.10490303012004@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-

> > > I even made a nice effort to explain to the standards people how some
> > > versions of the IBM Cobol compiler will reorder untouched working
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > It used to be pretty useful for us dump spelunkers.   I would stick a
few
> > similar commands within the dump.   We knew how to adjust to full word
> > boundaries as well.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One particular way the compiler likes is to move them to the garbage bin.
With OPTIMIZE(FULL) the unreferenced 77 or 01 level working-storage simply
isn't there!

This is well documented in Programmer's Guide for Enterprise Cobol for z/OS:

"If OPTIMIZE is specified without any suboptions, OPTIMIZE(STD) will be in
effect.
The FULL suboption requests that, in addition to the optimizations performed
under OPT(STD), the compiler discard unreferenced data items from the DATA
DIVISION and suppress generation of code to initialize these data items to
their VALUE clauses. When OPT(FULL)is in effect, all unreferenced 77-level
items and elementary 01-level items will be discarded. In addition, 01-level
group items will be discarded, if none of the subordinate items are
referenced. The deleted items are shown in the listing. If the MAP option is
in effect, a BL number of XXXX in the data map information indicates that
the data item was discarded.

"Do not use OPT(FULL) if your programs depend on making use of unused data
items. Two common ways this has been done in the past are:

  a.. A technique sometimes used in OS/VS COBOL programs is to place an
unreferenced table after a referenced table and use out-of-range subscripts
on the first table to access the second table. To see if your programs have
this problem, use the SSRANGE compiler option with the CHECK(ON) run-time
option. To work around this problem, use the ability of COBOL to code large
tables and use just one table.
  a.. Place eye-catcher data items in the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION to
identify the beginning and end of the program data, or to mark a copy of a
program for a library tool that uses the data to identify a version of a
program. To solve this problem, initialize these items with PROCEDURE
DIVISION statements rather than VALUE clauses. With this method, the
compiler will consider these items as used, and will not delete them.
Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards Issue

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)

Joe Zitzelberger joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com

pick that up and make it a required standard in their shop for all O1's.

I have seen shops that put copybook names in the first eight positions of a
copybook as a literal, and that string appears on every record of every file
developed with that standard.

sandwiches around 05 bundles. Stop it, please!

## Thread: CICS START command — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS START command

- **From:** andre.queiroz@netcabo.pt (Andr? Queiroz)
- **Date:** 2004-01-02T09:56:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,
 Can anyone tell me if it is possible to run a "background"
transaction with the CICS START command, releasing my own terminal
while doing the background process.
 I tried a START without the TERMINAL parameter but still my own
terminal was busy until the background transaction ended.
 Thanks in advance,

  Andre Queiroz
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS START command

- **From:** robertwessel2@yahoo.com (Robert Wessel)
- **Date:** 2004-01-02T14:48:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bea2590e.0401021448.238af4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>`

```
andre.queiroz@netcabo.pt (Andr? Queiroz) wrote in message news:<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>...
> Hello,
>  Can anyone tell me if it is possible to run a "background"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Thanks in advance,


That should be all you need.  If you do a "CEMT I TAS" from another
terminal while your background task is running, does it show it
attached to a terminal ("Fac()")?  What does the background task do? 
If it's strictly computational it'll never relinquish control to CICS,
and no other tasks will run.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS START command

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-03T07:44:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ApuJb.586988$0v4.23293326@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>`

```


Andr? Queiroz wrote:

> Hello,
>  Can anyone tell me if it is possible to run a "background"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Andre Queiroz

If you want your STARTed transaction to execuate asynchronously from 
your current CICS transaction, then I believe it will be necessary for 
you to START it to a terminal (or facility) that is different from 
your current terminal/facility.

I have used started tasks in CICS to perform file I/O asynchronously 
from network message processing.

I hope that helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS START command

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-03T10:43:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kodvvsn6dpijn1hr11377g8eiui7g5qef@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e571c199.0401020956.139cce5d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 2 Jan 2004 09:56:00 -0800, andre.queiroz@netcabo.pt (Andr? Queiroz)
enlightened us:

>Hello,
> Can anyone tell me if it is possible to run a "background"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Andre Queiroz

Unless you use the TERMID parameter of the START command, it is not
attached to any terminal.  Check your CICS manual.  It will state that
if the TERMID option is not used, the task is not attached to any
terminal and, as a result, can't do any terminal I/O.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Here lies the body of Jonathan Blake.
Stepped on the gas
Instead of the brake.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

## Thread: Novice Question About Console I/O — 8 msgs

### Novice Question About Console I/O

- **From:** Rich Chilausky <rchilausky@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-02T22:16:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C4mJb.181674$Vu5.13217984@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
I am using Fujitsu Cobol V3 (the free version) to learn Cobol. I am doing simple 
console I/O. I read from the keyboard and write to the screen. The 
compile/link/execute sequence in the Fujitsu Cobol V3 works as advertised.  I 
read from the keyboard and write to the console window via ACCEPTs and DISPLAYs. 
The program behaves as expected. I did a static link and generated an executable 
that runs from my home directory.  When I invoke the executable in a Windows XP 
command prompt window, it runs just the way it did in the V3 ide. It brings up a 
console window and prompts for my input. I had expected it to use the command 
prompt window for the DISPLAY destination. I want to read from the keyboard and 
write to the command prompt window. I've searched the documentation that comes 
with the compiler environment, but have been unable to figure out how to write 
to the command prompt window. I suspect it is my Cobol naivetï¿œ concerning i/o 
that prevents me from seeing the solution in the documentation. Thanks in 
advance for your help.

Cheers,
Rich Chilausky
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice Question About Console I/O

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-01-02T17:50:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5EmJb.16191$Vl6.3303780@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<C4mJb.181674$Vu5.13217984@twister.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<C4mJb.181674$Vu5.13217984@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
Rich Chilausky wrote:
> I am using Fujitsu Cobol V3 (the free version) to learn Cobol. I am 
> doing simple console I/O. I read from the keyboard and write to the 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Go back to the IDE, find the source code of the main program, and right 
click on it.  Select "Main".  Change the option from "display" to 
"window", then recompile and link.  You will have to force a rebuild, as 
the change will not trigger it.

It should work the same in both the IDE and the target machine. Note 
that once you go to a window rather than a console, you can use accept 
and display with line numbers and attributes, or you can use a screen 
section.

Donald
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T10:54:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401091054.4351762c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<C4mJb.181674$Vu5.13217984@twister.southeast.rr.com> <5EmJb.16191$Vl6.3303780@news20.bellglobal.com> <5CXKb.219833$dl.10980816@twister.southeast.rr.com> <OUZKb.46490$BA6.956232@news20.bellglobal.com> <afmLb.248226$I53.11607630@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
Rich Chilausky <rchilausky@nc.rr.com> wrote 

> I replaced my display ... with display ... at line x column y. It did change the 
> behavior. It opened a "screen" window, but all else was the same. The window was 
>   not the command prompt window.

Exactly.  Fujitsu 3 uses a 'screen' and/or a 'console window', neither
of which are the command prompt that started it.
 
> This is the scenario that I am trying to prove in. I have a working Cobol 
> program that requires a modest amount of configuaration information at startup. 
> I want to be able to invoke the program via a command prompt window or over a 
> telnet connection, supply the information required via line oriented prompts, 
> and then let it compute right along. Perhaps I need a totally different approach?

Or a different product.  To run in a _DOS_ session or a telnet session
you need to have a DOS compiler or use Linux.  To run a GUI session
you need to use RAS or VNC and then you should get the Fujitsu (and
other) windows.

For a small amount of configuration you could have a text file that
the program opens, or put it on the command line.

For telnet access you could try using Kobol from www.thekompany.com
demo version , real version is only %50.00 or so.  I've not tried the
Windows version but it may work.
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice Question About Console I/O

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T14:16:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401071416.13490055@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<C4mJb.181674$Vu5.13217984@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
Rich Chilausky <rchilausky@nc.rr.com> wrote

> When I invoke the executable in a Windows XP 
> command prompt window, it runs just the way it did in the V3 ide. It brings up a 
> console window and prompts for my input. I had expected it to use the command 
> prompt window for the DISPLAY destination. I want to read from the keyboard and 
> write to the command prompt window. 

Fujitsu uses two 'windows' one is a console and is used for ANSI style
ACCEPT/DISPLAY the other is the CRT used for X/Open style
ACCEPT/DISPLAY UPON CRT or ACCEPT/DISPLAY AT ... The latter is only
created if an X/Open style statement is executed.

Neither of these are the command prompt window where you typed the
command.

Instead of starting a command prompt window and then starting your
program by typing a command which then opens a console window, why not
put the command of a strtcut (icon on desktop) and start the console
only.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hand Held Windows PC and COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-04T14:50:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401041450.47ae9eb8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ff8512e$0$61325$a8266bb1@news.titannews.com>`

```
"Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote 

> Has anybody been able to develop code for a hand held PC using Windows CE? I
> use Realia Cobol and GuiScreenio for my development.

As I understand it, Realia compiles to Intel 80x86 code for MS
Windows.

Hand held devices are not Intel CPUs, they are Dragon or StrongARM or
XScale.  Windows CE is not 'Windows', but is somewhat the same but
also somewhat different.

When some one writes a compiler and/or runtime that will run on an
XScale CPU with CE then you may get to write Cobol for those machines.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hand Held Windows PC and COBOL

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T13:48:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1jqivvg82tnt59a2o0ce176e828e4qr5io@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3ff8512e$0$61325$a8266bb1@news.titannews.com>`

```
"Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote:

>Has anybody been able to develop code for a hand held PC using Windows CE? I
>use Realia Cobol and GuiScreenio for my development.

The Flexus Web Client can be used to develop a user interface for an
handheld PC using Windows CE.  Many of our customers have already done
this.

Most handhelds don't have the resources to develop a full Win32
interface.  But as far as I know, they all offer a web browser.

In addition, you can develop a standard HTML/Javascript interface with
Web Client, or you can develop a very sophisticated Flash interface
using the Macromedia Flash support with our Web Client product.  I'll
be happy to assist with further details through priovate e-mail.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Hand Held Windows PC and COBOL

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T08:37:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401050837.264d58eb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ff8512e$0$61325$a8266bb1@news.titannews.com>`

```
Stephen,

I'm speaking as a representative of Flexus right now.

There are not COBOL compilers targeting CE/Pocket PC at this point. 
Even the .NET compilers offered by Fujitsu and Micro Focus cannot
presently target this environment.

Flexus - http://www.flexus.com, offers a "Web client" product that you
can use with these devices, however.  This allows your COBOL to run on
a server - ie, Unix, Windows, VMS, Linux and your user interface to be
displayed on an TCP/IP attached device using a browser.  Recently,
Flexus completed development on a Flash interface as well - allowing
you to create a consistent user interface using MacroMedia flash - but
still use your normal traditionally COBOL program on the server.

The only caveat to this is that you need TCP/IP connectivity for the
handheld.

> use Realia Cobol and GuiScreenio for my development.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Hand Held Windows PC and COBOL

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2004-01-10T09:21:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0401100921.1762b2a5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ff8512e$0$61325$a8266bb1@news.titannews.com>`

```
"Stephen Plotnick"  wrote
> Has anybody been able to develop code for a hand held PC using Windows CE? I
> use Realia Cobol and GuiScreenio for my development.

Hi Stephen,
     If you use FlashMX's standalone PPC player you can compile an 
*.exe PPC program, that will run full screen (240*320) as a standalone
program, verses (230*250) with a HTML wrapper. This will work on Mac's
also, but you will need an Html wrapper for Linux or Sony's CLIE
pocket devices.  If your PPC program is to run standalone (not always
connected), you will need to use FlashMX's flat sequential files
called "Shared Objects" or Flash5 cookies (CLIE), these can be read by
Fujitsu 6.1, PHP and I will guest any other Language or COBOL
compilers that reads flat files. This method also works if you wish to
use PPC to Desktop (versus PPC to Server).  Go to
http://www.macromedia.com/devnet/mobile/articles/sometimes_connected.html
and numerous other mobile articles there.   The great thing about
using FlashMX for your GUI, is that you can compile it to a PC/Mac
desktop as a "Projector file" or with a C++ wrapper from various third
party dealers i.e. http://www.Northcode.com or to Linux if you are a
WINE genius or just HTML. Using a FlashMX GUI that senses devices and
screen-size, you can minimize work areas to small buttons, thus
allowing the same GUI and sub_program_calling code in one *.fla
project file (same as FSC's *.prj) across multiple sized devices and
OSs. A FlashMX compiled program can call other compiled programs,
including COBOL, providing they are in a special named directory
called FSCOMMAND. This is used extensity by programmers for desktop
and CD/DVD programming, even Macromedia's FlashMX Studio's CD
installer uses this method.
  Hope this info helps Stephen. 

MY QUESTION: Is anyone Successfully using a non_COBOL_GUI to COBOL
compiler for backend desktop and/or server for CGI across various
OS's? I have Fujitsu 6.1, however while I can call FSC desktop from
FlashMX, it seems that FSC in dropping their "Standard version" that
works well for confusing Multiple GUI methods that require extensive
GUI multiple rewrites across OS's and at least two major versions for
Windows (NET and screen-io). As a bonus you must pay FSC $1800.00 for
the standard Linux compiler versus a $500.00 or less for C++/JAVA
compiler or free PHP for client to server and the latter have
extensive examples and developer communities.  PowerCOBOL screens are
like going back to 1920's silent black and white movies verses Flash's
GUI and other COBOL GUIs are jokes. I have tried KOBOL, however there
are few examples and null developer community, and unfortunately not
well proven. Unless there is something new soon, my few COBOL programs
may need just to be rewritten, which I've been dreading in my not too
much spare time. Any input appreciated.
              Thanks. Mike
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hand Held Windows PC and COBOL

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)

thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote in message 
> Flexus products allow this, and there are numerous people doing this
> using the Flexus suite of tools - all compiler and OS independent.

Thanks for the reply Thane.
   However Flexus appears for to be for the desktop/thin-client to mid
to large servers; where I deal with a FlashMX PPC/desktop client to
desktop and/or small web server.  I am using flash for these now, as
noted in my previous post, as FlashMX's web client GUI can be compiled
to desktop/PPC native application or compiled for E_books etc, however
FlashMX can only use flat local files or RDBMS via remote calls and
lacks the ability to work with the desktops system resources, thus the
need of a language like COBOL, C++ or third party C++ extensions at
the backend. Flash Communication server AScript language works with
amazing features that PHP just will not ever have on its own(and many
it should have). Just looking for brainstorming schema solutions of
reworking older COBOL without complete rewrites. Thanks. Mike
```

<a id="t-b08d7b6ead"></a>
## Thread: NetCOBOL and reports — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### NetCOBOL and reports

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T15:52:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<btbto1$2ej$1@troll.uvt.nl>`

```
LS,

I'm searching for the best way to create reports that can be altered during
runtime by the end-user. The process of changing column-order, sort-order,
which data is contained in which column, which datafields are visible etc.
should be done by the user via a GUI form designer which stores the report
settings, or via an own written program which via for instance COM/OCX
directly influences the report, based on some stored settings.

I've taken a look at crystal decision and *believe* that the situations I
mention above (the report alteration) can be done via COM. Yet I haven't got
the hands-on experience and I'd like to hear the experience of those who
have worked with this product.

Are there any similar products to crystal reports? Are they
superios/inferior to it, and if so, on which features?

I'm using NetCOBOL/PowerCOBOL version 7. I'm aware of the existence of
PowerFORM, but this product doesn't offer runtime adjustment of reports
created with it.

Thanks in advance and kind regards,

Rik Hendriks
---------------------------------
Don't think you are, know you are.
```

<a id="t-d7dbea785c"></a>
## Thread: can cobol handle the handle? — 16 msgs

### can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T08:01:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

when we use the cobol command 'open i-o indexed-file', is there a way
that I can get or obtain the file handle from the cobol runtime system?
I need to use some win32 api functions and I must provide the file handle
on entry as a call parameter. I am using Net Express 3.1 with windows 2000.

thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T18:03:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com>`

```
"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com...

> when we use the cobol command 'open i-o indexed-file', is there a way
> that I can get or obtain the file handle from the cobol runtime system?
> I need to use some win32 api functions and I must provide the file handle
> on entry as a call parameter. I am using Net Express 3.1 with windows
2000.

I do not have the answer, but...

There is more than one system handle here, one for the data file and one for
the index file.

Maybe you should just "COBOL-OPEN"  indexed-file SHARED, and simultaneously
open the two files (the data file AND the index file!) with CreateFile (also
shared) and you'll get valid system handle(s) to the same physical file(s).

I just can't see how the system file handle(s) obtained by NetExpress under
these conditions is(are) anything but pretty useless. (Sorry, I cannot
imagine what API call you could be trying to use here... Oh, wait a
minute... one of the NetApi's so you can find "who" has the file open? Well,
the above suggestion handles that, too. If that's a bad guess on my part,
can you describe your challenge in application terms?).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T15:24:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<RFhKb.20986

Michael wrote:
can you describe your challenge in application terms?.

Kellie wrote:
I am trying to use the following win32 api function:

1). call "GetFileSizeEx" (get file size in bytes)
2). call "GetFileInformationByHandle" (have two copies of the same
          file on the hard drive, so we check for duplicates or not).
3). call "GetFileType" (check for disk file)

regards, kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T23:58:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1TmKb.21124$P%1.20308181@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com>`

```
"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:<RFhKb.20986
>
> Michael wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1). call "GetFileSizeEx" (get file size in bytes)

Use FindFirstFile against indexed-file. That will return a 64-bit size in
the WIN32_FIND_DATA structure.  (Not that that is going to be meaningful,
read on).

> 2). call "GetFileInformationByHandle" (have two copies of the same
>           file on the hard drive, so we check for duplicates or not).

???  Indexed-file in your program should have the name of your file, but if
you are talking about problems with "paths" you can take a look at the
GetFullPathName API. That will get you the fully-qualified name from a
relative name.  GetFileInformationByHandle would get the filesize,
attributes etc , too, but you can get the same info from FindFirstFile in
the WIN32_FIND_DATA structure.

If you want to conduct a search for a file, see the SearchPath API function.

> 3). call "GetFileType" (check for disk file)

Why call it? In this case, it will always return FILE_TYPE_DISK. Well, on
further review, I guess it could be a FILE_TYPE_PIPE because....

...in any event, the COBOL "OPEN I-O indexed-file"  may refer to ONE or TWO
(or more, I guess) physical files, and how you get to the individual
physical names from the COBOL 'OPEN'  verb is implementor-defined.

I.e., you might specifiy
indexed-file      PIC X(260) VALUE IS "myfile".

.. and the compiler will go looking for "myfile.IDX", which contains the
name of a separate datafile "myfile.dat".. or maybe vice-versa.

Bottom line...
To get the file size of a file for which you know the name, use
FindFirstFile
To determine the fully qualified name of a file for which you only have a
relative path name, use GetFullPathName
To search for a file along either the default or a custom path list, use
SearchPath

For professional assistance re using the WIndows API (with COBOL or 'all by
itself') ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T11:13:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81f64069d13ced4cb906b49f0de42467@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com> <1TmKb.21124$P%1.20308181@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <JcoKb.284762$Ec1.9812117@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
>
> What do you think about Microsoft's plan to replace the Win 32 API?

I don't like Steve Jobs, he is a serious jerk. But Jobs said one thing that
struck me with astounding clarity. (paraphrase) "It is not in Microsoft's
interests to make programming easier. What do they care if it costs one
or two million dollars more for the next release of Office? But it matters
to all the smaller developers with whom they compete." When you sell
10+ million copies of a several hundred dollar product, a $.10 higher
development cost per item is negligible. Also, Microsoft makes lots of
money from selling training and manuals. Microsoft isn't happy just
adding new features, they want to make you relearn stuff you already
know. Notice that in every release of Microsoft Office, for example,
they change the way you execute some common commands. Not to make
it better, just different. Every time they make a major change in the API
or user interface it is a windfall for them, though a pain in the neck for
everybody else. People buy more training and manuals, and are
prompted to upgrade their obsolete software, that still works perfectly,
just to get the new gee-whiz stuff.

This shouldn't be surprising, the auto manufacturers have done this for
decades.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T10:48:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KbudnR0j7d5fe2eiRVn-tw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com> <1TmKb.21124$P%1.20308181@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <JcoKb.284762$Ec1.9812117@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <81f64069d13ced4cb906b49f0de42467@news.teranews.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> This shouldn't be surprising, the auto manufacturers have done this
> for decades.

This is the problem for monopolies and near-monopolies. Their biggest
competitor is their own company.

In the case of software, autos, or other durable goods, the company has to
offer a better, or perceived better, product else their revenue stream just
damn STOPS.

Innovate or die is the motto.

This is obviously not the case with consumables - those companies use other
tactics. For example, Rockefeller managed to drop the price of kerosene from
$3.00/gallon to less than five cents within three years. In so doing, he
dramatically increased his customer base (and destroyed the whale-oil
business).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T16:47:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401061647.582abc4c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <RFhKb.20986$P%1.20199178@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <41758a6b.0401051524.6d11c4cd@posting.google.com> <1TmKb.21124$P%1.20308181@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <JcoKb.284762$Ec1.9812117@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <81f64069d13ced4cb906b49f0de42467@news.teranews.com> <KbudnR0j7d5fe2eiRVn-tw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> This is obviously not the case with consumables - those companies use other
> tactics. For example, Rockefeller managed to drop the price of kerosene from
> $3.00/gallon to less than five cents within three years. In so doing, he
> dramatically increased his customer base (and destroyed the whale-oil
> business).

First of all your figures are wrong:

"""Standard Oil began in 1870, when kerosene cost 30 cents a gallon.
By 1897, Rockefeller's scientists and managers had driven the price to
under 6 cents per gallon, ..."""

But this wasn't just because he was a good guy and wanted to help
consumers:

""" ... and many of his less-efficient competitors were out of
business -including companies whose inferior grades of kerosene were
prone to explosion and whose dangerous wares had depressed the demand
for the product."""

It was also to drive the competitors out of the market - those making
kerosene, not just whale oil. Not only would that low price drive
existing manufacturers out, but it acted as a barrier from anyone else
entering the market.  Standard Oil could make a profit at that price
because they had large volumes and a captive distribution network, but
also kerosene is a byproduct of petrol production which was increasing
at about 40% a year and so the total kerosene market had to be
increased otherwise it would be a waste product.

The 1870s was also a highly deflationary time when prices for
everything fell.
```

If you are using MS or Fujutsu, there is a subrourine library that 
returns those values for you.  Look for the CBL_(other stuff) routines 
in the software installation areas.  I expect all the other PC Cobols 
have similar libraries. Under MF, you can find it in the help files. 
With Fujitsu, you will have to search the PDF manuals.

There are lots of other primitives in those libraries, as well.  They 
are worth exploring.

riplin wrote:

I am trying to use the following win32 api function:

1). call "GetFileSizeEx" (get file size in bytes)
2). call "GetFileInformationByHandle" (have two copies of the same

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T18:56:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401051856.7eb2ce38@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051031.7a0142d0@posting.google.com> <41758a6b.0401051526.64505179@posting.google.com>`

```
In this case - obtain your file handle not by using a COBOL Open, but
instead by usng CreateFile or CreateFileEx API's.  CreateFile is a
misleading name because it is also used to open and not create an
existing data file - but it's the API you want.


KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote in message news:<41758a6b.0401051526.64505179@posting.google.com>...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0401051031.7a0142d0@posting.google.com>...
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> regards, kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can cobol handle the handle?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T19:24:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<41758a6b.0401050801.418ca226@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051031.7a0142d0@posting.google.com> <41758a6b.0401051526.64505179@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote 

> riplin wrote:
> Which particular functions were you intending to use and what did you
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1). call "GetFileSizeEx" (get file size in bytes)

You don't need to use a file handle to get the file size, it can be
done using the name.  For example run a command 'DIR name >dirlist'
and this file contains the file size.

> 2). call "GetFileInformationByHandle" (have two copies of the same
>           file on the hard drive, so we check for duplicates or not).

'Two copies' must be different names or be in different directories. 
If there are two files that you have opened and you could obtain the
handles then what would the 'file information' tell you that will
determine that they are or are not duplicates.

Even if they are the same size and date/time this does not mean they
are identical.  Only reading all the records will tell you that.

> 3). call "GetFileType" (check for disk file)
 
As distinct from what ?

Are any of these of any practical use or are you just playing ?

Why not use a WinAPI function to open the file and get a file handle
that way ?
```

## Thread: multiple file reads and sorts — 13 msgs

### multiple file reads and sorts

- **From:** ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie)
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T10:35:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I am having a little trouble with a program (using fujitsu compiler).

I am outputting the results of a sort to a report which will consist of records 
from 2 files.
'File1' has account details & 'File2' has further details of records from 'File1'.

I am trying to read File1, get the current Key then read File2 and get the records 
corresponding to that Key.
Where i am at the moment is, the report is outputting only one record.

I know, from hours of testing... that the problem is in the reads.
I'm not sure which file I should be reading first etc...
Maybe a fresh set of eyes can help me see where i'm going wrong?
Any help would really be much aprechiated.

Thanks,
Ritchie.

***********************************************************************
	sort-para.
	   SORT SORT-WORK ON ASCENDING
		SORT-DATE
		INPUT PROCEDURE IS SORT-INPUT
		OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS SORT-OUTPUT.

	SORT-INPUT.
		MOVE 1 TO File1Key
		START File2 KEY >= File2Key
			INVALID KEY 
				SET EOF TO TRUE 	
			NOT INVALID KEY 
				SET NOT-EOF TO TRUE
                          READ File2 NEXT RECORD 
				AT END SET EOF TO TRUE
			END-READ
		END-START

		MOVE File2Key TO File1Key
		PERFORM UNTIL EOF
		READ File1 
			INVALID KEY 
				DISPLAY "IN INVALID KEY"
				SET EOF TO TRUE
			NOT INVALID KEY 
				SET NOT-EOF TO TRUE	
                  			MOVE File1Key TO File2Key 
			READ File2 next AT END SET EOF TO TRUE
			IF File2Key = File1Key	
*moving file records to sort			
				MOVE CUSTOMER-NUMBER TO SORT-NUMBER
				MOVE CUSTOMER-NAME TO SORT-NAME
				...
			RELEASE SORT-RECORD
			END-IF
		END-READ
		END-READ
		END-PERFORM.

	SORT-OUTPUT.
		OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE
	   	OPEN OUTPUT SORTed-FILE
		SET NOT-EOF TO TRUE
                    
          	INITIATE REPORT1
		PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL EOF
			RETURN SORTed-FILE 
			AT END SET EOF TO TRUE
			NOT AT END
				MOVE SORT-NUMBER TO SORTed-NUMBER
				MOVE SORT-NAME TO SORTed-NAME
				...
				WRITE SORTED-RECORD
			GENERATE REPORT-DETAIL
			END-RETURN
 		END-PERFORM
		TERMINATE REPORT1 

      CLOSE SORTed-FILE, REPORT-FILE.
********************************************************
```

You have set File1Key

> 		START File2 KEY >= File2Key

You are using File2key without having set it to any particular value. 
As this is probably not that of any actual record you will probably
get an invalid key and thus set EOF.

> 			INVALID KEY 
> 				SET EOF TO TRUE 	
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 			IF File2Key = File1Key	
                   
This is withing that 'AT END' branch of the READ File2.  You will only
release one record at the end of file. You ned to put a NOT AT END
before the IF.

>                            *moving file records to sort			
> 				MOVE CUSTOMER-NUMBER TO SORT-NUMBER
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

ritchie wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Since you're only reading File1 only once, the results you're seeing seem
correct.

On a more stylistic note:

1. You have 2 "READ" statements and only one END-READ. While your code seems
correct, it is usually less confusing to match READ/END-READ, IF/END-IF,
etc.

2. Others may differ, but I personally find it confusing to imbed
programmatic logic within READ commands.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T16:56:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dac8594f89dd2049b7bc750f2273a793@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com>`

```
"ritchie" <ritchie_s01@yahoo.com> wrote:
>
> SORT-INPUT.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>              END-PERFORM.

You must test for File2 going past the key you're looking for, or you
will read to the end of File2 for every File1 record. :-) I made the
corrections above in the lines flagged with '>>>'.

But I don't think that's why you are getting only one record. I haven't
seen your actual Data Division but are you sure you aren't using the
same EOF field for both File1 and File2? If you are, that would give
precisely the result you describe, before adding the changes above.

P.S.- I reformatted your source above a bit. Though coding styles do
vary, if you indent farther for each deeper lexical level as above,
it will be easier to see where your logic points are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T11:21:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401061121.74c17f89@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote 

>  .......
> SORT-INPUT.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There is only ONE READ for File1 and it is a Random READ without the
key being specified.  This means that the code will READ File1 _once_
and then read all of file2 sequentially starting at where it was
STARTed with the key from File1 until EOF only outputting the
record(s) that had the same key as File1.

If you were to put in the END-READ for the File1 READ you would see it
was _after_ the END-PERFORM.

You need to PERFORM the whole paragraph until the end of File1 and
within that PERFORM until no more File2 records of the same key.

You also may have a design fault in your file keys.  An indexed file
can only have _ONE_ record of a particular key.  If a second record
with the same key is attempted to be written it will fail with a
file-status of '22' (duplicate).

This why you only got 1 record.  You only read File1 _once_ and then
read 'all' those in File2 with the same key and there was only one. 
Put a File status on the program writing these files and CHECK IT.

IT seems that File1 is some sort of master, and File2 is supposed to
be a transaction file.  You need to have File2 with a key that is more
than just the File1 key, it also needs to have a unique reference or a
sequence number of something.

           01  Account-File.    (File1)
               02  Account-Key     PIC X(6).
               02  Account-Name .....

           01  Transaction-File.  (File2)
               02  Transaction-Key.
                03 Transaction-Account   PIC X(6).
                03 Transaction-Date      PIC X(8).    (yyyymmdd)
                03 Transaction-Type      PIC X.
                03 Transaction-Ref       PIC X(6).    (eg invoice
number)
               02  Transaction-Value .....
  
You then must check Account-Key and Transaction-Account for equality.

Also use _two_ separate EOF indicators (I use HIGH-VALUES in the keys)
and recode to get rid of nesting the READs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T15:12:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401081512.429f7a39@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401061121.74c17f89@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401081151.4b7f93f7@posting.google.com>`

```
ritchie_s01@yahoo.com (ritchie) wrote 

> This has proved most helpful in narrowing down the errors.
> It has also uncovered extra problems, as always!, that i'll need to
> work on ie: duplicate record key errors - CODE "02...

File status first byte indicates success or failure.  '0' is a sucess.

The 2nd byte gives additional information '2' is duplicate.  

I would assume from this that you have an alternate key with
duplicates allowed and it successfully wrote, or read, a record that
creates an allowed duplicate key.

When checking file status for success you should never check for '00'
as there are several success codes that have additional information.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T20:29:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401082029.1d3b319a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401061121.74c17f89@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401081151.4b7f93f7@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401081512.429f7a39@posting.google.com> <fBlLb.855$Ix.720@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> Alternatively, if the logic for YOUR program indicates that having a duplicate
> alternate record key is an "application error" - the process it accordingly
> (as an error for YOUR program).

Why would the SELECT specify 'WITH DUPLICATES' if having a duplicate
alternate key was an 'application error' ?

Given that without this clause the file status would return '22' which
is a real error then I find your note rather puzzling.

A file status of '02' is only an 'application error' if the file is
wrongly designed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T05:38:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77rLb.1243$Pg.1010@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401061121.74c17f89@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401081151.4b7f93f7@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401081512.429f7a39@posting.google.com> <fBlLb.855$Ix.720@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0401082029.1d3b319a@posting.google.com>`

```
Because *if* the file were defined with an Alternate Record Key ... WITH
DUPLICATES (for use in another program) and you did NOT include it in the
Select/Assign clause, you *may* (depending upon implementation) get a FS=39 when
you try and open the file.  (Alternate Record Keys are "fixed file attributes" -
even if it is "implementor defined" which fixed file attributes are checked at
OPEN).  The whole "purpose" of having "02" versus "00" (or versus "05" or the
new "0x") file status codes is that an application MAY want to take "different"
logic paths depending upon which code is returned.  This is why I agree with you
that one should not "just check for '00'" - but do NOT agree that "0x" (where
"x" is not also "0") may or may not be "successful" for a specific program's
logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: multiple file reads and sorts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T11:23:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401091123.1e0afd57@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3bee6ba6.0401051035.4c8b91d2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401051524.20beb6f8@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401060429.70906588@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401061121.74c17f89@posting.google.com> <3bee6ba6.0401081151.4b7f93f7@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401081512.429f7a39@posting.google.com> <fBlLb.855$Ix.720@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0401082029.1d3b319a@posting.google.com> <77rLb.1243$Pg.1010@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> Because *if* the file were defined with an Alternate Record Key ... WITH
> DUPLICATES (for use in another program) and you did NOT include it in the
> Select/Assign clause, 

If you are using a file that allows duplicate keys then you may have
to expect those 'another programs' may actually write records with
such keys. If this is an 'application error' for the program then
there is a very poor system design, one that may never work correctly.

> The whole "purpose" of having "02" versus "00" (or versus "05" or the
> new "0x") file status codes is that an application MAY want to take "different"
> logic paths depending upon which code is returned.  

Yes.  But I was explaining to someone why they occurred, not what his
application should do about it.  If he wants to exclude duplicate
alternate keys he should make the file fail to write them.

> This is why I agree with you
> that one should not "just check for '00'" - 

Good.  I suspect that he was doing this.

> but do NOT agree that "0x" (where
> "x" is not also "0") may or may not be "successful" for a specific program's
> logic.

'Successful' in respect of file status has nothing to do with
'application logic' it is entirely related to the I/O operation
requested.  The program issued a WRITE (for example).  A status of
'0x' (including '02') indicates that the WRITE completed
_successfully_ whereas a status where the first byte is not '0' (eg
'22') indicates that the WRITE failed.

If the application decides on the basis of getting a '02' or '22' that
it doesn't want this record then it uses the 'success' or 'failure' to
know whether  it needs to DELETE it or to merely carry on with no
further action.

You are confused.  'Success' for a file operation has no implication
about application logic at all.
```

---

<a id="t-d9ecc2e84e"></a>
## Thread: Announcement: GUI ScreenIO Client/Server Layer — 1 msgs

### Announcement: GUI ScreenIO Client/Server Layer

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <support@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-05T23:14:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9qjvv8qg99dn7gp2qv7p61224gv33qg49@4ax.com>`

```
January 2, 2004

Norcom is pleased to announce the release of the GUI ScreenIO
Client/Server Layer.  

The Client/Server Layer enables existing GUI ScreenIO applications to
run across a TCP/IP network (including the Internet) without the need
to change your application.  This makes it very easy to
Internet-enable GUI ScreenIO applications.

The application itself runs on the server and only sends screen
updates to the client computer.  The client computer runs the GUI
ScreenIO Network Client software, which handles screen displays, file
transfers, and security.  The GUI ScreenIO application behaves exactly
as though it is running locally on the client.

Client/Server computing has many advantages, including better control
of your application software.  Because the applications run on your
server, it's only necessary to update the server's copy of your
software when you make changes.  You no longer need to distribute new
versions to all of your users.

Client/Server computing is also scalable; you can add servers and
communications bandwidth as your user base grows.  The GUI ScreenIO
Server software could also be ported to any hardware platform that
supports COBOL and TCP/IP; it does not require a Windows platform.
Contact Norcom if you wish to explore this this option.

--------------------------------------------
INTRODUCTORY PRICING

Introductory pricing is $250 for a server license that supports 5
concurent users; per-user cost decreases as volume increases.  It is
not necessary to license clients unless you wish to take advantage of
security and user-customization features. 

--> Combine products and receive an additional 25% discount!

If you purchase licenses to two or more selected Norcom products, the
total price will be reduced another 25%.  For example, if you license
GUI ScreenIO ($595) and a 5-user Server ($250), you'll pay only $634
instead of $845.

------
GUI ScreenIO works with the most popular 32-bit PC COBOL compilers,
including AcuCOBOL, CA-Realia COBOL, Fujitsu COBOL, IBM Visual Age
COBOL, and Micro Focus COBOL. 

Norcom, based in Juneau, Alaska, has been marketing screen managers
and other COBOL programming tools since 1985.

For more information, visit Norcom's website:  http://www.screenio.com
-----
Kevin
Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

## Thread: IBM User Group (SHARE) info — 1 msgs

### IBM User Group (SHARE) info

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T12:02:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JtxKb.22771$lo3.7447@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
For IBM mainframe (COBOL or otherwise) users,

"EARLY-BIRD SAVINGS EXPIRE JANUARY 9
Time is running out to take advantage of the Early-Bird registration discount
for SHARE User Events in Long Beach, valid only through January 9. Don't delay -
members save $275 per attendee! Be sure to use the Promotion Code EM6 to ensure
your savings!

While one low registration fee grants you unrestricted access to all three
events, registering for the event you primarily wish to attend allows SHARE to
provide you with a customized event experience. Register today for the event
that best suits your needs. Your training and education includes access to over
750 sessions collectively delivered by three separate events, regardless of
which event you register for.

Complete session information is now available online for all three co-located
events, each taking place February 22-27, 2004:
     SHARE User-Driven Training Event & Expo - http://longbeach.share.org/share
    Application Development & Integration Symposium -
http://longbeach.share.org/adi
    Enterprise Systems Management Symposium - http://longbeach.share.org/esm

Don't miss out on the savings - register today for a solid cost-savings to start
out your new year!"

P.S.  For those interested in INFLUENCING the future of IBM's COBOL products,
you may want to plan on attending a session that I will be "hosting", Wed at
6:00 p.m. -

"SHARE in Long Beach, CA - Session 8250 Details

COBOL, PL/I and LE Requirements Review

SHARE has the opportunity to influence IBM development activities by submitting
Requirements indicating changes and additions for existing products, and for new
products to meet user needs. This session will discuss the SHARE Requirements
process and how SHARE members can submit and participate in the process.
Requirement that have been previously submitted to IBM, and any responses that
IBM has provided to those requirements, will also be discussed. As time allows,
there may be suggestions from attendees for new Requirements to be submitted
using the SHARE Requirements process."
```

### [Q]: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr>
- **Date:** 2004-01-06T19:21:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`

```
Hi Listers


I made some research on the net to find out how many lines of cobol were
running in today's production systems, but I could not find out a strong
validated and (why not audited) figure.

In my understanding from the various sources, it is counting in billions of
lines....which is quite large in term of evaluation....Does anybody could
give me more precise numbers, specially on past, present and future
estimations??

Thanks in advance.

FA
```

#### ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T10:58:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```

news:3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr...
> Hi Listers
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> In my understanding from the various sources, it is counting in billions
of
> lines....which is quite large in term of evaluation....Does anybody could
> give me more precise numbers, specially on past, present and future
> estimations??
>
Gartner Group are the ones who provided most of the existing estimates.

They claim around 3 billlion lines a year are still being written; in 1999
it was 5 billion.

These figures are totally suspect because...

(a) Gartner Group have a vested interest in COBOL.

(b) There is no really adequate way to assess the situation Worldwide.

Besides, what does it matter?

Pete.
```

hilarity in any product managment meetings....

FA

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T08:15:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<btm9i2$941$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`

```
In article <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>,
Francis ANDRE <francis.andre@easynet.fr> wrote:
>Because I have to argue the importance of COBOL in the computing world
>against product managers, investors and other guys that are more on the
>"new" Java/J2EE/Unix side than on "old" Cobol/CICS/OS390 side....

Let me get this straight... you need to argue the importance of an 
installed base with those who have little or no technical background?

My first suggestion is to find another job, fast; such people, in my
experience, tend to dismiss their own ignorance with a sweeping 'Well, I'm
not *technical* (sniff of disdain) but I don't see why...' to which an
oft-suppressed response is 'The reasons for 'why not' are readily apparent
to someone who has spent a few months educating themselves on technical
matters.'

That bit of frustration aside... try comparing COBOL to an internal
combustion engine... the device it'sself may date from the late 19th
century but any attempts to deal with matters of transportation which do
*not* take this particular device into account are most likely doomed to
failure.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-10T13:28:13+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`

```

"Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr> wrote in message
news:3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr...
> Because I have to argue the importance of COBOL in the computing world
> against product managers, investors and other guys that are more on the
> "new" Java/J2EE/Unix side than on "old" Cobol/CICS/OS390 side....Giving
> figures like the Gartner ones needs to be "validated" from my point of
view
> otherwise a number like 180 billions lines of Cobol worldwide can cause
some
> hilarity in any product managment meetings....
>

Yes, I see your problem.

Here's my take on it. (Totally opinion and many here will disagree. But,
like you, I have also had to argue COBOL's case with senior and middle
management, and you are absolutely correct that making wild,
unsubstantiable, claims for zillions of lines of code simply destroys your
credibility and makes them less likely to listen to what you are really
trying to say.)

1. COBOL IS declining in importance. The reasons for this are manifold.
Newer, quicker, cheaper, techniques, methodologies, and tools, and a failure
by the COBOL User base to keep COBOL in the forefront by embracing the
latest facilities in it. (The IT development World is moving away from
procedural code into components and Objects and there is only a limited
place for standard COBOL in this arena. It isn't just COBOL; PL/1 is also
affected similarly...)

So, accepting that COBOL is no longer "the only game in town", is there
still a place for it?

The answer is an unqualified "YES" and it has nothing to do with how many
COBOL programmers there are worldwide (Thane reckons more than 6,000,000; I
would say nearer 3,000,000 (I am excluding China), but neither of us can
support our claims, and it is irrelevant if you are looking at a specific
case in a specific organization), or how many lines of COBOL code are in
existence (which is also irrelevant, even if you COULD substantiate the
claims, UNLESS your organisation is planning World domination and will take
over the COBOL maintenance and development for the entire planet <G>...I see
the Chinese are getting into COBOL through the ISO ...you're not connected
with them I take it <G>?)

The FACT is that in your organisation COBOL  is currently being used. (If it
isn't, then you are on a hiding to nothing. To suggest starting COBOL
development NOW would be an extremely difficult position to defend, despite
the passionate arguments you will hear from people in this group, UNLESS
there is a clearly defined IT strategy for the organisation where COBOL can
be shown to play a role...see below).

Given that you HAVE COBOL code and it isn't "broken" then there is certainly
a good case for continuing to ensure some COBOL expertise remains on your
site. The cost of replacing the existing code before it has returned on the
investment made to develop it, should not be acceptable, given that the only
drive to do this is "fashion"...

2. There are much broader issues that need to be considered when you look at
it from the point of view of management of the IT resource, rather than the
narrow view of a COBOL programmer on the shop floor. Irrespective of the
number of lines of code currently being maintained, is your company likely
to continue to develop bespoke systems in-house for the foreseeable future?
Has NOBODY suggested outsourcing or a package solution? Will the current
level of investment in IT development be maintained for, say, the next 5
years? Is the company likely to increase its exposure on the Web and
Intranet? In other words, is there an IT strategy that is clearly defined so
that everyone knows where the company is going "ITwise"? If there is, it is
much easier to argue where COBOL fits into said strategy; if there isn't,
then it is like the tail wagging the dog..."We'll build a strategy around
COBOL or Java/Unix..." (As if the two are mutually exclusive...they're
not.)No. Decide where you are going, THEN decide what you need to get there.

3. I have worked with COBOL for 38 years. I have seen the language and its
role in IT change. The way of the future is NOT the way of the past, but,
during the transition, there is a very valid case for maintaining some level
of investment in COBOL. How long will the transition take? I said "15 years"
in 2000. I have seen nothing to make me change that estimate.

All of the above are reasoned and (hopefully) valid arguments for using
COBOL in 2004. You don't need exorbitant claims for a Worldwide COBOL user
base. which has doubtful bearing on your specific case, anyway.

As an aside, let's consider that there are several trillion lines of COBOL
in existence, and around 3 billion are currently being produced annually.
(Nobody knows what the real figures are, but few would contest that the
number of lines per year is declining...).

So, it is fair to say that the World has a pretty solid investment in COBOL
legacy systems.

BUT, even if this is so, the "new" techniques of data mining, warehousing,
and code refurbishment have automated tools that can rip through source code
at more than a million lines a minute...

I have seen demonstrations of tools that can isolate COBOL code functions
and turn them into objects. We all know there  are tools out there can can
convert COBOL to Java (to quibble about whether it is GOOD Java or not is
simply churlish; it is a start. The original COBOL may not have been that
"good" either...).

The point is that if all the COBOL code in the World currently, needed to be
replaced, it could be. (And in a realistic time scale, say, less than a
year...remember it is NOT a serial process; it is occurring on many sites
all over the World, even as you read this.). As systems reach their "sell
by" dates, the pressure to replace them becomes more urgent.

The Gartner estimate of $25 a line is based on it being done MANUALLY. It
won't be. The real cost is more likely to be fractions of a penny per line.

That is why I said that the number of lines of code and the number of COBOL
programmers, doesn't matter when considering whether to use COBOL or not.

COBOL must stand or fall on its own merits, measured against the needs
(current and future) of the organisation, and the organisation's IT
strategy.

Currently, it can do this. Whether it will still be able to do so in 15 to
20 years, only time will tell.

Pete.

> FA
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I made some research on the net to find out how many lines of cobol
were
> > > running in today's production systems, but I could not find out a
strong
> > > validated and (why not audited) figure.
> > >
> > > In my understanding from the various sources, it is counting in
billions
> > of
> > > lines....which is quite large in term of evaluation....Does anybody
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > They claim around 3 billlion lines a year are still being written; in
1999
> > it was 5 billion.
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2004-01-11T04:23:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20040110232337.19282.00002605@mb-m13.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
I personally maintain the billing/sales systems on our company's mainframe.  It
is one of several such systems but the only one in COBOL and the only mainframe
one.

It is relaatively small - I have aprox 18 on-line programs at about 1000 lines
of code each.  I'm not sure how many batch programs there are as I haven't
workied on them much lately and have lost track (I'm rewriting a lot of the
on-line programs because we're switching from VSAM to DB2 for the files they
access and adding features etc so they are foremost in my mind now).

The shipping, receiving, BOM, warehousing, and other systems on the mainframe
are also in COBOL and are much much bigger than mine.  

The only PC COBOL is the barcode system.

There are 8 1/2 programmers to maintain all this. 

All this being said if the company's time table is on track all this will be
gone in 2 years.  If I've counted correctly there are at least 120 programmers
for the new system.

The irony of all this is that I'm a contractor, and I'm also what is called by
my company a staff manager (some call them site managers) and one of my charges
is on the team dillegently working to put me out of a job ::sigh::.
```

> >
> > I have seen demonstrations of tools that can isolate COBOL code
functions

can
> > convert COBOL to Java (to quibble about whether it is GOOD Java or not
is
> > simply churlish; it is a start. The original COBOL may not have been
that
> > "good" either...).
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I dunno. Check out Caravel, PerCOBOL, COB2C, COBOL2JAVA (by Thomas Li, who
has contributed here), LeTS, CORECT (A toolset that was 6 years in
development and has some impressive capabilities), just for starters...

Ask THEM what they do about GO TO, I don't want to know...<G>.

> Do you have such a tool running?  I'd love to see the output from some
> goto-laden code.

As I said above, I saw some demos. I don't have, need, or want, such tools,
insofar as they convert COBOL to some other language.

I AM interested in tools that can take structured code (COBOL, RPG, PL/1)
and produce OO Based components from it. (These are in their early days yet,
but there are some very promising indications.)

The output from such tools would be a component (Object creator) with
defined Properties, Methods, and events.

The details of the method code are neither visible nor relevant. As it is
never going to be maintained (anymore than you would "maintain" a brick, a
bolt, a screw, or a PCB), the horrendous goto code may simply be frozen in
time for ever...

That's what "encapsulation" is all about.

Pete.
```
