###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-12T19:55:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com>`

```
This whole argument is silly.  

The many Open source COBOL compilers almost all convert to C first.

There has been - for YEARS - a true COBOL compiler which
translates/compiles to Java.  http://www.legacyj.com

So you can test this yourself.  Who CARES what the Java looks like -
no one is going to maintain it anyway.  (At least in Java!)



Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com>...

>  "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> goto-laden code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T23:00:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, C supports a goto/label structure, so a one-for-one conversion is 
possible.

LegacyJ, IIRC, produces its own class files for the JVM in bytecode -- 
not in Java-the-language. 

If you are going to convert to Java -- why not toss out the Cobol and 
continue on?  If you only want Cobol to run on a JVM there are plenty of 
solutions that do not require translation to Java first.

In article <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com>,
 thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

> This whole argument is silly.  
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> > goto-laden code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T23:33:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401142333.7fcab528@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401140923.42406213@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-400550.19081314012004@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> > > If you are going to convert to Java -- why not toss out the Cobol and 
> > > continue on?  If you only want Cobol to run on a JVM there are plenty of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fujitsu NetCobol would be the one that comes to mind. 

For JVM ? I think not.

> I hear they have also recently target the MSIL/CLR of .NET.

Yes, with a subset.
 
> Didn't MicroFocus make a NetExpress product that targeted JVM's?  I know 
> they target MSIL/CLR with the recent version, but I thought the product 
> was older than .NET -- what did they target previously?  There ought to 
> be a user here somewhere.

I think that you are confused.  Many PC and Unix systems (such as MF,
Accu, RM) have a 'virtual machine' run-time system that can run system
independent programs (eg .int).  But these have nothing to do with the
JVM.

Here is a list of languages that will run on the JVM:

http://grunge.cs.tu-berlin.de/~tolk/vmlanguages.html

PerCobol is the only Cobol listed.
```

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T23:12:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <217e491a.0401142333.7fcab528@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> For JVM ? I think not.

Fujitsu NetCobol is widely advertised as having a Java bytecode backend.    
Some quick cites:

http://developer.netscape.com/software/tools/listing/application/legacy/F
ujitsu/netcobol.htm

http://sunsite.uakom.sk/sunworldonline/swol-04-1998/swol-04-cobolconversi
on.html

http://cobolreport.com/columnists/tw/part2.asp


> > I hear they have also recently target the MSIL/CLR of .NET.
> 
> Yes, with a subset.

I'm not sure what you mean by subset -- CLR is CLR, you can instantiate, 
extend and use the objects and libraries built in for any of the .NET 
languages.  If only a subset exists -- why not use the full C# or VB set?


> > Didn't MicroFocus make a NetExpress product that targeted JVM's?  I know 
> > they target MSIL/CLR with the recent version, but I thought the product 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> JVM.

I did say I didn't know HOW NetExpress allowed things to play well with 
Java, thats why I asked for a user of it to clariify.  The do claim to 
play well with Java and to interoperate with various different bean 
containers.  (BTW, their current product web pages specifically says 
they target MSIL).


> Here is a list of languages that will run on the JVM:
> 
> http://grunge.cs.tu-berlin.de/~tolk/vmlanguages.html
> 
> PerCobol is the only Cobol listed.

That list is missing at least one that I can see right away -- the Mack 
Pascal compiler that was hacked up to target Jasmin assembler instead of 
x86 isn't there.
```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-85AC68.23122515012004@corp.supernews.com...
> In article <217e491a.0401142333.7fcab528@posting.google.com>,
>  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > If you are going to convert to Java -- why not toss out the Cobol
and
> > > > > continue on?  If you only want Cobol to run on a JVM there are
plenty
> > > > > of
> > > > > solutions that do not require translation to Java first.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think not also, so was very interested to see the links posted...

> Fujitsu NetCobol is widely advertised as having a Java bytecode backend.
> Some quick cites:
>
> http://developer.netscape.com/software/tools/listing/application/legacy/F
> ujitsu/netcobol.htm

I took this link and it is NOT a Fujitsu Corporation link. I read with
interest their description of the compiler I use on an almost daily basis
and found it is supposed to have facilities (like generating Java bytecodes)
which I was never aware of. I then did some searches on the version 7
documentation for facilities like EXEC HTML which this web site claims are
in there.

Nothing.

I don't know where they got their information from but it looks as if they
are describing something that is NOT
Fujitsu NetCOBOL.

>
> http://sunsite.uakom.sk/sunworldonline/swol-04-1998/swol-04-cobolconversi
> on.html
>

Don't know about this one as I received consistent 404s when accessing it.


> http://cobolreport.com/columnists/tw/part2.asp
>
>
This is a well written, but simply arguable article, with the same old
unsubstantiable statistics for COBOL that have been argued here. It is so
full of holes you could use it to strain pasta...

It also makes the same claims as the article above and I suspect the author
may have used the above Web Site to get his notes on Fujitsu COBOL (or maybe
he wrote the text on both sites...).

BOTTOM LINE:

To my knowledge, Fujitsu NetCOBOL does not generate Java code or even Java
bytecodes. But I could be wrong; the NetCOBOL RTS may indeed be a series of
Java bytecodes, just don't know.

If you search the Fujitsu Site there is no mention of it, so if it is true
they are certainly not making a noise about it. Certainly Java and NetCOBOL
interact well together, but that means little in this context. I can't find
any facilities in Fujitsu documentation or on their site which would show me
the Java bytecode generated by my compiles, if such code is indeed
generated.

Pete.
<snip>
```

Fujitsu and MicroFocus both let you "communicate" COBOL to Java - and
I know MicroFocus lets you "communicate" via Java Beans back to COBOL
"objects", but other than LegacyJ PerCOBOL I don't know of any other
solution for COBOL in the JVM.

- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffb301f_8@news.athenanews.com> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401140923.42406213@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-400550.19081314012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401150829.1e0bd470@posting.google.com> <TlHX8BGYgsBAFwy7@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <xfSdnSffv6KtjprdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <Waemj$BxaCCAFwJ6@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
>>
>> Failed miserably.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledge of it.

Neither. The failure was management.

Get a clutch of world class Java programmers and they will be unable to
create an efficient (working) world-wide oil and gas royalty payment system.
All of Java's horses and all of Java's men cannot create 50 million Social
Security checks every month.

COBOL has similar difficulties with flames and shoot-the-monkey pop-ups.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T18:57:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401151857.cb7c6e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3ffea4ac$0$385$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <3fff48f4_9@news.athenanews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E52F91.19390711012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401140923.42406213@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-400550.19081314012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401150829.1e0bd470@posting.google.com> <TlHX8BGYgsBAFwy7@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message news:<TlHX8BGYgsBAFwy7@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>...
> As an aside in this debate, has anyone taken a serious Cobol application 
> and written the Java equivalent? If so, I'd be interested in any 
> observations.

What's the definition of a "serious COBOL application"?

One thing I like to do with "new" compilers is check sort and indexed
file access.  I did the same with some production code and the LegacyJ
Per-COBOL compiler.  It really does turn the COBOL into Java.  I was
pleased with the performance and ease of use of the compiler.  It even
works with COBOL Sp2 via simple calls.

I was really impressed with the native indexed file support.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-18T21:41:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401182141.4cff7595@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <bfdfc3e8.0401121955.724a878@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401140923.42406213@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-400550.19081314012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401150829.1e0bd470@posting.google.com> <TlHX8BGYgsBAFwy7@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0401151857.cb7c6e2@posting.google.com> <ca4kDeBpYCCAFwJH@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
One answer below...

Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message news:<ca4kDeBpYCCAFwJH@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>...
> In message <bfdfc3e8.0401151857.cb7c6e2@posting.google.com>, Thane 
> Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> writes
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> And how easy would the Java code have been to support?

Suprisingly - not too difficult.  However, one has to ask WHY?  When
it can easily be maintained using COBOL - why maintain it in Java -
you get the best of both worlds.  COBOL clarity and ease of
maintenance, and Java JVM execution.

PerCOBOL makes excellent use of a ".jar" file that has many of their
functions embedded in it - making the Java that is created via the
compiler exceptionally clear and easy to follow.
```

- **From:** star_ledge@yahoo.com (Bounder)
- **Date:** 2004-01-21T05:04:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2613cb8f.0401210504.44697b30@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <joe_zitzelberger-F0B22D.23002913012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401140923.42406213@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-400550.19081314012004@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0401150829.1e0bd470@posting.google.com> <TlHX8BGYgsBAFwy7@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0401151857.cb7c6e2@posting.google.com> <ca4kDeBpYCCAFwJH@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0401182141.4cff7595@posting.google.com> <gAzL1YAB1YDAFwHF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message news:<gAzL1YAB1YDAFwHF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>...
> In message <bfdfc3e8.0401182141.4cff7595@posting.google.com>, Thane 
> Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> writes
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the old Cobol dinosaurs from the equation.

It's a two way street.  The Java beannies are dinos too.  I migrated a
cobol system from legacy to NT.  The Java beannies said "it couldn't
be done".  I proved the proprietary legacy file system calls by
prototype on NT.  I prototyped queues, batch requirements, flat file
programming, scheduling, etc.  The Java beannies said "it wouldn't
work".  They wrote letters to management expressing their dire
concerns and our so-called lack of foresight and understanding of
modern computing.

The Java beannies had estimated 1.5 years and 250K to convert the
system.  We did it in 4 months, including month end compliance
testing.  Our group juggled our work load and did the project on the
side.  We spent 5K on a compiler.  Walking down isles, the Java guys
wouldn't look us in the eye.

It's not about technology, it's about money, but too many in computing
are all to ready to impose their technological prowess at any cost. 
Too often systems are converted to the likes of Java because people
don't have clue "how" to do it any other way and fail to give
alternate solutions fair consideration.  If the legacy guys are
braindead about "new" technologies, the Java beannies are braindead
about system concepts in general.  They had rewritten the
corporation's core manufacturing system in Java on Unix.  They had
fifteen percent implementation and required four times more resources
than legacy to keep their side running.   With eighty five percent of
the implementations, the legacy resuorces cost less.  The CEO wised up
and sent the whole thing to India.


> 
> >When
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >compiler exceptionally clear and easy to follow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Cobol to language w/o goto (was: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Q]: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T06:20:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0zNKb.2083$Ub6.63921@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<vvmjob9ke9km42@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <vvmjob9ke9km42@corp.supernews.com>`

```


LX-i wrote:
> Francis ANDRE wrote:
> (snip)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

In 1996, as part of a Y2K project, we counted up how many COBOL 
programs and lines of code we had in production in order to estimate 
the scope of our Y2K work.  It worked to something like 4,500 
production programs and about 7 million lines of code.  This was a 
shop with maybe 100 to 150 actual programmers.  Of course, the number 
is a moving target as new programs are written, existing programs are 
enhanced (usually resulting in a slight increase in LOC), and even 
occasionally deleting an old program.

I can easily believe the number would be in the billions just for the 
USA, but there's no way to actually count it.  We missed some programs 
that weren't stored in the proper libraries.
```

> You can also get into the whole "what's a line of code".  Pre or post
> copybook expansion?  Comments - do they count?  Etc etc...
>
> A relatively small system I maintain has 140,000 lines of code
> precopybook expansion and including comments.

I count copy code once, not for each use. I believe comments are an
integral part of source code. If the comments aren't needed, take them
out. If they are needed, then they are part of the code. :-)

I know of an entire class of monolithic COBOL programs approaching half a
million lines of (very dense) source code apiece.  I grant that these are
*generated* programs, but they are COBOL programs that are in production
nonetheless.  I suspect an estimate of hundreds of billions of lines of
COBOL code in use worldwide is too low.

    -Chuck Stevens

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@nospam.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T15:59:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zt_Kb.47988$BA6.1027935@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ve_Kb.43285$Pg1.41050@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <T5ednWlIjblB9maiRVn-tw@giganews.com> <bthbcr$1i2l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <YNZKb.46088$BA6.1005989@news20.bellglobal.com> <ve_Kb.43285$Pg1.41050@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Donald,
>   I know that Chuck wouldn't be (very) familiar with the IBM mainframe "code
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The standard Cobol program reduces down into a perfectly defined OOP 
object, with each data name being a property, and each sentence being a 
method. Paragraphs are collections(as are group items and sections), and 
the Cobol spec is the defintion.

When you think of them in those terms, then making the platform a 
property and supplying a method to rewrite the code by platform makes a 
lot of sense.  From a practical standpoint, it seems to me you could go 
that far by re-writting nothing but the copy statement ... the rest is 
encapsulated data. Yoou could also re-write the code with each sentence 
as an OOP method, and doing simple "sentence-step" debugging, all 
generically.  That is the type of thing that "getting it" means to me 
... addressing your post of a few weeks back.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T15:22:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b83edb9af1b12f08de7cbe711b3408ea@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr> <T5ednWlIjblB9maiRVn-tw@giganews.com> <bthbcr$1i2l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> I know of an entire class of monolithic COBOL programs approaching half a
> million lines of (very dense) source code apiece.  I grant that these are
> *generated* programs, but they are COBOL programs that are in production
> nonetheless.  I suspect an estimate of hundreds of billions of lines of
> COBOL code in use worldwide is too low.

I agree, Chuck. My guess is that the U.S. government alone has more than
200 billion lines of COBOL. How many states, counties and cities in the
U.S. alone have COBOL shops twenty or thirty years old, with staffs of
COBOL coders cranking out code all that time? Now factor in the number
of businesses in the U.S. that have COBOL systems. I would not be very
surprised if in the U.S. alone there is over a trillion (10**12) lines of
COBOL. A couple of factors non-COBOL'ers don't realize is that most
COBOL programs are unique, and that there are lots of 20 and 30 (maybe
even 40) year old COBOL systems out there, chugging away, that nobody
much is even aware of. Some are running on emulators of emulators and
haven't been compiled in decades. Y2K flushed out many of those. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr>
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T11:35:49+01:00

- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`

Here are some figures commonly found on the web.

>> 75% of business data is processed in COBOL (Source: Gartner).
>> There are 180 billion to 200 billion lines of COBOL in use worldwide
(Gartner).
>> 15% of new applications are written in COBOL (Gartner).
>>  Replacement costs for COBOL systems estimated at $25 per line are in the
hundreds of billions of dollars (Tactical Strategy Group).

According to your own beliefs and experiences, do these numbers reflect more
or less the "reality" of Cobol???

3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr...

of

#### ↳ Re: How many lines of Cobol running worldwide???

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-07T11:14:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ebd6f9864cd97c8e9c4316355ab5109@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ffafcb5$0$386$afc38c87@news.easynet.fr>`

```
"Francis ANDRE" <francis.andre@easynet.fr> wrote:
>
> I made some research on the net to find out how many lines of cobol were
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> estimations??

In the late 90's the Gortner Group claimed that between 60%-80% of all
code in use was COBOL. Don't remember any specific counts. But the
U.S. government undoubtedly has in the hundreds of millions of lines, at
the very least, very possibly billions (10**9). There are certainly billions
of lines of COBOL in existence. Are there trillions (10**12)? Not sure,
but it may not be entirely out of the question.

Considering that so much of the code being written in the 'languages du
jour' these days is obsolete in a few years, I seriously doubt that COBOL
is in danger of being replaced as the language in which more operational
code exists than all others put together. :-)
```

## Thread: Encryption algorithm — 18 msgs

### Encryption algorithm

- **From:** aul1231@yahoo.com (Vik Mehta)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
appreciated.

Thanks,
Vik
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

Vik Mehta wrote:
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
> appreciated.

Find a 10-character word with no repeating letters (i.e., "manuscript") then
INSPECT ... CONVERTING
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T19:58:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vvs2mvao3plme2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<wKudndOXM6_I0WCiRVn-ig@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com> <C9WdnVOLZaLx52Gi4p2dnA@giganews.com> <wKudndOXM6_I0WCiRVn-ig@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> JerryMouse wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Oops. This little technique is good for converting numbers only.

How so?  This is the test program I wrote using Fujitsu 3...

=-=-=-=-=

  Identification Division.
    Program-ID.  ROT13.
*>_______________________________________________________________

  Data Division.
    Working-Storage Section.

  01  Test-String   Pic X(13) Value Spaces.
*>_______________________________________________________________

  Procedure Division.

  Lets-Do-It.
	
      Move "FRIDAYSRGREAT" to Test-String

      Display "Before [" Test-String "]"

      Inspect Test-String
        Converting "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
                to "NOPQRSTUVWXYZABCDEFGHIJKLM"

      Display "After  [" Test-String "]"

      Stop Run.

=-=-=-=-=

Now, here are the results...

=-=-=-=-=

Before [FRIDAYSRGREAT]
After  [SEVQNLFETERNG]

=-=-=-=-=

It found all the "A"'s and "R"'s...
```

Yours works great. Mine was for converting numbers to obscure letters. You
sometimes see price tags with letters beneath the numbers. These are the
"code" for the cost price so the merchant knows his bottom haggle limit. In
my example, I was using
MANUSCRIPT to map the digits 0123456789 to something like: ANUS.

Bad example.

Oh well.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T11:00:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401091100.52c64437@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com> <C9WdnVOLZaLx52Gi4p2dnA@giganews.com> <wKudndOXM6_I0WCiRVn-ig@giganews.com> <vvs2mvao3plme2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote

> >>Find a 10-character word with no repeating letters (i.e.,
> >>"manuscript") then INSPECT ... CONVERTING
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> How so?  This is the test program I wrote using Fujitsu 3...

What JerryMouse suggested is only good for numerics because he said
use 10 different characters for the converting key.  You used 26
characters and this allows you to encrypt strings with 26 different
values.  You would need a longer one for strings that include lower
case, digits, space and punctuation.

Actually encrypting is never a problem, it can be done with any random
string, it is decrpting back to the original text that is the problem,
this requires the encrypting key to be carefully chosen.
```

- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com> <C9WdnVOLZaLx52Gi4p2dnA@giganews.com> <wKudndOXM6_I0WCiRVn-ig@giganews.com> <vvs2mvao3plme2@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0401091100.52c64437@posting.google.com> <ldGdnYztwYNnrWKi4p2dnA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> THEQUICKBROWNFOXJUMPEDOVERTHELAZYDOG.
  ^^^           ^     ^     ^^^ 

> > Actually encrypting is never a problem, it can be done with any random
> > string, it is decrpting back to the original text that is the problem,
> > this requires the encrypting key to be carefully chosen.

Thank you for an excellent example of a poorly chosen substitution
key.  Using that phrase in an INSPECT .. CONVERTING will give 'E' for
'C', 'U' and 'Y', and 'O' for 'K', 'O' and 'W', which make it
impossible to recover the original text.
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T10:40:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ffc7d3e_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

```

"Vik Mehta" <aul1231@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com...
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
> appreciated.
>

Presumably you want the encryption as a COBOL Subroutine?

You will need a COBOL compiler that supports the XOR function.

(Microfus and Fujitsu do.)

"Here's one I prepared earlier..." (MUCH earlier...over 6 years ago, in
fact. It works extremely well despite being simple, and is ideal for short
strings that need encryption before writing to a database or file (like
passwords).)

Remove anything in square brackets "[...]" before compiling.

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. 'HERACrpt'.
*AUTHOR. Peter E. C. Dashwood.
* This program encrypts/decrypts an 8 byte password.
*
*DATE_WRITTEN. August 1997.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 configuration section.
 source-computer. IBM-PC.
 object-computer. IBM-PC.
*------------------------  DATA   DIVISION  ----------------------
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  encryption-key  pic x(8) value 'ANYTHING'.

[You can set this key to be any character string you like. My applications
do not use "ANYTHING"... It must be the same length as the field you wish to
encrypt, so in your case you need to make it 13 bytes and set a value in it.
(I suggest: ANYTHINGULIKE while you are testing it...<G>  This encryption
key can be any combination of  characters you like. You don't have to
remember it, but you should make sure that the source code of your
encryption module is not freely available, for obvious reasons. (You could
save it as an encrypted WORD document <G>)]

 01  xor-return-code  pic s9(4) comp-5.
 01  hex-table.
     12 filler  pic x value '0'.
     12 filler  pic x value '1'.
     12 filler  pic x value '2'.
     12 filler  pic x value '3'.
     12 filler  pic x value '4'.
     12 filler  pic x value '5'.
     12 filler  pic x value '6'.
     12 filler  pic x value '7'.
     12 filler  pic x value '8'.
     12 filler  pic x value '9'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'A'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'B'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'C'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'D'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'E'.
     12 filler  pic x value 'F'.
 01  filler redefines hex-table.
     12 hex-values        pic x occurs 16
                                indexed by hv-x1.

 01  ws-byte-stuff.
     12 filler  pic x.
     12 lod-byte  pic x.
 01  ws-binary redefines ws-byte-stuff pic s9(4) comp.

 01  quotient  pic 99.
 01  rem   pic 99.

 01  num   pic s9(5) comp-5.

 01  two-bytes.
     12 hod-hex  pic x.
     12 lod-hex  pic x.

 01  work-string.
     12 ws-char   pic x occurs 16
                                indexed by ws-x1.
 01  xor-len  pic s9(9) comp-5.


 LINKAGE SECTION.

[The module takes a single parameter that includes a return code, an
indication whether to ENCRYPT or DECRYPT (there's not much point encrypting
stuff if you can't decrypt it...) and a passed string that will be 26 bytes
in your case (It is twice the length of the string you want encrypted, as it
must return each encrypted character as a hex byte.i.e. 2 bytes per byte of
source)]

 01  encryption-block.
     12 eb-return  pic x.
        88 eb-ok   value zero.
        88 eb-bad-action value '1'.
        88 eb-bad-char value '2'.
     12 eb-action  pic x.
        88 HeraEncrypting  value 'E'.
        88 HeraDecrypting  value 'D'.
     12 eb-string  pic x(16).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Encryption-block.
 MAIN SECTION.
 a000.
     move zero to xor-len
     inspect eb-string
             tallying xor-len for
             characters BEFORE space

     evaluate TRUE
        when HeraEncrypting
             perform xorit
             perform asc2hex
        when HeraDecrypting
             perform hex2asc
             perform xorit
        when other
             set eb-bad-action to TRUE
     end-evaluate
       .
 a999.
     exit program.
*--------------------------------------------------------
 xorit  section.
 x000.

     CALL "CBL_XOR"
          USING encryption-key
                eb-string
             BY VALUE xor-len
          RETURNING xor-return-code
     END-CALL
     if xor-return-code = zero
        set eb-ok to TRUE
     else
        set eb-bad-char to TRUE
     end-if
     .
 x999.
     exit.
*--------------------------------------------------------
 asc2hex   section.
 ah000.
     move eb-string to work-string
     move spaces to eb-string
     perform
        varying ws-x1
           from 1
             by 1
            until ws-x1 > xor-len
              move low-values to ws-byte-stuff
              move ws-char (ws-x1) to lod-byte
              divide 16 into ws-binary
                     giving  quotient
                     remainder rem
              add 1 to quotient
              add 1 to rem
              move hex-values (quotient) to hod-hex
              move hex-values (rem)      to lod-hex
              string
                 eb-string
                    delimited by space
                 two-bytes
                    delimited by size
                       into eb-string
              end-string
     end-perform
     .
 ah999.
     exit.

*--------------------------------------------------------
 hex2asc   section.
 ha000.
     move eb-string to work-string
     move spaces to eb-string
     perform
        varying ws-x1
           from 1
             by 1
            until ws-x1 > xor-len
              move low-values to ws-byte-stuff
              set hv-x1 to 1
              search hex-values
                  at end set eb-bad-char to true
                         go to ha999
                  when hex-values (hv-x1) = ws-char (ws-x1)
                       set num to hv-x1
                       subtract 1 from num
                       multiply num  by 16 giving ws-binary
              end-search
              set ws-x1 up by 1
              set hv-x1 to 1
              search hex-values
                  at end set eb-bad-char to true
                         go to ha999
                  when hex-values (hv-x1) = ws-char (ws-x1)
                       set num to hv-x1
                       subtract 1 from num
                       add num to ws-binary
              end-search
              string
                 eb-string
                    delimited by space
                 lod-byte
                    delimited by size
                       into eb-string
              end-string
     end-perform
     divide xor-len by 2 giving xor-len
     .
 ha999.
     exit.


*----------------    END OF PROGRAM 'HERAcrpt' ----------------



> Thanks,
> Vik

You're welcome...

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rNwOsS0P2A0M2@comcast.net>

    I also use this method, but be aware that it can be broken.  Ie, if you
use
it to encrypt a password in a configuration file, it will likely have
trailing spaces,
which would be the same every time.

    But it is quick and easy.

    Note that you don't need to differentiate between encrpt and decrypt.
It
works both ways.  Eg, if you do it twice with the same password, you get
the original clear text back.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-10T12:22:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fff3850_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com> <3ffc7d3e_6@news.athenanews.com> <n9idnZb92_Yws2KiRVn-gg@comcast.com>`

```
That's very interesting, Russell.

I actually "invented" this technique based on my knowledge of Boolean
Algebra.

I didn't realise it was a standard way of doing it.

The XOR will certainly give you back what you started with, but I need to
think some more (and do some tests, which I currently don't have time for)
before I can be persuaded that there is no need for the decrypt.

Trailing spaces SHOULD not be a problem and they SHOULDN'T encrypt to the
same character. (At least, if they do, then I have got it horribly wrong
<G>.)

As for being broken, yes, it is not an algorithm that could be used for
matters of national security, but it is WAY better than a straight
transformation...

Pete.

(Top post, nothing below.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rNwOsS0P2A0M2@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-10T01:37:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com> <3ffc7d3e_6@news.athenanews.com> <n9idnZb92_Yws2KiRVn-gg@comcast.com> <3fff3850_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
top post.

    Actually, under the proper conditions, this method is the very best
cypher known.  No one, at all, can break it if you use a "key" that is
the same length as the cleartext, and NEVER reuse the key again.

    Called the "one time pad" method.

    I understand that this is the sort of thing that goes on inside RAID
drives, at
least a 3 drive unit.  I have no idea how they handle more than 3 drives.
You put "A"
on drive 01, "B" on drive 02, XOR those two (different) things, and put
result on drive 03.

    Any two will allow you to recover original "A" and "B".

    The trailing spaces bit assumes that you always use the same key, and
never
try to obscure the data.

    The matter was not important enough to go further in my case.

Thanks for that.

I'm feeling pretty smug that I thought of it for myself...<G>

(Don't worry, that'll wear off pretty quickly when I read the responses to
my other mail...<G>)

Back in Neanderthal times when we programmed computers in things like BAL
and PLAN, I remembered a little trick we did with XOR, that enabled the
swapping of two fields without the need for an intermediate one (storage was
expensive in those days). It was that long distant memory that gave me the
clue for the algorithm.

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T16:58:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3184357468c6962ecfa37d895e7e953c@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

```
"Vik Mehta" <aul1231@yahoo.com> wrote:
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
> appreciated.


How 'strongly' do you want to encrypt it? If you simply don't want it to
be read by casual observation, a technique similar to the one JerryMouse
proposed is sufficient. If you want it to resist analysis, you will need a
more powerful method.

For a simple character substitution method, reasonably secure from
casual decription, but vulnerable to analytical methods, run the
following QBASIC program. You can copy/past it Notepad or other
text editor for input into QBASIC or most any QuickBasic compatible
compiler or interpreter such as PowerBasic. Don't past it directly
into the BASIC IDE, or the indenting will get messed up. If you don't
have one, email me and I will send you a Windows/DOS executable.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
DIM Chars(255)

FOR I = 0 TO 255
   Chars(I) = I
NEXT

OPEN "TRANTAB.COB" FOR OUTPUT AS #1
PRINT #1, "       01  TRANSLATION-STRING."
PRINT #1, "           03  TS-PLAIN."
FOR I = 0 TO 255
   PRINT #1, "               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"; CHR$(34); RIGHT$("0" + HEX$(Chars(I)), 2); CHR$(34); "."
NEXT

RANDOMIZE TIMER
FOR I = 0 TO 255
   J = INT(RND * 256)
   SWAP Chars(I), Chars(J)
NEXT
FOR I = 0 TO 255
   J = INT(RND * 256)
   SWAP Chars(I), Chars(J)
NEXT

PRINT #1, "           03  TS-ENCRIPTED."
FOR I = 0 TO 255
   PRINT #1, "               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"; CHR$(34); RIGHT$("0" + HEX$(Chars(I)), 2); CHR$(34); "."
NEXT

CLOSE #1
END
---------------------------------------------------------------------

which will create a disk file TRANTAB.COB containing a COBOL table
description like this:

       01  TRANSLATION-STRING.
           03  TS-PLAIN.
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"00".
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"01".
                   ... (252 more entries, "02"-"FD")
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"FE".
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"FF".
           03  TS-ENCRIPTED.
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"C5".
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"2F".
                    ... (252 more random, unique entries)
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"5F".
               05  FILLER    PIC X(01) VALUE x"72".

where the values under TS-ENCRIPTED are all the hex values 0-255
in random order. Each time you run the program you will get a different
sequence. This is to prevent anyone else from using this program to
easily decript your data. If your COBOL compiler doesn't accept the
x".." form of specifying hexidecimal literals, you will need to reformat
it for your compiler.

Copy the above 01 record into Working Storage of any program that
will encript or decript.

To encrypt a plain key use this:

           INSPECT <field>
               CONVERTING TS-PLAIN
                       TO TS-ENCRIPTED

To decrypt an encrypted key use this:

           INSPECT <field>
               CONVERTING TS-ENCRIPTED
                       TO TS-PLAIN

This should encript/decript any kind of data you can put in a PIC X(??)
data item.
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-08T19:14:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vvs048nece1m18@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

```
Vik Mehta wrote:
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
> appreciated.

There are several different things you could probably do...  Some may
consider these "obscuring" rather than "encryption", but what you get
from these is worth every bit of what you're paying for them.  :)

  - ROT-13 - This can be done by Inspect...Converting.

      Inspect My-String
        Converting "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
                to "NOPQRSTUVWXYZABCDEFGHIJKLM"

  - Reversing - Again, Inspect...Converting.

      Inspect My-String
        Converting "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
                to "ZYXWVUTSRQPONMLKJIHGFEDCBA"

Those two only work for letters - of course, the advantage to these is 
that you run the string through the same algorithm to encode and decode. 
  There are some other options as well...

  - You could convert the string to a 39-digit number by using a loop 
combined with Function Ord (which you'd decode with Function Char).

      01  My-39-Digit-Number.
          12  My-3-Digits   Occurs 13 Times Pic 9(03).

      Perform Varying My-Idx from 1 by 1
        Until My-Idx > 13
          Move Function Ord (My-String (My-Idx:1))
            to My-3-Digits  (My-Idx)
      End-Perform

This has the advantage of working with pretty much any character you'd 
have in your ASCII character set.

If you wanted to get more fancy, you could strip the highest bit off, 
making the characters 7 bits each, then cramming them all together. 
Viewed in 8-bit format, it would look like junk, and it'll handle most 
keyboard characters.  Of course, that's a bit more complex, and without 
knowing the environment you're in, it's more difficult to put together a 
quick-and-dirty solution.

There is one more option - if you're using a database system that had 
functions for one-way hashes (such as MD5 or SHA32), you can utilize 
them from within your COBOL program.

      Exec SQL
          Select MD5(:My-String)
            Into :My-Hash
            From [Any_Table]
      End-Exec

What exactly are you wanting to do with this encrypted string?  That has 
a lot to do with how you go about encrypting it.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T13:06:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0401091306.1b59f12@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

```
aul1231@yahoo.com (Vik Mehta) wrote in message news:<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>...
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Vik

Hello Vik,

I think most suppliers provide APIs or subroutines that can do this
for you.  As others have mentioned, unless your requirement is just to
prevent casual observation, you should be very wary of roll-your-own
solutions because cryptography is a problematic subject liable to many
pitfalls that allow crackers to easily circumvent your encryption. 
There are several books on the subject that make this point and I have
no doubt there are also several online discussions.  There is probably
at least one cryptograpby news group.

Regards 

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption algorithm

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-10T08:05:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vuGdnemtAJYkm53dRVn-jw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0401070700.67c3ec95@posting.google.com>`

```
Vik Mehta wrote:
> I am looking for a simple encryption algorithm to encrypt a 13
> character alphanumeric string in a COBOL program.  Any help will be
> appreciated.

Here's another thought: Do you ever need to de-crypt the result?

Passwords, for example, may never need de-crypting - just one-time hashing
and you store the hash.

For (a simple) example. Suppose you assign a numeric value to each letter,
1, 2, 3, 4, etc.

Add the values for the password

DCBA = 4+3+2+1 = 10 [or 1*4 + 2*3 + 3*2 * 4*1 = 20 or more exotic as
desired]

Store the "10," repeat the hash each time a password is required, and
compare the results to the stored value. This way, the original passwords
are never stored on the computer.
```

Jeff wrote:
> I have a ridiculous question, that I hope someone knows of a utl\ility
> that may work. I am missing source code, but have the object created
> by the missing source. Is there anyway of converting it back to get
> the source? I'm sure there isn't but what the hell, figured I'd try

On the IBM mainframe, there is a company that performs this magic act. 
It is called Essential Systems Technical Consulting - "ESTC".  Their 
technology was employed for Y2K under the name Source Recovery Co.

Since the object module is target platform dependent, I assume that 
extending this capability to other platforms would be quite labor 
intensive.  There is so much COBOL on the mainframe that the effort was 
worthwhile for ESTC.
```

## Thread: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** mkbobba@yahoo.com (Bob)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T03:51:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

We have MF cobol running on Sun Solaris server. Our's is a
Datawarehouse setup. We get EBCDIC files from mainframe. We transform
those files and load them in to a SYBASE datawarehouse. We use COBOL
to transform and Shell scripts to load the data.

My today's first question is concerning some input fields defined as

01 INPUT-FIELD3  PIC S9(03) COMP-3.

What is a comp-3 definition?. Some cobol books gave me an idea that
this type of definitions are for space saving.
Does that mean this field will not occupy 4 character spaces in memory
and less. If this is correct, how much space they will occupy?

Can somebody point to some documentation where I can learn all about
COMP-3 fields?.

Second question:

Scenario

We are moving a PIC S9(03) COMP-3. field to a PIC X(03).

If the value in the input field is '100' will it be moved as '10 ' or
'100' in the receiving field?.

One of the senior developers in our shop says that it will be received
as '10 ' with a space in the 3rd position.

Can somebody clarify this and give me some examples of move?.

I can post the piece of code if somebody wants to look further and
help me.

Thank you,

Murali
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T07:37:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rsydnZLh5qQ-M2Oi4p2dnA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com>`

```
Bob wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> and less. If this is correct, how much space they will occupy?

Correct. COMP-3 fields occupy from 1 to 10 bytes.

>
> Can somebody point to some documentation where I can learn all about
> COMP-3 fields?.

You really don't need any. See below.

>
> Second question:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> We are moving a PIC S9(03) COMP-3. field to a PIC X(03).

No known compiler will let you do that (or at least it shouldn't).

>
> If the value in the input field is '100' will it be moved as '10 ' or
> '100' in the receiving field?.

The move you've described above is not allowed or, at the least, will
generated a truncated value (plus, where does the sign go or how is it
represented in the "X(3)" destination. You should move the C-3 field to a
numeric display field first.

>
> One of the senior developers in our shop says that it will be received
> as '10 ' with a space in the 3rd position.
>
> Can somebody clarify this and give me some examples of move?.

COMP-3 is both a space-saver and optimizer. Space saving is obvious, but
many computers have base-10 logic boards which process C-3 fields directly.

C-3 fields look like this (each character is a half-byte or nibble)

NN NN NN ... NS

where "N" is a digit (note: a DIGIT, 1-9). The "..." means as many bytes as
necessary to satisfy the length definition of the field. "S" is the
algebraic sign of the field: F,A,C,E are all considered positive; B,D imply
negative.

So, then, examples, decimal to C-3 (bytes of storage needed):
1 = 1F (1)
10 = 01 0F (2)
100 = 10 0F (2)
1000 = 01 00 0F (3)
-1 = 1D (1)
-1234 = 01 23 4D (3)
-12.34 = 01 23 4D (3) [decimal positioning is defined by the data defintion
and is not carried with the data]

In your example, PIC 9(3) COMP-3, occupies 2 bytes of memory and can contain
99 9D through 99 9F (-999 thru 999+).

You can move a C-3 field to a numeric display field:

MOVE [C-3] TO [S9(3)],

but don't move a C-3 field to an "X" field.

I'm sure someone will post a link to a grand exposition of COMP-3 fields; I
just don't happen to know one off-hand (although I suspect Google is your
friend).
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** nagiscool@yahoo.co.in (Nagaraj Reddy)
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T12:21:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f078b180.0401091221.7c00b9ec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com>`

```
mkbobba@yahoo.com (Bob) wrote in message news:<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
This field will not occupy 4 character spaces instead it saves the
data in Hexa Decimal in 2 bytes. Sign uses 1/2 byte and 3 digits are
stored in 1 1/2 bytes.
It is stored in Hexa Decimal to make faster computing and save space.

> Can somebody point to some documentation where I can learn all about
> COMP-3 fields?.
> 

I cannot correctly point out to documentation but I can confidently
say it.
If you need verification you can refer to IBM COBOL lang reference
manuals.

> Second question:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

After making the above move the results cannot be predicted easily
because comp-3 fields are stored in HEX for pure calculating purposes
whereas the other data item with PIC X(03) is purely for display.
If you still need it's internal storege here it is:
In comp-3 field value '+100' will be stored as C1 00
In display i.e with PIC X(03) value '100' will be internally stored as
F1 F0 F0

My suggetion to get the desired results would be to declare another
data item with PIC 9(03) and then use COMPUTE statement to transfer
the value.
for example.

77 A PIC S9(03) COMP-3.
77 B PIC S9(03).
..............

COMPUTE B=A.

If you use MOVE statment then you cannot get the expected results for
sure b'coz it just transfers the internal storage values from one data
item to other. In this case since internal storage is different it is
always better and preferred to use COMPUTE statement.

If you still need the value to transferred to the dataitem with PIC
x(03) , then you can only move the contents from data item with PIC
9(03).
You cannot direwctly move from comp-3 field. you should definitely use
the intermediate field like B PIC s9(03).

 
  
> I can post the piece of code if somebody wants to look further and
> help me.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Murali
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-09T16:52:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6c-dnWgU3tszrWKiRVn-jw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com> <f078b180.0401091221.7c00b9ec@posting.google.com>`

```
Nagaraj Reddy wrote:


I hope you will permit a few corrections.

>>
> This field will not occupy 4 character spaces instead it saves the
> data in Hexa Decimal in 2 bytes. Sign uses 1/2 byte and 3 digits are
> stored in 1 1/2 bytes.
> It is stored in Hexa Decimal to make faster computing and save space.

No. There is no hexadecimal notation involved in COMP-3 data.

>> Second question:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> In comp-3 field value '+100' will be stored as C1 00

Not on any machine I've ever seen. 00 1C on IBM. Maybe C1 00 on some Intel
representations.

> In display i.e with PIC X(03) value '100' will be internally stored as
> F1 F0 F0

On EBCDIC. Different for ASCII.

>
> My suggetion to get the desired results would be to declare another
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> always better and preferred to use COMPUTE statement.

No. The compiler will generate the necessary conversions when you use MOVE A
TO B.

ADD, COMPUTE, and (where permissable) MOVE generate identical results. You
can take that to the bank.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
"Bob" <mkbobba@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com...
<snip>
> Scenario
>
> We are moving a PIC S9(03) COMP-3. field to a PIC X(03).

<snip>

Can you tell us what you WANT to happen?  If the "sending" field has the value
of "-123" (regardless of how it is stored), exactly what do you WANT to be in
the "3 byte" receiving field?

My *guess* is that you want to have an application that stores the value "-123"
or possibly "123-" in a 4 (not 3) byte receiving field.  If this is the case,
then what you want to do is something like (and there are a number of ways to do
this).

01  Sending-Comp-3   Pic S9(3)  Comp-3.
01  4-byte-alphanumeric.
     05   Display-Numeric   Pic +9(3).
   ...
Move Sending-Comp-3  to Display-Numeric

display "4 byte field:" 4-byte-alphanumeric
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** mkbobba@yahoo.com (Bob)
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T07:26:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<XqILb.977$1e.543@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>...
> "Bob" <mkbobba@yahoo.com> wrote in message
> news:64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> display "4 byte field:" 4-byte-alphanumeric



Sending field is an input record of
the County Code numbers storing numbers 1 to 100.
           05  CNG-COUNTY                       PIC S9(03) COMP-3.
Receving field is 

01  WS-LOOKUP-SEARCH-CR-CTY-NUM   PIC X(03) VALUE SPACES

       01  COUNTY-REGION-TABLE.
           05  CR-TABLE OCCURS 500 TIMES
               INDEXED BY CR-IDX
               ASCENDING KEY CR-COUNTY-NUMBER.
               10  CR-COUNTY-NUMBER         PIC X(03).
               10  CR-COUNTY-NAME           PIC X(30).
               10  CR-REGION-FOR-COUNTY     PIC X(01).
               10  CR-TERRITORY-FOR-COUNTY  PIC X(01).
               10  CR-FEDERAL-FIPS-CODE     PIC 9(03).
               10  CR-TIMESTAMP-OF-LAST-CHG PIC X(26).
               10  CR-USER-WHO-CHANGED-BY   PIC X(08).

       01  WS-CR-MAX                        PIC 9(04) COMP-5 VALUE 500.
       01  WS-LOOKUP-SEARCH-CR-CTY-NUM      PIC X(03) VALUE SPACES.
       01  WS-FOUND-CR-COUNTY-NUMBER        PIC X(01) VALUE SPACES.

Moving this input field into the receiving field and
performing binary search.

           IF  CNG-COUNTY                    OF WFINPUT-REC
               NUMERIC
->             MOVE CNG-COUNTY               OF WFINPUT-REC
                   TO WS-LOOKUP-SEARCH-CR-CTY-NUM
               PERFORM S610-000-COUNTY-BINARY-SEARCH
               IF  WS-FOUND-CR-COUNTY-NUMBER = 'N'
                   ADD +1                   TO
                       FIELD-BAD            OF FILE-I(FILE-NO FLD-NO)
                   SET FLD-NO               TO
                       I-FIPS-COUNTY-CODE   OF SSFAC100-INFO
                   ADD +1                   TO
                       FIELD-BAD            OF FILE-I(FILE-NO FLD-NO)
               ELSE  

So far as per the posts I have read, this search should not work
and strangely it is working. We wonder how it is working.

For ex: It should work only if 34 stored in the COMP-3 field
is received as 34 by the receiving field and matched.


Thanks.

Bob

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T18:25:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0401171825.76bb32b6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com> <XqILb.977$1e.543@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <64713ce9.0401150726.38690c9c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Bob,

According to my COBOL II manual (admittedly somewhat dated) numeric
INTEGER moves to A/N fields is permitted. Non-integer moves are not.

If the sending field is signed (as in your ex.) an unsigned value is
used in the MOVE. Since you expect values 1 thru 100 this presents no
problem.

In addition, the max value possible in the COMP-3 field is 999;
therefore a 3 byte receiving field [pic X(03)] is sufficient for your
needs and no spaces will be inserted into the receiving field. I'd
restate your ex. as follows: "It should work only if 034 stored in the
COMP-3 field is received as 034 by the receiving field and matched."
It will be.

The code you posted should (and does) work fine. I'm curious though
about how the search is conducted.

Regards, Jack.




mkbobba@yahoo.com (Bob) wrote in message news:<64713ce9.0401150726.38690c9c@posting.google.com>...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<XqILb.977$1e.543@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>...
> > "Bob" <mkbobba@yahoo.com> wrote in message
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** "Easton Beymer" <eastonbeymer@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-11T02:12:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kk9Mb.50785$gN.32869@fed1read05>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com>`

```
> 01 INPUT-FIELD3  PIC S9(03) COMP-3.
>
> What is a comp-3 definition?. Some cobol books gave me an idea that
> this type of definitions are for space saving.

Comp-3 is a signed packed decimal field.

> Does that mean this field will not occupy 4 character spaces in memory
> and less. If this is correct, how much space they will occupy?

It will occupy 2 bytes in your example.  The right most half nibble contains
the sign, typically 'c' = positive, 'd' = negative, and 'f' = unsigned.

> Scenario
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> '100' in the receiving field?.

The field will contain hexadecimal '100f' in two bytes. The X(03) receiving
field will contain hex '100f20' in three bytes which is pretty worthless.
No conversion occurs because the move was to a display field.  The hex '20'
in the third byte is a space which is the default padding character when a
receiving display field is longer than the sending field.

If the move was to a 9(3) field instead, this field would then contain hex
'f1f0f0' (ascii assumed) in three bytes which would display as '100', which
obviously is useful.

The length of a comp-3 field is the number of digits in the field divided by
2 (remainder is dropped) plus 1.  For example a s9(7)v99 comp-3 field would
be 5 bytes long (9 / 2 = 4 + 1 = 5).  Also, a s9(6)v99 comp-3 field would
be 5 bytes long (8 / 2 = 4 + 1 = 5).  .  If either field contained the
number '-123456.78', the hex string for that number would be '012345678d' in
the 5 bytes.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Novice: All about COMP-3 fields

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T21:42:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401132142.195afac2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401090351.6dd85003@posting.google.com> <Kk9Mb.50785$gN.32869@fed1read05>`

```
"Easton Beymer" <eastonbeymer@hotmail.com> wrote

> If the move was to a 9(3) field instead, this field would then contain hex
> 'f1f0f0' (ascii assumed) in three bytes which would display as '100', which
> obviously is useful.

On an ASCII machine it will be x'313030' which is characters '100'. 
You must be thinking of EBCDIC.
```

## Thread: IGYLI0090-W 3 sequence errors were found in this program. — 4 msgs

### IGYLI0090-W 3 sequence errors were found in this program.

- **From:** leotepl@yahoo.com (Leo)
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T04:47:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60e94e16.0401130447.f513e54@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all.
During compilation of my COBOL program I get a message:

IGYLI0090-W   3 sequence errors were found in this program.

Can anybody explain what does i mean? I use IBM Enterprise COBOL for
z/OS and OS/390 3.2.0. Compilation step ends with the code of 4, so it
is not severe, but I would like to understand what does it mean and
get rid of it.

Thank You in advance.
Leo.
```

#### ↳ Re: IGYLI0090-W 3 sequence errors were found in this program.

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T08:06:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bu0qh5$elj$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<60e94e16.0401130447.f513e54@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <60e94e16.0401130447.f513e54@posting.google.com>,
Leo <leotepl@yahoo.com> wrote:
>Hi all.
>During compilation of my COBOL program I get a message:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Can anybody explain what does i mean?

You are probably using COPY or INCLUDE members which do not have numbers 
which follow 'in sequence' to your source code.

>I use IBM Enterprise COBOL for
>z/OS and OS/390 3.2.0. Compilation step ends with the code of 4, so it
>is not severe, but I would like to understand what does it mean and
>get rid of it.

Try invoking the compiler with the NOSEQ parameter.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IGYLI0090-W 3 sequence errors were found in this program.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T15:57:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0AUMb.7420$1e.2845@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<60e94e16.0401130447.f513e54@posting.google.com> <bu0qh5$elj$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
DD is correct that using the NOSEQ compiler option should solve your problem.
See:

   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.43

However, it is POSSIBLE to get this message whenever the sequence area (column
1-6 of your source code) is not in ascending order.  If you use ISPF (or Roscoe
or almost any other online editing system) *with* its "automatically number for
COBOL" feature turned on, then you (probably) will never see this EXCEPT for
COPY statements (including those created by generators, precompilers, etc).
However, you may want to use whatever your editor has for a feature to "look at"
column 1-6 to see exactly what IS there.

FYI,
  If you compile with the FLAG(I,I) compiler option, you SHOULD see the message
wherever the problem actually occurs in your source code (I think - some
messages only appear at the beginning or end, but I don't think is one of them).
```

#### ↳ Re: IGYLI0090-W 3 sequence errors were found in this program.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T10:55:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g45800pgt6n019a6vf0bjh1fu1c4mbhbu5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<60e94e16.0401130447.f513e54@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Jan 2004 04:47:00 -0800, leotepl@yahoo.com (Leo) enlightened us:

>Hi all.
>During compilation of my COBOL program I get a message:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Leo.

It means two things.  One, you have the sequence checking option
turned on.  Second, the sequence numbers in columns 1-6 are out of
order in 3 places.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Sir John Strange.
Here lies an honest lawyer,
And that is Strange.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

## Thread: Mainframe - Cobol - CICS Program Question — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe - Cobol - CICS Program Question

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T11:04:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com>`

```
I'm in the process of designing a program for a client that must run
in a CICS region and will be accessed by several different
applications.  Its purpose will be to take a data stream sent to it,
reformat that data stream into a common transaction layout and then
send the whole thing off to another computer in another place and
await a response and report back to whomever accessed this program.

I am trying to anticipate all the needs of this program before actual
specifications are delivered so that I can head off any technical
problems in the very beginning of the project.

One such requirement I am anticipating is the manner in which the
program will be accessed.  I currently believe the client will need at
least two ways.  1) through MQ Series; 2) via CALL or CICS LINK.
Since the method of retrieving a data stream and returning same would
be different, the program would need to know how it was accessed.

For the life of me, I cannot think of a mechanism to accomplish this
and I'm not even sure it is possible.  If someone out there in this
most diverse group has knowledge of this one way or the other, could
you please share?

Thanks.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


Real Tombstone Epitaphs:

A lawyer's epitaph in England:
Sir John Strange.
Here lies an honest lawyer,
And that is Strange.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe - Cobol - CICS Program Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T16:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IWUMb.7459$1e.165@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com>`

```
Steve,
  I don't KNOW that I know an answer, but a couple places to start.  (I am
assuming the CALL statement will be CALL identifier - as I don't see how a
"cross-system" static call could work).

1) CALL identifier requires a PPT entry, but I *think* does NOT create a new
run-unit - while EXE CICS LINK does (I think) create a new run-unit.  There may
be some way to test this.

2)  In searching for a way you could test the above, I found a section in the LE
"vendor interface" book that MIGHT provide some guidance.  Check out

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceev1130/13.3.10

and see if anything in there MIGHT help.  (It is a "little complex" for me - at
least at a brief glance)

  ***

You may well get a better answer from the CICS-L list.  If you aren't a member
of this (list-server only) list, send me a private email and I will give you
information on joining.
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe - Cobol - CICS Program Question

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T22:56:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-411E0A.22565613012004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com>`

```
You have a few different things to check.

The first, STARTCODE, might tell you some things, but it will apply to 
the entire transaction.  E.G. SD will tell you started as a background, 
with data -- but will not tell you that MQ did it.

I think your best bet might be to do a RECEIVE and see if you get an 
END-OF-CHAIN condition (CALL) or an MQ Trigger Message structure.

In article <h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com>,
 SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

…[39 more quoted lines elided]…

Top Post

My interpretation of what you want is that you have and input data stream
coming from MQM and from various other sources. Why not store the input
data in a database including fields describing where the input came from,
where it is to be sent and any other info needed. Then you can call or link
to a common program passing it the database key of the data to be
processed. 

I may not completely understand what you want but by analysis and clever
partitioning of the functions you should be able to achieve what you want.
Good luck.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe - Cobol - CICS Program Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T12:34:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9i7g0097ts0rdua0t8c3anh1eb4f5dttfm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com> <01c3db1d$b74cbce0$54c2f943@chottel> <t2bd00pa78ns66s8qag0r5n57fu8b2v77m@4ax.com> <KlWRsJHcrsBAFwRY@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Thu, 15 Jan 2004 17:16:12 +0000, Alistair Maclean
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> enlightened us:

>I may be missing the obvious but why don't you add a field to the 
>COMMAREA to tell the program how it has been called/executed?
>

It is a matter of how the data is retrieved.  If the program is called
or linked to, there is indeed a COMMAREA passed to it.  However, if MQ
invokes the program, the data stream must be RETRIEVED into an area.
That is why the program would need to know how it was invoked.


>>>SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote in article
>>><h75800lt6v6m759indjg422un7ku8c253p@4ax.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
