<a id="t-595e212124"></a>
## Thread: Standards question on edited fields — 49 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T19:23:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Good evening.

Got a question for you standards folks.  We ran into something that 
seems odd in our compiler, and I've submitted it as a potential 
deficiency.  However, if it's behaving the way the standard says, I'll 
withdraw it.  :)  (I've looked, but haven't seen any clarification.)

We have a field that is 9 characters - a 2-digit day, a 3-character 
month, and a 4-digit year.  It's defined as follows...

01  My-Date.
     03  My-Day   Pic 9(02).
     03  My-Month Pic X(03).
     03  My-Year  Pic 9(04).

Then, we have an alphanumeric edited field defined as follows...

01  My-Edited-Date   Pic XXbXXXbXXXX.

One would expect that moving My-Date (which contains, for example, 
"13JAN2004") to My-Edited-Date would result in "13 JAN 2004".  However, 
it's still all crammed together.  Now, if we move My-Date to another 
field, defined as...

01  My-Non-Edited-Date   Pic X(09).

And then move My-Non-Edited-Date to My-Edited-Date, the editing works fine.

My question is this - reading the compiler's manual and the COBOL 2002 
standard, I didn't see where elementary items were required for moves 
that needed editing, or that editing isn't supported for group-level 
sending moves.  Is this a requirement?  Or, have I correctly identified 
this as a discrepancy?

Thanks...  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-13T19:58:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10098i0jkah7n25@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Top post...

As a follow-up, I did the same thing with Fujitsu 3, and the results 
were the same.



LX-i wrote:

> Good evening.
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T03:07:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yn2Nb.8607$zj7.1713@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`

```
There is a (relatively) common misconception that a "group item" (non-National
group in the 2002 Standard) is treated  EXACTLY the same as an alphanumeric
elementary item.  In truth and fact, there is a relatively obscure "difference"
which you have discovered.  If you have a copy of the '85 Standard, you will
need to look at the MOVE general rules (3 & 4 specifically) on page VI-104.

The bottom-line is that EDITING in a move to an edited field *only* applies to
MOVEs where *both* the sending and receiving items are elementary.  In the case
of a group sending item, it is "as if" the receiving item where either redefined
as not edited or as if it where reference modified (1:).

So yes, both of your compilers are working AS REQUIRED by the Standard (Old,
very old, and NEW).

Please do NOT ask me WHY this was ever defined this way, but it certainly would
"break" some programs if the rules were changed now.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T20:51:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<100c02p5on5m23@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<yn2Nb.8607$zj7.1713@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <yn2Nb.8607$zj7.1713@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> There is a (relatively) common misconception that a "group item" (non-National
> group in the 2002 Standard) is treated  EXACTLY the same as an alphanumeric
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> as not edited or as if it where reference modified (1:).

We found that in the compiler manual this morning.  We had been 
concentrating on the "picture clause" portion, not the "move" portion. 
Thanks for the explanations to all.
```

#### ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T23:37:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Daniel,

I was about to explain then I saw Bill's post.

As usual, he has covered it comprehensively.

I was "disappointed" by this behaviour when I first encountered it many
years ago, but I understand why it is so.

A group move will ALWAYS be Alphanumeric UNEDITED. (This makes life a tad
easier for the compiler writer, and is a useful thing for the programmer to
know, as well.)

(There could be times when you want the editing temporarily overwritten...)

If you want an edited move you can't use that mechanism, so it has to be an
elementary source.

Another associated point...

For years now, it has become possible to "de-edit" an edited field, i.e. use
it as the source field in a MOVE. It wasn't always so; there was a time when
once you moved something to a field with an edit mask, that was it. You only
used such fields in edited output.

I have seen people posting here with all kinds of problems from de-editing
fields.

In my book it is "best avoided".

Pete.



"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com...
> Good evening.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> And then move My-Non-Edited-Date to My-Edited-Date, the editing works
fine.
>
> My question is this - reading the compiler's manual and the COBOL 2002
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T10:04:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0401141004.747229f1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com>...
> Another associated point...
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

De-editing MOVE is a requirement of ANSI X3.23-1985; some earlier
COBOL implementations may also include it as an extension.

In our case (Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85) "de-editing" is quite a bit more
costly than the corresponding MOVE in the other direction; that's
because there's direct hardware support for the composition of the
edited field, but not for its decomposition.

In terms of reliability, however, I'd stack its behavior up against
anyone's, and would suggest that anyone who has a problem with it in
which de-editing fails to work as required file a high-priority
trouble report, as the failure is directly indicative of user data
corruption.  I just went through our records, and our customers have
reported exactly two problems with de-editing MOVE (both were with
floating insertion editing), one in 1989 and one last year.  Software
containing the correction was promptly made available for distribution
to (or retrieval by) all users.

What I can't find in the '85 standard is an explicit statement that
the contents of the source field in a de-editing MOVE have to be a
possible result of an edited MOVE to the item in the first place.  If
indeed that's an omission, it's been rectified in the '02 standard
(page 398, paragraph 14.5.12.2 (2), Incompatible data).

I suspect some user problems with de-editing MOVEs result from
attempts to use them with data that wasn't produced (or isn't
producible) by the corresponding edited MOVE in the first place, and
my response to anyone complaining about that is "well, duh ...".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T20:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gdhNb.8828$1e.6503@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com> <c60a0b82.0401141004.747229f1@posting.google.com>`

```
Chuck,
   Although it is not ALWAYS possible where "MOVE de-editing" is possible, do
you have any opinions (for your compiler or in general) on when to use MOVE
de-editing vs NumVal / NumVal-C - where the compiler supports intrinsic
functions?  In the '89 Amendment, you couldn't use this in a MOVE statement -
but you can in the 2002 Standard - and using a "simple" Compute rather than a
move with the '89 Standard isn't that hard.

Note:
  The 2002 Standard would even allow you to "detect" an invalid argument
(telling you exactly where the "bad" data was) in an argument to NumVal /
NumVal-C *or* to detect incompatible data in the MOVE Statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T13:55:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0401161355.72e86f94@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com> <c60a0b82.0401141004.747229f1@posting.google.com> <gdhNb.8828$1e.6503@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
I thought I'd already replied to this on the web, but can't find it in
my archives.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<gdhNb.8828$1e.6503@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>...

> Chuck,
>    Although it is not ALWAYS possible where "MOVE de-editing" is possible, do
> you have any opinions (for your compiler or in general) on when to use MOVE
> de-editing vs NumVal / NumVal-C - where the compiler supports intrinsic
> functions? ... 

Point 1:  NUMVAL-C, and particularly NUMVAL, are more restrictive in
content than the de-editing MOVE.  Anything that's allowed in the
PICTURE is allowed in the source argument of the de-editing MOVE;
that's not true of the functions (NUMVAL-C doesn't cover asterisk
suppression or inserted zeroes, as I see it).

Point 2:  The functions return a *numeric* value.  In our case, for
example for a dollars-and-cents value, they will return a
*floating-point* representation of the value instead of the
integer-pennies internal value that the de-editing MOVE would produce.
 This means that the answer for these functions may be *less exact*
than that produced by the de-editing MOVE.

Point 3:  I'd expect NUMVAL-C to perform about the same as, or a
little worse than, the de-editing MOVE in similar contexts.  I have
not tested this expectation.

Thus:  When the programmer has a reasonable expectation that the
information in the source field does indeed match the PICTURE
character-string, I would strongly recommend the use of de-editing
MOVE, even to the point of trying de-editing MOVEs with a few
different PICTUREs.  Avoiding a floating-point internal representation
of a fixed-point noninteger value is a huge plus in terms of accuracy.
 I'd personally reserve NUMVAL-C (and NUMVAL) for the cases in which
the programmer hasn't a clue what the data might look like *and* an
approximation of the numeric value is OK.

> Note:
>   The 2002 Standard would even allow you to "detect" an invalid argument
> (telling you exactly where the "bad" data was) in an argument to NumVal /
> NumVal-C *or* to detect incompatible data in the MOVE Statement.

Yes, but the '89 amendment doesn't have this.  In our implementation,
if you do a de-editing MOVE with data that doesn't match the PICTURE
you get whatever it is that you get; the logic doesn't account for the
possibility that it might not match.  In contrast, data that violates
the rules for NUMVAL and NUMVAL-C arguments cause a run-time error.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T15:12:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401141512.2f8baa8f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com> <c60a0b82.0401141004.747229f1@posting.google.com>`

```
charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens) wrote 

> What I can't find in the '85 standard is an explicit statement that
> the contents of the source field in a de-editing MOVE have to be a
> possible result of an edited MOVE to the item in the first place. 

I always understood that ANS'85 de-editing moves can only be done with
aligned data.

However MF has always (since CIS Cobol and Level II Cobol) been able
to do unaligned de-editing moves of numeric data.  It required a
compiler option of de-edit"1" but then allowed left justified numeric
items as may be keyed into entry fields to be moved to numeric fields.

eg [2.1      ] in PIC -(8).99 -> PIC S9(8)V99    gave 0000000210

The field accepted would be a PIC X(11) redefined as PIC -(8).99 and
the latter moved to the numeric.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T09:35:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0401190935.55e74fea@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <40051c6d_6@news.athenanews.com> <c60a0b82.0401141004.747229f1@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0401141512.2f8baa8f@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0401141512.2f8baa8f@posting.google.com>...

> I always understood that ANS'85 de-editing moves can only be done with
> aligned data.

"Aligned" is probably not the appropriate way of looking at it, at
least from the standards perspective.  The information that you're
"de-editing" is presumed to have been produced using the PICTURE
clause, in the data space that the PICTURE clause describes, in the
way the PICTURE clause describes it.   The contents of the source
field *must* be a possible result of a move *into* the item that uses
editing.

> However MF has always (since CIS Cobol and Level II Cobol) been able
> to do unaligned de-editing moves of numeric data.  It required a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That would be an extension to '85 and '02 COBOL.  2002-compliant
compilers would be required to raise an exception
(EC-DATA-INCOMPATIBLE) on such a MOVE; the standard explicitly
describes the contents of the destination as undefined, though as an
extension an implementor might choose to define them for such a case. 
Personally I think it's not a very good idea.

> The field accepted would be a PIC X(11) redefined as PIC -(8).99 and
> the latter moved to the numeric.

Not exactly how it works, as I see it.  The information in the PIC
-(8).99 field is "decoded" with the use of the PICTURE
character-string into what is essentially a PICTURE S9(8)V99
temporary, and that temporary MOVEd to the destination (whatever the
destination's USAGE or PICTURE clause).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T10:27:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401141027.2fde6536@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote 

> 01  My-Date.
>      03  My-Day   Pic 9(02).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> And then move My-Non-Edited-Date to My-Edited-Date, the editing works fine.

You only need to use redefines:

    01  My-Date            PIC X(9).
    01  My-Date-Bits REDEFINES My-Date.
        03  My-Day ....
        ...

and then MOVE My-Date TO My-Edited-Date.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T19:56:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F9hNb.8825$1e.3865@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
or even simpler, "reference modification" e.g.

01  My-Date.
      03  My-Day   Pic 9(02).
      03  My-Month Pic X(03).
      03  My-Year  Pic 9(04).
        ...
      move My-Date (1:)  to My-Edited-Date

(Only valid on '85 Standard or later compilers)
```

```

On 14-Jan-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> 01  My-Date.
>       03  My-Day   Pic 9(02).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

That's interesting.   So My-Date is a group level move, but My-Date(1:) is an
elementary alphanumeric move?

Why?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T21:32:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CFDNb.11813$zj7.9300@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0401141027.2fde6536@posting.google.com> <F9hNb.8825$1e.3865@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bu6ed8$150$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Reference modification (always?) creates a temporary ELEMENTARY field.

As to "why" this is true, but referencing a "group field" is not always treated
as an elementary alphanumeric field, I think I will need to quote "Fiddler on
the Roof" and its answer to similar (IMHO) questions, ...

   "Tradition!!!"

P.S.  Things do NOT get any better (still IMHO) with the 2002 Standard's
introduction of the "Group-Usage" clause ... and if you don't know about it
already, don't ask <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T13:02:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40072a93_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0401141027.2fde6536@posting.google.com> <F9hNb.8825$1e.3865@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bu6ed8$150$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bu6ed8$150$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 14-Jan-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's interesting.   So My-Date is a group level move, but My-Date(1:) is
an
> elementary alphanumeric move?
>
> Why?

The Reference modified field has no structure, other than an elemental one.
If you take a "slice" out of a string (which is, in effect, what refmodding
does) then there can be no substring or other structure attached to that
"slice". It is, by it's very nature, an explicit element.

That's why.

Bill says it is because the refmodding creates an intermediate elemental
field; this would be one of the extremely rare occasions when I believe he
may be incorrect. (And even if he's right, it doesn''t really answer your
question.).

HOW the refmodding is implemented could vary wildly across platforms but I
see no need for an intermediate field NECESSARILY. (Machines with indexed
move instructions could simply set an index pair and execute directly on the
source field; but it is really irrelevant...)

Although we consider group fields in the same way, that is IMPLICIT, not
directly so.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T21:02:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<100c0n26n36lv4c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0401141027.2fde6536@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0401141027.2fde6536@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

> LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote 
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> and then MOVE My-Date TO My-Edited-Date.

Well, I figured as much.  This came up in our date/time proc, where 
everything is definied (for the most part) as numeric.  i.e.,

01  Date-S8                Pic 9(08).
01  Date-S8-X         Redefines Date-S8.
     12  Date-S-CC          Pic 9(02).
     12  Date-S6            Pic 9(06).
     12  Date-S6-X     Redefines Date-S6.
         16  Date-S-YY      Pic 9(02).
         16  Date-S-MM      Pic 9(02).
         16  Date-S-DD      Pic 9(02).
01  Date-S8-X2        Redefines Date-S8.
     12  Date-S-CCYY        Pic 9(04).
     12                     Pic 9(04).

...which means that you've got basically any part of the date (century, 
4 digit year, 2 digit year, date with century, date without century, 
month, and day) all defined as numeric - and, if you have something like

01  Fancy-Date    Pic 9999-99-99.

...and move Date-S8 to Fancy-Date, it'll work.  However, for military 
dates, they didn't define a top level, since they're alphanumeric (being 
group items) anyway.  Then, another programmer was trying to move it to 
the edited field, which prompted the research and question.

Thanks for the advice.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T10:38:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90-oUvKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <100c0n26n36lv4c@corp.supernews.com> <40079833.6010803@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
     Am  16.01.04
schrieb  Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com (Rosa Fischer)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  40079833.6010803@fujitsu-siemens.com
  ueber  Re: Standards question on edited fields

RF> My compiler thinks that "Pic 9999-99-99" in "Fancy-Date" is an
RF> invalid picture character string. Is this a new format?

  And I think your compiler is right.

  The standards document I have available here -- it is only the "Draft  
Proposed Revised X3.23 American National Standard Programming Language  
COBOL" of 1981 -- explains that the symbols "+", "-", "CR", and "DB" in  
a Picture string "are used as sign control symbols. When used, they  
represent the character position into which the editing sign control  
symbole will be placed. The symbols are mutually exclusive in any one  
character-string" [Nucleus, 5.9.1 (8)].

  Understanding the standard correctly, a "-" or "+" should only appear  
once, or as a floating zero suppression symbol, and then only several of  
them in a row, not interspersed among other characters.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Ohne Pressefreiheit, Vereins- und Versammlungsrecht ist keine
Arbeiterbewegung mï¿½glich"        - Friedrich Engels      (Februar 1865)
```

>
> We have a field that is 9 characters - a 2-digit day, a 3-character
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>      03  My-Year  Pic 9(04).

Why would you store a date in such a way? Dates in that format would
not sort correctly, and it seems you would not present such a format to
a human directly without editing, as your question demonstrates. I can't
see a reason for storing a date in such a format, because it would need
to be modified for either internal computer use or external human use,
making it useless in its current form. Is there a valid reason for this, or
is it just sloppy design? :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T11:50:38-08:00

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:<0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com>...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> is it just sloppy design? :-)

Hello

I think 02JAN2004 or 31OCT2003 are both compact and very readable ways
of expressing dates in display formats.  There is no confusion as to
whether the months are days or vice versa as in all numeric forms, and
the year is a four digit version, which is semi-permanently proof
against confusion.  I have seen them used very effectively and
wouldn't hesitate to recommend such a format for display on paper or
screen for English speaking users.

Regards,

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com...
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> making it useless in its current form. Is there a valid reason for this,
or
> is it just sloppy design? :-)

Whoa, Judson....read the above again and try and see why I would respond to
it.

Are we to take it that anything YOU can't see a purpose for, must be
justified to your satisfaction or else it is sloppy design <G>?

Man, you really have a manner that presses my buttons...<G>

I don't think you intend to come across as arrogant but the above certainly
does. And putting a smile on it doesn't make it less so. (Like I did in  the
line above...catch my drift....?)

I use dates in this format (as well as in many other formats).

One advantage of it is that it is immediately apparent to a user what month
is being referred to.

(I knew a girl once who used to count on her teeth with her tongue, if you
gave her a month number; seriously, I couldn't make it up <G>. Turns out,
many people have a problem with month numbers once they get past April or
so...) )

It also lends itself to different language cultures where the month can
appear in the abbreviated form that is expected in that culture.

Either way, and for whatever reason, you are not the "System Police" and
people can do stuff with or without your approval, and it doesn't mean they
are sloppy designers.

By all means have an opinion about it, by all means express that opinion,
but expect flak when you suggest the ONLY way is your way...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

```
Wow, Pete!  I simply asked a question and explained why I was asking
it, for goodness sake! I did not state that it was bad design, I asked if it
was bad design. I simply said that I didn't see it's use, explaining why,
and explicitly allowed that there might be a good reason. Why is it that
you get so wound up over my posts? Seems that you are the one acting
as a 'Post Police' here. Please try to actually READ what I write before
you get so wound up, okay? :-)


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> but expect flak when you suggest the ONLY way is your way...

How in the world could you actually read my post and come to the
conclusion you express here?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T00:21:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4007c9d3_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <40072dca_1@news.athenanews.com> <c75476be4fb13cde34193e208e288a34@news.teranews.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:c75476be4fb13cde34193e208e288a34@news.teranews.com...
> Wow, Pete!  I simply asked a question and explained why I was asking
> it, for goodness sake! I did not state that it was bad design, I asked if
it
> was bad design. I simply said that I didn't see it's use, explaining why,
> and explicitly allowed that there might be a good reason. Why is it that
> you get so wound up over my posts? Seems that you are the one acting
> as a 'Post Police' here. Please try to actually READ what I write before
> you get so wound up, okay? :-)

I read what you wrote, considered it, then posted. I was not "wound up" and
I make no claim to police anything here, but the general tone of your posts
often bugs me. (It is a pity, because sometimes the content is really
worthwhile, but it gets diminished by the container in which it is
delivered.)

If you were having a conversation with me and demanded an explanation as to
why I did something, suggesting it might be just sloppy design, you'd be
unlikely to get any response (apart from possibly a smack in the mouth <G>).

On the other hand, if you were puzzled as to why a certain approach was
taken and expressed genuine interest in understanding it, (WITHOUT the
judgemental qualifer that it might be "sloppy design" or anything else...),
I'd take a lot of time to explain it to you. In the course of that
explanation we might both learn something.

You don't seem to grasp that there are other possible approaches that are
perfectly valid, but may not gel with your Universe.

We've covered all this in civilised discussion and debate and I accept that
you live in a different World from the one I inhabit.

If you'll try not to be so arrogant, I'll try not to be so cutting...:-)

Pete.

<snipped previous posts>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T16:56:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ecf8d0c6481f5e2331c38a505718ad1@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <40072dca_1@news.athenanews.com> <c75476be4fb13cde34193e208e288a34@news.teranews.com> <4007c9d3_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> delivered.)

Pete, you are the only person who has ever taken that attitude about my
posts. Yes, occasionally I do post direct challenges, or posts that might
engender some annoyance from people who disagree. But this is not one
of them.

I did not say "this is bad," I said "I don't see why this is good, is there
a reason for it." I also explained my position. At every point I *explicitly*
made allowance for there being a good reason. There is simply no way
you can justify such a post as being inflammatory. From the way you are
constantly denigrating me (e.g. comments like "you don't understand", or
"you don't seem to grasp"), I see no other explanation than that you either
don't like me, or that you are offended at me personally (say by my SIG
perhaps?). Even other people in this group have commented to you about
your denigrating comments to me, but you, as usual, sloughed it off as their misunderstanding, not yours. In the post in question I
made an *explicit*
request, and made allowance, for a reason why I might be wrong. Have
you been doing that? Did you make such provision in this post? No, you
make your statements categorically and absolutely with no possibility of
a mistake. Pete, you are doing exactly what you accuse me of doing. I am
merely stating my opinion, but you are constantly and explicitly stating
that I, or others, simply do not understand. You seem to refuse to accept the
possibility that it might be you is misunderstanding. I believe any unbiased
person would have to conclude that from reading these exchanges. If you
don't like me, or are offended by me, please simply say so and I'll stop
responding to your posts. If this is not the case, then please stop telling me
that I do not, or cannot, understand. I do understand your posts, and your
stated positions. I just don't always agree with them, as others have already
tried to tell you.

> If you were having a conversation with me and demanded an explanation as to
> why I did something, suggesting it might be just sloppy design, you'd be
> unlikely to get any response (apart from possibly a smack in the mouth <G>).

If we were speaking directly, I would have simply asked why you did it.
But in this medium, where immediate interaction is impossible, posts must
be more detailed to be clear. You certainly understand that in your own
posts, why not in mine? Who's showing the hostility here?

> On the other hand, if you were puzzled as to why a certain approach was
> taken and expressed genuine interest in understanding it, (WITHOUT the
> judgemental qualifer that it might be "sloppy design" or anything else...),

Pete, it is clearly you who do not understand the difference between
saying "this is sloppy design" and asking "is this sloppy design, or is
there a reason?" Believe me, there is a great difference. The first does
not allow for an explanation, the second not only allows for it, but
explicitly asks if there is one, and what it might be.

> I'd take a lot of time to explain it to you. In the course of that
> explanation we might both learn something.
>
> You don't seem to grasp that there are other possible approaches that are
> perfectly valid, but may not gel with your Universe.

See, once again you denigrate me. Obviously, there is no possibility
that you might be the one who "doesn't seem to grasp." Who's being
arrogant here?

> If you'll try not to be so arrogant, I'll try not to be so cutting...:-)

Pete, it is you who are being both arrogant and cutting. I have been
neither. Even my negative comments have been made in an attempt
to reach an understanding, not to denigrate.

Anybody else care to comment on this? I believe my response here
has been balanced and just. But if I am the one who is being arrogant,
please let me know, and show me where I am being so. I will try to
make amends to Pete and any others I have offended, and also try to
correct my attitude.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <40072dca_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Top post

Thanks for the amusing responses. Obviously using teeth as a calculator
struck a chord with some of you <G>.

The same person who worked out the month in this way could also do
arithmetic by positioning her tongue at various points in her mouth.

I have never heard of or met anyone else who does this, although she
reckoned she picked up this behaviour when she was at school (in the North
West of England), and several of the kids in her class did it.

It makes me wonder what happens when she gets older and loses some or all of
her teeth... I have visions of an old lady in a supermarket taking out her
dentures in order to check the till roll...<G>

The variations on the old "30 days hath September..." rhyme were new to me
and I particularly liked the Bugs Bunny one.

Pete.

news:40072dca_1@news.athenanews.com...

to
> it.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think you intend to come across as arrogant but the above
certainly
> does. And putting a smile on it doesn't make it less so. (Like I did in
the
> line above...catch my drift....?)
>
> I use dates in this format (as well as in many other formats).
>
> One advantage of it is that it is immediately apparent to a user what
month
> is being referred to.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> people can do stuff with or without your approval, and it doesn't mean
they
> are sloppy designers.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T15:40:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bu90m1$a5i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com>`

```

On 15-Jan-2004, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> > 01  My-Date.
> >      03  My-Day   Pic 9(02).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why would you store a date in such a way?

It is a useful way to display.   Almost.   If the move to edited field had
worked, it would have created an unambiguous date in the format that I had to
use when I was a USAF pilot.

So I suppose the trade off is between not having to do much conversion to
display the date and between being in a form that is useful for date
comparisons.   The choice would depend upon business needs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T17:11:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<712bce791fc31b9ee21387838dc8ccbf@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <bu90m1$a5i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> comparisons.   The choice would depend upon business needs.

I would rather design for the possibility that you may need to sort or
make relative comparisons on the date at some point, and store it
internally as numeric YYYYMMDD, and convert on output, and input
if necessary. This is more storage efficient and more useful internally.
After some 70+ systems, I have found that it is far better to over design
a system than the reverse. Better to go to extra trouble than have a
design that is insufficiently flexible to support future needs. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T20:05:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<100h635amf82v15@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why would you store a date in such a way?

Who said I was storing it?

> Dates in that format would
> not sort correctly, and it seems you would not present such a format to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is it just sloppy design? :-)

I can't think of a military-format date in our database.  However, being 
in the military, we have many requirements to display dates this way 
(today would be "16 Jan 04" most places, "16 Jan 2004" in a few).  We 
either display the current date, or convert a data out of the database, 
which is usually stored in YYYY-MM-DD (relational) or YYYYDDD (DMS).

So, it's not a sloppy design at all - and there is a valid reason.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T17:18:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc5339c15ba15bdbd5576d8c17accd7a@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <100h635amf82v15@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, it's not a sloppy design at all - and there is a valid reason.

I believe you, and having spent four years programming at command
level for the Air Force myself, I understand the requirement for such
display and print formats. I'm just curious what your specific reason
is for having a date that is (currently) stored inside the computer in
that form? I didn't say 'permanent storage'. :-)

Perhaps it's changed a lot in the three decades since I was in the USAF,
but I saw lots of crummy design. Lots of good design too, of course. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-18T18:49:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<100madoh0hn8scf@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<fc5339c15ba15bdbd5576d8c17accd7a@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <100h635amf82v15@corp.supernews.com> <fc5339c15ba15bdbd5576d8c17accd7a@news.teranews.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> that form? I didn't say 'permanent storage'. :-)

We have a date/time proc that handles all our date and time processes. 
There's a paragraph that's executed in the init logic of all of our 
programs called P315-Get-All-Dates-Times.  This fills working storage 
variables with the current date and time.  We support 4 different 
formats (Julian, standard, military, and relational) and 3 different 
references (local base, host base, and Zulu).  So, in every run, you 
know that

W315-Current-Date-ZJ7  (CCYYDDD)
W315-Current-Date-ZS8  (CCYYMMDD)
W315-Current-Date-ZM9  (DDMMMCCYY)
W315-Current-Date-ZDT  (CCYY-MM-DD)

all have the Zulu-referenced date in the various formats.  This allows 
any program to display and compare the current date in any format they 
may have already, without doing a lot of conversion first.  (Expansion 
routines are provided for 2-digit years, which we still have on several 
of our screens, but not in the database.)  Of course, these aren't the 
only 4, but the others follow this basic design.

> Perhaps it's changed a lot in the three decades since I was in the USAF,
> but I saw lots of crummy design. Lots of good design too, of course. :-)

This design is brilliant (and I can say that without being cocky because 
I'm not the one that came up with it, although I'm now the custodian of 
it).  There are many date/time routines (addition, subtraction, range 
checking, offsets, etc.) in this proc, and a recent performance 
evaluation, which I thought would flag a lot of problems in it, actually 
showed that it's some of the most efficient code we've got, in terms of 
processor cycles and time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
<Joke>  Too bad you work on a compiler/system that requires yout to "do this
yourself".

For IBM environments (MVS, OS/390, z/OS, z/VM, OS/400, *and* emulator such as
Micro Focus), there are "supplied" niffty-keen routines to do date reformating
(sensitive to locales - even Japanese and Chinese Eras). These *do* support the
"MMM" formats - as well as "easily" converting from "absolute" (Lilian) dates to
other formats - and back.

See (for example):
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/3.5.22

with valid "picture strings" discused at:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/B.0
```

- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <100h635amf82v15@corp.supernews.com> <fc5339c15ba15bdbd5576d8c17accd7a@news.teranews.com> <100madoh0hn8scf@corp.supernews.com>`

I've long lamented the fact that you could not use '-' as an insertion character.
It seems to me that if used surrounded by other characters (PIC 999-99-9999)
there would be no ambiguity with its use as a sign, which would always be to

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards question on edited fields

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-20T09:32:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bujonb$1861$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10096gir1pnkeec@corp.supernews.com> <0860916642202a2764e96769f902fb9e@news.teranews.com> <100h635amf82v15@corp.supernews.com> <fc5339c15ba15bdbd5576d8c17accd7a@news.teranews.com> <100madoh0hn8scf@corp.supernews.com> <38a0e5e82a98b755301714633bd279e8@news.teranews.com>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:38a0e5e82a98b755301714633bd279e8@news.teranews.com...

Unisys MCP/AS COBOL dialects have had this extension for some time.
However, the way it was originally implemented (back in the early days of
COBOL(68) on the Burroughs Large System) it wasn't just the hyphen -- it was
any character not in the strict syntax for ANSI compliance that was treated
as a "simple insertion character".

The consequence of that particular convention was essentially that it became
impossible for the compiler to distinguish a syntactically-correct PICTURE
character-string from one that was not, unless the PICTURE exceeded some
sort of limits (overall length of the PICTURE string and length of the
described item, in particular).   I speak from lengthy and bitter
experience:  travel any distance at all down that path, and yonder be
dragons.

Unisys now actively discourages the use of this convention.

The Burroughs Medium System (V Series) had a different convention:  any
character except the blank could be used as a simple insertion character by
the simple method of preceding it with "I".  Recently this latter convention
was introduced to MCP/AS COBOL74 and COBOL85, and has become the default in
current software.  These compilers are now capable of recognizing that , for
example, "PICTURE X(7." is more likely to be a typographical error and thus
a syntax error than it is to declare a three-character edited-alphanumeric
data item with a single variable characters trailed by the insertion
characters "(" and "7".    If you really wanted PICTURE X(7 then the
unambiguous way to code that is PICTURE XI(I7 according to this newer
convention, and by default PICTURE X(7 will now produce a syntax error.

Over the decades, there have been similar proposals to INCITS/J4 to allow
arbitrary insertion characters in PICTURE character strings; as I recall the
most recent proposal is to enclose the character in quotes.  But at least in
part  because of the fragility of the PICTURE rules as demonstrated over the
history of COBOL, andin part because of some conflicts between the proposal
and currency-sign conventions, the standards committee has been reluctant to
incorporate this capability.  I believe it remains on the "candidates" list,
but not for the upcoming standard.

Had the Burroughs Large System COBOL(68) compiler limited the rule to
hyphens (and perhaps colons!), the approach might *perhaps* have been
viable, but alas, that wasn't the case.

> I've long lamented the fact that you could not use '-' as an insertion
character.
> It seems to me that if used surrounded by other characters (PIC
999-99-9999)
> there would be no ambiguity with its use as a sign, which would always be
to
> the left or right of any data characters.

The current preferred approach in the MCP/AS COBOLs would result in PICTURE
999I-99I-9999; the most recent standards proposal, as I recall it, PICTURE
999"-"99"-"9999.  I have to admit, having implemented (and re-implemented!)
PICTURE parsers, preferring some sort of EXplicit indication of such
insertion characters (and allowing a whole range of such characters) to the
suggestion relating to hyphens alone, or its extension to anything that's
out-of-context according to the rules.  .

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-5b1f3f7d6d"></a>
## Thread: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

- **From:** Daniel Klopfer <dk@grunecker.de>
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T12:11:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com>`

```
Hi all,

I am trying to access a SQL-Server database from within an Cobol
program. 
The first attempts were succesful, but now I've got a problem with
some record-dscriptions: The variable-types "INT" , "INT NOT NULL"
and "VARCHAR(nn)" can be  represented by PIC 9(9), PIC S9(9) and PIC X
clauses.

What PIC must I use for variable-type "DATETIME" and "DECIMAL(X,Y)???

Any hint is welcome.......

Thanks
Daniel
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T02:59:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40054bea_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com>`

```

"Daniel Klopfer" <dk@grunecker.de> wrote in message
news:r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com...
> Hi all,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Dates are problematic. If you really must store them as DATETIME (I store
them as CHAR (YYYYMMDD) and do all the arithmetic and conversion in my
Programs, using a standard component I wrote) then try  "USAGE COMP-2" (NO
Picture clause). Dates are actually stored on the database as 64 bit
floating point numbers. You can also get away with PIC 9(18) the maximum
numeric size in COBOL that hasn't implemented the new standard. Use your
debugger to see what is actually returned in these fields.

DECIMAL (X,Y) is PIC 9(X)v(Y). so, 123.45678 is PIC 9(3)v9(5).

HTH,

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T11:25:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401141125.465d2e7b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com> <40054bea_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> DECIMAL (X,Y) is PIC 9(X)v(Y). so, 123.45678 is PIC 9(3)v9(5).

DECIMAL (X,Y) is PIC S9(Z)v9(Y) where Z is X - Y.  The X is the total
number of digits, Y of which are the decimal part.

     DECIMAL (10,2) is PIC S9(8)V99.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```

Das war fuer mich ein vergnugung, und gleichfalls mit der Grussen, von Neu
Seeland <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-20T01:33:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<builjl0jvb@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com> <40054bea_1@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0401141125.465d2e7b@posting.google.com> <4005adb4$1_2@news.athenanews.com> <mqfc0096u95hsdp8keqo4k412qn9advuke@4ax.com>`

```
"Daniel Klopfer" <dk@grunecker.de> wrote:
> Thanks for the information. For myself I wouldnt use DATETIME, but I
> have to access an existing database in which the dates are defined in
> this way.

Just a bit of info about DATETIME in MS parlance -- that's an 8-byte IEEE
floating point.  It is a simple offset from a fixed zero date (December 30,
1899 if I remember correctly.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing MS-SQL Server from within ACU-Cobol

- **From:** Jon Scally <jscally@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T07:34:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2004.01.16.07.34.57.266000@sbcglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<r98a00hd6jkol8i681i9gnui8iu3el3o3j@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 Jan 2004 12:11:35 +0100, Daniel Klopfer wrote:

> Hi all,
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Daniel

For a DATE field you would want to use a PIC 9(8), but you will also need
to use the DATE directive for Acu4GL for MS SQL to identify to the
database that it knows the field is a DATE field:

01 MY-REC.
$XFD DATE=YYYYMMDD
   05 DATE-FLD		PIC 9(8).

Hope that helps.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu Linker — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Linker

- **From:** davewhitehead@bigfoot.com (DaveW)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T03:26:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4e06385e.0401140326.31524fd4@posting.google.com>`

```
Just loaded the Fujitsu CobolV3 and tried to Link Sample1 for
starters. Received the following message.

NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'
Stop.

Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

/OUT:C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\samples\SAMPLE1\Sample1.exe
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\samples\SAMPLE1\Sample1.OBJ
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\F3BICIMP.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LIBC.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB
LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
Linking files ended.
Please close the window.

Any suggestion gratefully accepted
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Linker

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-14T10:24:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401141024.4c70db9b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4e06385e.0401140326.31524fd4@posting.google.com>`

```
davewhitehead@bigfoot.com (DaveW) wrote

> LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined

You need to specify that the program you are compiling is 'MAIN'. 
This is done with a compiler option.  Either put MAIN in the compiler
options dialog box or add a line to the top of your source code that
is '     @OPTIONS MAIN'.

One program in a link of several programs that are to be linked
together must be marked as MAIN and this is the entry point. Programs
that are CALLed are not marked as MAIN when compiled.
```

<a id="t-7f90c2a075"></a>
## Thread: Size of ISAM file — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Size of ISAM file

- **From:** mkbobba@yahoo.com (Bob)
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T07:07:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64713ce9.0401150707.191111eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

If I convert a line sequential file to an ISAM file, will the size of
the file will increase or decrease or remains same?.

If increases/decreases , by how much percentage?.

Thanks.

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: Size of ISAM file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T09:15:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yJidnXb_rPIZM5vdRVn-hw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401150707.191111eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Bob wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

Depends on the file system, data, and phase of the moon. In some cases the
file size increase by 100% or more(1), in other cases the file size may
shrink by 50% or more(2).

For example:
(1) File consists of data that are all keys and alternate keys (NAME, ZIP,
PHONE)
(2) File is mostly repeating characters (like blanks) and the file system
does run-length-encoding(RLE) to compact the data.
```

#### ↳ Re: Size of ISAM file

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T12:49:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4007278a_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401150707.191111eb@posting.google.com>`

```

news:64713ce9.0401150707.191111eb@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob

It will increase by 17.13425%.

Or does that depend on the keys structure?

Hang on a minute...I did this once many years ago...that's right, it
decreased by 7.43781%, due to buffer overrun in the index areas.

But we sold that version to MicroSoft...

If I take a piece of red string and tie it to a piece of blue string, what
is the chance of getting a piece of purple string that is the same length as
either of the pieces I started with?

How long will the resulting piece of string be?

<sigh>...Guess I'll just have to try it and see...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Size of ISAM file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T12:10:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FwQNb.1068$BA2.892@newssvr26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<64713ce9.0401150707.191111eb@posting.google.com>`

```
> If I convert a line sequential file to an ISAM file, will the size of
> the file will increase or decrease or remains same?.
>
> If increases/decreases , by how much percentage?.

If you are working on IBM mainframe creating VSAM 'isam' files, you can get
this free software to assist in planning your space requirements:

http://www.talsystems.com/tsihome_html/techcorner.html


VSAMSPACE.EXE
VSAMSPACE.ZIP
Windows/32 software to calculate space requirements (cylinders/tracks) for
VSAM files based on media, record size, CI Size/freespace percent and more.
Enables "tuning" of parameters to optimize DASD usage. Freeware.
```

### OT - Bugs in software

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2004-01-15T08:02:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0401150802.4c7e0e5e@posting.google.com>`

```
Found this on the Google.Groups.support.

Dear Tech Support,

Last year I upgraded from Boyfriend 5.0 to Husband 1.0 and noticed a
distinct slow down in overall system performance - particularly in the
flower and jewelry applications, which operated flawlessly under
Boyfriend 5.0.

In addition, Husband 1.0 uninstalled many other valuable programs,
such as Romance 9.5 and Personal Attention 6.5, and then installed
undesirable programs such as NFL 5.0, NBA 3.0. and Golf Clubs 4.1.
Conversation 8.0 no longer runs, and Housecleaning 2.6 simply crashes
the system. I've tried running Nagging 5.3 to fix these problems, but
to no avail.

What can I do?
Signed,
Desperate

=========================

Dear Desperate:

First keep in mind, Boyfriend 5.0 is an Entertainment Package, while
Husband 1.0 is an Operating System!

Please enter the command: "http: I Thought You Loved Me.htm" and try
to download Tears 6.2 and don't forget to install the Guilt 3.0
update.

If that application works as designed, Husband 1.0 should then
automatically run the applications Jewelry 2.0 and Flowers 3.5. But
remember, over use of the above application can cause Husband 1.0 to
default to Grumpy Silence 2.5, Happy Hour 7.0, or Beer 6.1. Beer 6.1
is a very bad program that will download the Snoring Loudly Beta.

Whatever you do, DO NOT install Mother-in-law 1.0 (it runs a virus in
the background, that will eventually seize control of all your system
resources). Also, do not attempt to reinstall the Boyfriend 5.0
program. These are unsupported applications and will crash Husband
1.0.

In summary, Husband 1.0 is a great program, but it does have limited
memory and cannot learn new applications quickly. You  might consider
buying additional software to improve memory and performance. We
recommend Hot Food 3.0 and Lingerie 7.7.

Good Luck,
Tech Support
```

Tony Morris wrote:

> Can someone suggest a COBOL compiler (and/or resource) for a newcomer to the
> language wishing to gain a fundamental understanding through practice and
> reading please ?

Fujitsu has a free compiler you can download - it's version 3, which is 
pretty old, but the price is right.  http://www.adtools.com, click on 
"Students", then click on the top link on the left on that page, fill 
out a form, and you got it.

As far as reading, the manuals are also available for download - but, I 
haven't found any really good COBOL tutorial online.  (Haven't looked 
very hard, though...)  http://www.cobolportal.com has a lot of links - 
that might be a good place to check out.

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T16:11:45+00:00

- **References:** `<bu791a$k94$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <100h6p9squ9ab30@corp.supernews.com> <40089dcf$0$4046$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au>`

"Tony Morris" <dibblego@optusnet.com.au> wrote:

>Righto cheers.
>I don't know how legitimate it will be if it is intended for students only,
>but I'm certainly not going to be using it for anything in production.
>I'll give it a burl - thanks for the tips.

Tony:

The best place to get an inexpensive compiler or to learn how to write
COBOL code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

The book only costs about $20.00 US$ and includes the compiler at no
additional charge.

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.chapters.ca
http://www.indigo.ca
http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com
http://www.amazon.com





Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

cobol programm.  i dont know what compiler im using and i kind of

to get the YYYY for the year to print out or how to find out what
compiler im on would be great help.
brent
```

#### ↳ Re: how do i get a 4 digit year to appear in cobol

- **Date:** 2004-01-17T10:59:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40085f67_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com>`

```

"robert" <rbnorthcutt74@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com...
> i just want to know how i can get the a 4 digit year to print out on a
> cobol programm.  i dont know what compiler im using and i kind of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> brent

dude...the whole thing with dates is just too much...like, who needs it?  it
doesn't matter dude. ur using the cheech and chong compiler and the best way

#### ↳ Re: how do i get a 4 digit year to appear in cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T22:09:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<piZNb.13716$zj7.8114@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com>`

```
If your compiler (that you don't know what it is) supports intrinsic functions,
use the one for getting the full date.

If your compiler supports the 2002 Standard (or as an extension to the '85
Standard) optional keywords on the ACCEPT statement, use that.

If your compiler doesn't support any of the above, but does support access to
the system date (via a systems call), use that.

If your compiler doesn't support any of the above, then you will probably have
to use logic to GUESS what century it is (possibly depending upon what year it
is).

  ***

Note:
  If this is for a COBOL class, I suggest attending your classes, reading the
books, and going to your labs. Any of these may help.
```

```
> i just want to know how i can get the a 4 digit year to print out on a
> cobol programm.  i dont know what compiler im using and i kind of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> brent

When you compile your program most compilers print a date on the listing.
Usually it is a 4 digit year that the compiler will "print out on a
cobol programm". Also, usually, the compiler will print its name which
will tell you what it is. So look at one of your compile listings you
should have your answers.

For example, I compiled a program today and the listing said:
IBM OS/VS COBOL   January 16, 1904.

The program was written in 1968 but, hey it works! Wanna guess
how many JAVA programs written in 2004 will still be running in 36 years?
```

##### ↳ ↳ For shame! Using OS/VS COBOL 90 years before it went off support!

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@_AT_adelphia.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T04:30:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4008BA5C.9080707@_AT_adelphia.net>`
- **References:** `<31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com> <XvydnXs3vLrWD5XdRTvaKA@giganews.com>`

```
Seriously, why are you using OS/VS COBOL?

IBM is beginning to announce that OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II programs 
are not supported by DB2, and actually cannot be used in CICS.

Isn't the fact that this compiler went off support in 1994 enough to get 
your shop to use a supported compiler?

I can tell you that you are NOT alone, though.  I work for an 
outsourcing firm, and we support the West Coast applications of part of 
a large customer.  Applications on the East Coast are supported by the 
company's own programmers.  They never so much as licensed VS COBOL II. 
  And while they have a "new COBOL" compiler installed, they have ZERO 
production programs which were compiled with the new compiler.  If that 
weren't enough, they compile everything NORES, so that the COBOL library 
routines are linked into their load modules.  And they use the OS/VS 
COBOL library (not Language Environment) when they link!
```

```
It's been a while since I asked, ...

Is anyone in this group still using (or know anyone still using) IBM's "ANS
COBOL V4" to compile production code?
```

> In article <4008BA5C.9080707@_AT_adelphia.net>,
> Colin Campbell  <cmcampb@_AT_adelphia.net> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
No, I meant NORES(ident).  Programs compiled NORES have all of the COBOL 
library routines in the load module.  They are effectively "self 
supporting", and do not have to worry about Language Environment vs. the 
older run times - until they stop working, that is.

I doubt whether they have yet divined that there is such a thing as a 
static vs. dynamic CALL.  After all, who needs to CALL a subroutine, 
when you can just put all that code into your own program "for clarity"!?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: For shame! Using OS/VS COBOL 90 years before it went off support!

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@NoMoSpam.ORG>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T16:59:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NsucnY9J0-PaI5TdRTvaKA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com> <XvydnXs3vLrWD5XdRTvaKA@giganews.com> <4008BA5C.9080707@_AT_adelphia.net>`

```
> For Shame! Using OS/VS COBOL 90 years before it went off support!

LOL! Good One.

> Seriously, why are you using OS/VS COBOL?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> your shop to use a supported compiler?

Well, while they have VS COBOL II and even OS/390 COBOL a considerable
amount of code is still compiled with OS/VS Cobol. As a consultant at
this shop my job was to get year-end production working.
Not to rewrite a bunch of "WRITE BEFORE POSITIONING", "EXAMINE",
"EXHIBIT NAMED", "MOVE CURRENT-DATE" etc. statements, and then go
through all the debugging necessary. There's a "culture" at this
shop that discourages upgrading programs even though newer compilers
are available. At least they compile "RES,DYNAM". This same "culture"
extends to programming style. Even code written just last year
follows "old style" convention; "GO TO" everywhere. "We don't
like to change things". I could go on but you get the idea.
So to answer your question, I use the OS/390 & VM compiler when
I can but I'm stuck a lot the time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how do i get a 4 digit year to appear in cobol

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-17T15:16:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cPSdnXwNLriuO5TdRVn-hA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<31822b77.0401161317.3c10514f@posting.google.com> <31822b77.0401171117.385857d7@posting.google.com>`

```
robert wrote:
>
> this deal, it is for a class and i do attend everyday, you got to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> brent

Let's break this down, shall we? Are you having trouble GETTING today's date
or are you having trouble PRINTING today's date?

If your difficulty is GETTING today's date, there are several solutions, not
all of which may work on your compiler:

I. ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE FROM DATE YYYYMMDD
   where TODAYS-DATE is defined as PIC X(8).

II. MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO TODAYS-DATE
   where TODAYS-DATE is defined as PIC X(21) the first 8 bytes are today's
date in yyyymmdd format
   the remaining are time of day. Actually, if you're not interest in the
time, you can define as PIC X(8).

III. Define:
   01  TODAYS-DATE.
     02  TODAY-DATE-CC  PIC X(2).
     02  TODAYS-DATE-YYMMDD PIC X(6).
then
  ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE-YYMMDD FROM DATE
  IF TODAYS-DATE-YYMMDD < '40'
      MOVE '20' TO TODAYS-DATE-CC
  ELSE
      MOVE '19' TO TODAYS-DATE-CC.

IV (For extra credit)
   DISPLAY 'What is todays date in YYYYMMDD format?' UPON CONSOLE
   ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE FROM CONSOLE.
   where TODAYS-DATE is defined as X(6).

If you're having trouble DISPLAYING today's date, that exercise is left to
the reader.
```

## Thread: Umwandel Hex 'C1' to BLANK mit cobol. — 3 msgs

### Umwandel Hex 'C1' to BLANK mit cobol.

- **From:** cyber100@gmx.de (john)
- **Date:** 2004-01-16T22:23:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f69d9e5.0401162223.79d81ac6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hallo

ich habe da ein kleines Problem mit Cobol. Ich habe Daten die an der
ersten Stelle ein nicht abdruckbares Steuerzeichen enthalten. Hex ist
es C1.

Diese Zeichen will ich durch ein Blank ersetzen und vorher ein
Leerzeile einfuegen.

Was muss ich tun damit ich auf Hex C1 abfragen kann ?

Die Satzlaenge soll bei Ein und -Ausgabe 133 sein.

ich dachte mir das in etwa so:

01 DRUCKSATZ.                                                      
    02 GRUNDINFO.                                                 
       05 FELD1        PIC X(1).                                 
       05 FELD1-COMP REDEFINES FELD1.                            
           10 F1-COMP        PIC 9(2) BINARY. ===> ?????
       05 FILLER       PIC X(132).

Dann In der Procedure Division die Abfrage ob F1-comp                 
                  = '41' oder ???

Da ich mir aber nicht sicher bin wï¿½re ich fï¿½r jeden Tip dankbar.

Danke fï¿½r die Unterstuetzung
John
```

#### ↳ Re: Umwandel Hex 'C1' to BLANK mit cobol.

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T09:39:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<400B97CE.4F2C4D26@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<1f69d9e5.0401162223.79d81ac6@posting.google.com>`

```
john schrieb:
> ich habe da ein kleines Problem mit Cobol. Ich habe Daten die an der
> ersten Stelle ein nicht abdruckbares Steuerzeichen enthalten. Hex ist
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Was muss ich tun damit ich auf Hex C1 abfragen kann ?

Hallo John,
wenn das Programm auf einer 'EBCDIC-Hardware' ablaufen soll, dann
entspricht X'C1' einem grossen 'A' und koennte direkt so abgefragt
werden; flexibler ist aber die Definition eines symbolic character:
 ...
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 ...
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
 ...
  SYMBOLIC CHARACTERS hex-c1 IS 194 
 ... 
und das dann in den Abfragen verwenden oder als Wert in einem
conditionnamen fï¿½r das erste Byte des Satzes, etwa
  88 vorschub VALUE hex-c1.
(Anmerkung: in der Symbolic characters Angabe duerfen mehrere solche
Namen definiert werden; der Dezimalwert entspricht der Position des
hexzeichens im Zeichenvorrat der Hardware, wobei COBOL ab 1 (nicht 0!)
zaehlt - Details siehe im Sprachmanual zu Deinem Compiler!)

Karl Kiesel
Fujitsu Siemens Computers GmbH, Mï¿½nchen
```

The message above does not point to working storage - it points to a line in
the procedure division. For example

MOVE XYZ TO MYVAR

Where "XYZ" is a PIC X(3) field and MYVAR is a PIC S9(3) COMP-3 field
will/may generate the message.

in effect?  It is (vaguely) possible that one of these is causing the problem.

in it?  I am reaching for straws here, but again, it really SHOULD work.

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(3) COMP = gives compile error with fujitsu power cobol

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T19:44:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401191944.645b53fb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f3ee9b61.0401180108.227c74a7@posting.google.com>`

```
This is from the FAQ/ERRATTA on my book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in
24 hours.  While this is not your exact error, I think it has the same
cause:

Non US readers may encounter the following error message when trying
to compile the examples in Hours 3 and 4 relating to the use of $ by
COBOL to indicate currency:

JMN1142I-S INVALID CHARACTER IS SPECIFIED IN PICTURE CHARACTER-STRING.

PICTURE 9 ASSUMED.

The simplest solution to this problem is to go into the Windows
regional settings from control panel, and set the currency symbol to
$. Another solution is to change the compiler directives from:

@OPTIONS MAIN to 

@OPTIONS MAIN,CURRENCY($)

=====================
The problem comes in when Windows has an "S" specified for the
"currency" sign.



d.castignone@axenta.com (d.castignone@axenta.com) wrote in message news:<f3ee9b61.0401180108.227c74a7@posting.google.com>...
> Hi
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> thx for help !

<a id="t-b0b36d36d7"></a>
## Thread: Using Telephony from COBOL — 1 msgs

### Using Telephony from COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T11:06:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<400b03ef_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
During the last few weeks I have been evaluating Telephony Appplication
Programming Interface (TAPI) components which enable your application
program to talk to a Voice modem, sound card, or digital telephony card.


They range in price and function from some very basic "after school" type
components written in VB, right up to fully abstracted development
environments that let you build an IVR application by dragging and dropping
"functions" (like, "answer the phone", "detect a digit pressed", "play a
sound file"...) around on your screen until you are happy with the flow, and
you never need to cope with the underlying TAPI functions.

There is a certain element of "fun" in doing this as you can use a good
waveform editor (I strongly recommend  AutoEdit Deluxe from Mystik
Media...worth the registration fee many times over) to build your own
outgoing messages and sound files.

I have looked at more than a dozen such components and am not completely
"happy" with any of the ones I can afford.

Many are written in China and the associated Help files are in pidgin
English and do not cover the ground properly.

I've therefore resolved to write my own, using COBOL.

The problem is that the TAPI functions seem to be fairly unstable and leave
your system in a state where it has to be rebooted after failed tests that
use certain functions of the interface. I suspect this is down partly to my
own inexperience in this area and partly to evolving TAPI standards. (Either
way, it can only get better...<G>)

My question is this:

Has anybody in CLC used the TAPI interface to build an answerphone, IVR, or
similar system?

Can you share what was good, bad, etc?

If anyone is interested in this kind of software and would like to
participate, please mail me privately.

Pete.
```

---

<a id="t-eede05ae7a"></a>
## Thread: Call DLL from Screenset — 2 msgs

### Call DLL from Screenset

- **From:** bigmarwin@seznam.cz (BigMarwin)
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T02:46:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1c5b0131.0401190246.6fc64b16@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
I need to call procedure "Someting" in "somedll.dll" from screenset.
Know somebody how I do this?
Thanks 
BigMarwin
```

#### ↳ Re: Call DLL from Screenset

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-19T19:46:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401191946.38a66091@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1c5b0131.0401190246.6fc64b16@posting.google.com>`

```
Lots of assumptions here.

I assume that you are calling it via Dialog System and MicroFocus
COBOL.

You can call the DLL from Dialog - MAYBE - using a COUT.  However, if
this doesn't work for you - wrap the call to the DLL in a COBOL
program and use COUT to do it.

If you are not talking about Dialog system - ignore this message.



bigmarwin@seznam.cz (BigMarwin) wrote in message news:<1c5b0131.0401190246.6fc64b16@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
> I need to call procedure "Someting" in "somedll.dll" from screenset.
> Know somebody how I do this?
> Thanks 
> BigMarwin
```

Hello all

Cobol IDE. What does it have to do? Your thoughts will be taken into
account.

Sergey Grigorchuk.
Lemo Soft Company. www.lemosoft.com

#### ↳ Re: New Cobol IDE

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-20T17:02:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bujn02$k9t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 20-Jan-2004, ukman@yandex.ru (Sergey Grigorchuk) wrote:

> We are developing new Cobol IDE. We've added a lot of powerful features but
> we would like to ask you express your suggestions and wishes about "ideal"
> Cobol IDE. What does it have to do? Your thoughts will be taken into
> account.
> Thanks

You are developing it in what environment for what target customer?

Are you wanting to integrate it with JavaBeans on a Unix machine, with an IDMS
database on an MVS machine?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New Cobol IDE

- **From:** ukman@yandex.ru (Sergey Grigorchuk)
- **Date:** 2004-01-21T01:32:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d9b87324.0401210132.2a1bc043@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d9b87324.0401200044.19a8dc83@posting.google.com> <bujn02$k9t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Our IDE is writen on Java. It uses Eclipse as UI base (but it isn't
Eclipse's COBOL subproject) so it can work on Windows, Unix, Linux,
MacOS (all platforms that Eclipse supports). We don't develop it for
any particular customer. We orient on IBM, Microfocus, UniSYS versions
of COBOL.

The main goal of our IDE is to provide useful operations with sources
(syntax analysis, errors detection, browsing, usage reports,
refactoring...). Not all features are ready yet but we are working.

Could you please send me more details about your proposal on
sng@lemosoft.com

Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New Cobol IDE

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-21T16:36:26+00:00
