## Thread: [Fwd: Re: Mainframe not a good architecture for interactive was Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???]] — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### [Fwd: Re: Mainframe not a good architecture for interactive was Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???]]

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-01-25T21:35:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bv1r7c$pgt$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`

```
I belated posted Pete Dashwood's comments to the mainframe list ibm-main 
and almost instantly got the following response.  John does not follow 
comp.lang.cobol.

-------- Original Message --------
Subject: Re: [Fwd: Re: Mainframe not a good architecture for interactive 
was Re: What is the  future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???]
Date: Sun, 25 Jan 2004 04:58:39 GMT
From: John S. Giltner, Jr. <giltjr@earthlink.net>
Organization: EarthLink Inc. -- http://www.EarthLink.net
Newsgroups: bit.listserv.ibm-main
References: <3FE0AC45.3070208@istar.ca>

Personal opinion:  Whomever Pete is seems to be trying to compare a
networking protocol (TCP/IP) to a computer architecture (mainframe) and
a application server (CICS).  He should try comparing:

	TCP/IP to SNA
	IBM s/390 cpus to Intel 486's or IBM PowerPC, Sun's Sparc
	CICS to ?????

Other comments inserted below:

Clark F. Morris, Jr. wrote:

> In the posting below Peter Dashwood is agreeing with Howard Hinman on
> the inappropriateness of the architecture based on his experience with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> compute power and main memory than the 3090.

CPU yes, I/O I am not sure.

> 
> When applications are replaced in your shop (and many mainframe COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that platform?

We are look at three possibility, all use IBM current mainframe as the
server:

	EJB's under WAS with EJB handing the DB calls.
	EJB's under WAS using JDBC
	EJB's front ending CICS apps and CICS apps doing the DB calls.

Data base is DB2.


> 
> -------- Original Message --------
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Hum, I bet Pete was an application programmer and not a system
programmer.  CICS only maintains the state of terminal that are in
session with it, just like any and all "distributed" server
applications.  In the early days CICS did need to have pre-defined
tables entries for any terminal that was going to connect to it, but you
have been able to dynamically defined and delete this entries for over
20 years now.  If distrubuted servers share the same single buffer for
all connections/sessions how does it keep track if information that is
unqiue to each connection/session.


> PC networks don't work that way. The devices on the network are
> recognised by addressable hardware cards. There are a number of
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the network were destroyed by nuclear attack.)

The comparison is of totally different things.  One is a application
server (CICS) the other is a networking protocol (TCP/IP).  SNA does
keep track of the status of every terminal that it owns.  TCP/IP does
not keep track of every IP address within it's subnet.  However, a
distributed server using TCP/IP as it networking protocol does keep
track of each client that is connected to it by IP address.  A web
server has a "DFHCOMM area" for each HTTP connection.  A since getting a
single web page from a single client can create up to 5 HTTP connections
  to a Web Server, this means that for an equal number of clients, a Web
server is keeping track of 5 times the connections.

> 
> Howard claims ASP was 9 times faster than the mainframe. Presumably,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> seconds).

Depends on the application design and size of the mainframe (just like
in a distributed environment) the number of transactions and response
time.  We average 30 tps with avg. response time of less than .5
seconds.  We can peak in the 100 of tps, however it is not CPU that is
restricting this, it is the speed of the network connections to our
customers.  Guess what, the majority of our sessions connections are
TCP/IP, not SNA.

> 
> The problem is that when you come to compare the cost of a LAN, served
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> cost, which includes infrastructure support, systems programmers,  etc.)

Umm, I can remember reading cost comparisons as recent as 3 years ago
where a full distributed environment supports 2000 users with Windows
servers was 4 times more expensive than a mainframe environment and
using UNIX based servers was 2 times more expensive.  I can remember
studies from 10 years ago where the numbers were 7 and 5 times more
expensive.  The two major issues was the personal cost of supports 2000
PC's and the fact that it took many, many distributed servers to run the
same number of applications as were being run on a single mainframe.
Most distributed servers run one application per box, or in some
instances multiple boxes run the same application either for backup or
because a single box can not handle the load. A large scale Intel box,
IBM pSeries, or Sun Sparc can get into the millions, just like a
mainframe.

> 
> That is probably why Client/Server has been encroaching on what was
> previously the mainframe's domain.

Define Client/Server.  That I am aware of the development of client
server applications are on the downward trend and Web based applications
are being used.  The argument could be made that Web is "thin" client
server, but then it could be argued that CICS and 3270 is "thinner"
client server.  Yes, some Web based applications can get thick, with
Javascript or Java applets, but these offload some of the processing off
of the "sever" (be it a MF, *inx, Windows box).

> 
>> CICS is currently used
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> is about dollars (this is where the mainframe is losing badly...)

Not really.  If you look at performance only the mainframe is more
expensive, however when you start looking at reliability a distributed
environment start getting more expensive when you want to keep it up.

> 
>> Sub-second response
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> billion transactions per day, without requiring mainframes.

A mainframe enviroment can do billions of transactions a day.

How do you know GOOGLE is not running mainframes?  They are using Linux
based Web server and Linux does run on IBM mainframes.

Define mainframe. Unisys make the ES/7000 a hugh box with many many
processors and many independent PCI buses.  The box is designed to be
used as a central computer in a computing environment, to me this means
mainframe.  The same thing can be said for some if IBM pSeries servers,
future xSeries, and some of Sun's large systems.

Now, you also have look at what the transactions are.  The majority of
GOOGLE's transactions are queries, very few updates.  Updates are what
slow things down.  IBM lastest performance number on their largest
mainframe shows that a fully configured plex could do the same number of
transactions in 24 hours that are done on the Internet in one week.  I
fully confiugred plex is 32 boxes.  Lets see 32 distrubuted boxes do thtat


> 
>> If personal
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> significantly. CICS just won't cut it...)

What is wrong with TCP/IP?  Mainframes have been supporting TCP/IP
longer than Windows.  They can support up to 24 Gigabit Ethernet
adapters per box and drive all of them to line speed.

CICS is NOT a network, it is application server.

What distributed software is better than mainframe software.


> 
> I see the future role of the mainframe as Batch Processing, until such
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I doubt if batch processing will ever go away.  I mean are backups batch?
```

I just had to drop in a few comments.  Please forgive the middle-posting:

In article <bv1r7c$pgt$1@news.eusc.inter.net>,
 "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
> I belated posted Pete Dashwood's comments to the mainframe list ibm-main 
> and almost instantly got the following response.  John does not follow

Because of excessiive posting and reposting, I'm not sure if C.F.Morris 
or J.S.Giltner posted this...but I have to comment.

>[much snippage]
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> unqiue to each connection/session.

The conceit of this statement is amazing.  Do you realize that all of 
the helpful applications that you, as a sysprog, use every day are 
written by applications programmers???

There were days when sysprogs were selected from the ranks of 
applications programmers for their willingness to understand the lower 
level operations of things.  That was something like 30 years ago.

Today, there is a pretty clear difference between "programmer" and 
"administrator".

From previous discussions on C.L.C. I know Peter Dw is quite clueless in 
the areas of modern CICS.  But I also have seen enough snippets of his 
code to know that he is one of the few Cobol programmers that groks 
sensible code construction, and is all around quite functional.


> > PC networks don't work that way. The devices on the network are
> > recognised by addressable hardware cards. There are a number of
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> server is keeping track of 5 times the connections.

Here, you show that you do not understand subnets.  TCP/IP keeps just 
fine track of all the addresses on a single host.  A subnet is only a 
possible range -- using your implied criteria, does SNA keep track of 
all possible LU's defined by an 8-byte name space?

And an HTTP connection can create as many connections as the receiving 
client (and feeding server) will allow.  Why only 5?  Stateless 
protocols operate in stateless ways.

You seem to be trying to confuse the benefits of fixed circuit 
networking with virtual circuit networking.  Or maybe just having an IE 
moment.


> >> Sub-second response
> >> time is a major goal for many CICS systems.  3000 transactions a second
> >> may not be unusual for some of the larger systems.

Which decade are you in?  You could add an order of magnitude to that.

```
Hello all.  After Clark posted part of some comments to the 
bit.listserv.ibm-main I decided to check out comp.lang.cobol.

I also will "middle-post" and if this is a no-no here I do apologize, 
some of the news groups I follow allow it, in fact they encourage it on 
lost posts.

Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> I just had to drop in a few comments.  Please forgive the middle-posting:
<--- SNIP --->

<--- END SNIP --->
> 
>>[much snippage]
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> written by applications programmers???

I did not mean to offend anybody by guessing the Pete was an application 
programmer.  My comment was to point out that most application 
programmers do not understand the inner working of CICS and that CICS 
does not keep track of ALL terminals in a mainframe network.  This is 
something that an application programmer would not normally know because 
they do not need to know this.  I respect application programmers, they 
write code that I do not have the skills to write and it does make my 
life easier.

<-- SNIP -->

<-- END SNIP -->
> 
>>>PC networks don't work that way. The devices on the network are
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> all possible LU's defined by an 8-byte name space?

I do fully understand subnets.  SNA does not keep track of ALL possible 
LU's defined by an 8-byte name space.  I did not state this.  In SNA 
there is a concept of a physical node and logical nodes, and physical 
nodes do keep track of the status of all physical nodes directly 
connected to them, and all logical nodes defined on those physical 
nodes.  In a IP network a host only keeps track of the IP address 
defined on that host.  It does not keep track of other IP hosts, not at 
the IP level, you can write programs that keep track of other nodes, but 
this is not part the the IP archicture.  Keeping track of other devices 
connected to you and devices (either physical or logical) that you own 
is part of SNA.  This does cause a lot of trivial network traffic and is 
why SNA is a chatty protocol.

I guess I was trying to compare the statement made by Pete about CICS to 
a typical TCP/IP based C/S system, Web Server/Browser.  He was 
attempting to state that CICS had to keep track of every terminal in the 
network and that CICS had buffers for the status of every terminal in 
the network.  My point was that CICS does not keep track of every 
terminal in the network, it only keeps track of the terminals that are 
currently in session with CICS.  He, in my opinion, seemed to indicate 
in a C/S application based on TCP/IP that there was nothing that kept 
track of the sessions and no buffers used.  The point I was attempting 
to make was that it does not matter what environment you are in, if 
there is a session between a client and a server (be it CICS and a 3270 
terminal, Web Server and a Browser, POP3 server and POP3 client, or 
anything else) the server must keep track of the session and must have 
buffers allocated to do this.

> 
> And an HTTP connection can create as many connections as the receiving 
> client (and feeding server) will allow.  Why only 5?  Stateless 
> protocols operate in stateless ways.

Yes, HTTP as a defined protocol can create as many connection as allowed 
However, most modern web browser limit the number of connections created 
to render a single web page to 5.  The 1st connection is used to get the 
main html page and they use up to 4 other concurrent sessions to get the 
other files that may be reference in the HTML document.  Many years back 
you had the option to change and the range was from 1 to 8, at least in 
the first version of Netscape that I used you had this option.  You may 
still be able to this, but it is not obvious to me.  If you deployed 
your application based on HTTP, or wrote your own Web Browser, you could 
start as many connections as you wish.  In fact in Mozilla based browers 
you can enable HTTP pipelining and this will use just a single HTTP 
connection to transfer all files referenced in the html document.


> 
> You seem to be trying to confuse the benefits of fixed circuit 
> networking with virtual circuit networking.  Or maybe just having an IE 
> moment.
>

I was, and it seems that I was unsucesfull, attempting clear up some of 
Petes comparison of CICS (me assuming SNA networking) to distributed 
(Pete stating TCP/IP networking).  Which does bring me back to my 
original comments about Petes comments.  Comparing CICS to TCP/IP is a 
bad comparision, they are not the same thing, one is a application 
server that uses networking protocols, the other is a networking 
protocol.  It like comparing a car to a the road.  And I did a bad job 
attempting to show that the two were different and a direct comparision 
could not be made.

<--- SNIP --->
```

state

…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> the network.

No I wasn't. I was saying it had a terminal dependent buffer for each
connected terminal it is in discussion with.  I believe it is called a
DFHCOMMAREA? In IMS it is a Scratch Pad Area (SPA). (It is 30 years since I
dealt with these things and my head is now full of OO... so I can't be as
accurate as I once was... ;-). I did say that system tables were defined to
define which terminals it COULD be in pseudo-conversation with. You pointed
out that that requirement is not true in modern CICS. I have no problem with
that.

I am also well aware of the differences between a Network Protocol and a TP
monitor...


> My point was that CICS does not keep track of every
> terminal in the network, it only keeps track of the terminals that are
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, I did indicate that. I was trying to contrast the non-stateless nature
of  a mainframe TP monitor (which uses terminal dependent buffers that
''remember'' the previous point in the conversation from that terminal),
with the stateless nature of a Web Page, which doesn't. I'm not suggesting
Web Browsers don't use buffers; they just aren't terminal dependent and do
not carry previous states (as a serially reusable program might on the
mainframe). The Web Server does have a TCP/IP address and it can have
Session variables which allow continuity across web pages from the same
terminal, but it does not behave as a TP monitor does on a mainframe. I
believe that the mainframe method is not as good; but I accept that that is
arguable opinion and I don't really care enough about it to argue it.
> >
> > And an HTTP connection can create as many connections as the receiving
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Pete stating TCP/IP networking).

Yes, I could have done a better job of explaining what I was really getting
at. (BTW, I programmed SNA applications in the very early releases of it
when we were all so impressed it could do peer-to-peer. It was a far cry
from B/QTAM... although I seem to remember they were still there ''under the
covers''<G>)


> Which does bring me back to my
> original comments about Petes comments.  Comparing CICS to TCP/IP is a
> bad comparision, they are not the same thing, one is a application
> server that uses networking protocols, the other is a networking
> protocol.

It was never my intention to compare them in that way. My emphasis should
have been on the states, rather than the protocol.

Cheers,

Pete.

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

<--- SNIP --->
<--- END SNIP --->
> 
> It was never my intention to compare them in that way. My emphasis should
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Keeping track of states of a conversation is differnt.  However, they 
have nothing to do with the platform, mainframe vs. distributed.  That 
deals more with the way the server and client applications are written.

EJB's have statefull session beans, which are basically the same as 
conversational CICS transactions (which have been a no-no for many 
years).  EJB's have stateless session beans, which are more like CICS 
pseudo-conversational  transactions.  It is my understanding, I could 
have been confused, that session beans keep track of the state if the 
session, just like CICS.

Yes, CICS is some what different from 20 or 30 years ago (even 10 years 
ago).  In fact it can run "native" TCP/IP applications such as:

	It has a build in Web Server.
	It can run java servlets.
	It can run EJB's (stateless sessions beans only right now.
	It support using IIOP and SOAP.
	Or you can roll your own.

If you all want you may want to look at the group bit.listserv.ibm-main 
where there are many more post with this subject.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Mainframe not a good architecture for interactive wasRe: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???]]

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T21:23:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bv998502946@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bv1r7c$pgt$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <joe_zitzelberger-55AFDA.22271526012004@corp.supernews.com> <zTlRb.146$zG1.71@newsread3.news.atl.earthlink.net> <RwwRb.120657$6y6.2389843@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

In article <RwwRb.120657$6y6.2389843@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> writes:
> 
> John S. Giltner, Jr. <giltjr@earthlink.net> wrote in message news:zTlRb.146$zG1.71@newsread3.news.atl.earthlink.net...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > to render a single web page to 5.

> Microsoft Knowledge Base Article - 183110
> INFO: WinInet Limits Connections Per Server
> http://support.microsoft.com/default.aspx?scid=kb;en-us;183110

We're off-topic (as usual), but here are a couple of brief comments
about that article:

1. It's out of date.  RFC 2068 is no longer the HTTP/1.1 specification;
RFC 2616 superceded it.  However, the relevant material in this case
appears to be unchanged.

2. It's misleading if read over-broadly.  It's about WinInet, not HTTP
clients in general.  RFC 2616 says:

   Clients that use persistent connections SHOULD limit the number of
   simultaneous connections that they maintain to a given server. A
   single-user client SHOULD NOT maintain more than 2 connections with
   any server or proxy. (8.1.4)

Points to note:

- This is a SHOULD requirement.  It's mandatory for full compliance,
but not for conditional compliance.

- It applies only to clients that use persistent connections.  That's
probably not the case in the example John gave, since persistent
connections are generally combined with pipelining, and a pipelining
client doesn't want to open multiple connections to the same server.

- It applies only to single-user clients.

- There are other recommendations for proxies.  If your HTTP server is
serving a fully-compliant HTTP/1.1 proxy, it might well have more than
2, or 5, connections.

HTTP/1.1 is a fairly complex protocol - it's not LU6.2, but it's not
SMTP either.  It's often not the best choice for an example because
it's hard to remember everything in the spec, existing practice is
frequently non-compliant, and many people are confused about what the
spec actually requires.
```

<a id="t-b661f77498"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus Developer Forum — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus Developer Forum

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-26T19:48:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dgra10hqohcvgfqj1drar98vu8tcuf8mp3@4ax.com>`

```
Comp.lang.cobol folks:

This message is for all comp.lang.cobol participants who also use
Micro Focus products.

I am a firm believer in face-to-face communication between customer
and vendor.  This applies to training sessions, technical forums, user
conferences, etc.  I think that it would be very beneficial to the
entire COBOL community in general, if we had more of these types of
conferences.

Therefore,, I wanted to alert the Micro Focus customers that Micro
Focus is sponsoring a Developer Forum from February 29, 2004 through
March 3, 2004 in Dallas, Texas.

This is an opportunity for you to speak directly with the management
and technicians who provide you with your COBOL IDE.

They have established the vaery reasonable price of $595 for advanced
registration and $695 for onsite registration.

I encourage all Micro Focus customers to attend.

You can learn more at www.microfocus.com/devforum




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

Hi,
    I'm buying sources of small Cobol applications, only for Windows.
    To those who interested, please send the description of the application,
the price, the database (SQL, ODBC ou ISAM) and the tool used to build
reports.
    We give preference to applications built with PowerCobol.
    Send the information to euromercante@sapo.pt.

Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-27T23:59:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401272359.6f0513@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com>...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> > http://msn-cnet.com.com/2100-1046_3-5148148.html
> 
> Those that thought OO was a fad have been vindicated.

The author of the article and the unfortunate who said "away from OO"
were misleading.  XML based interprocess communications ARE OO BASED. 
They mean SOAP - even if it's internal and the plumbing is hidden. 
I'll leave it to you to lookup what SOAP stands for.

It is further proof however, that we continue in the age of "form"
above "substance" (read the quote at the bottom about the goal).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T21:28:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40177340_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> > http://msn-cnet.com.com/2100-1046_3-5148148.html
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Nope. Not by a long chalk. That claim can be made when the World goes away
from it.  (By then it will have been around so long that saying it was a
"fad" would be inaccurate.)

 Because one man in one department in MicroSoft, who has yet to show a
working product, feels that COM is getting too much of the budget, does not
spell "the end" for OO or Component technology. I believe these things are
bigger than that man, possibly even bigger than MicroSoft.

"Services" are certainly a "Good Thing" but to date they are being developed
(for the most part) using OO and COM/CORBA. Changing the way they exchange
messages won't change that significantly.

Pete.
```

> The two are not mutually exclusive. The company with the predominant market

The problem is that 'the keeper of the standard' uses this as a weapon
to keep market dominance rather than a unifying and standardising
process.  And the sheep don't notice.  "Where do ewe want to go today
?"

In the article the 'standard' of COM and DCOM is dead, the new
'standard' of .NET will die and will be replaced by something else in
Longhorn.

> > Maybe not to you.
> 
> Nor to 90% of the desktops in the world. Technology doesn't count for spit
> compared to other considerations except in one, small, area: a justification
> for hating Micros~1.

Which is exactly why those 90% are virus infected, spam re-mailing,
archaic, over-bloated toys where form is everything and function is
marginalised.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T13:17:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401291317.310ea108@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <163715e7.0401280455.5477cdf3@posting.google.com> <xNOdnZlbVoLXXIrdRVn-gw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0401281035.374fee1b@posting.google.com> <bvb5h3$fls$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > No.  The best marketeer has the most sheep following them.
> 
> OK.   The sheep set the standard.   Look at the many web pages designed to work
> with I.E. but fail when used with a browser that follows rules.

Often the web site is completely unaware that the pages don't work
with standards based browsers, often the web sites are unaware that
there are other browsers.  They just use 'Front Page' to make pretty
pages and MS ensures that Front Page generates code that breaks
browsers other than the latest MS one.

This is intended to force non-MS software users to buy MS software,
and MS software users with older software to upgrade.  Exactly the
same happens with MS Office documents.

It is only dumb sheep who think that this represents any form of
'standards'.

In some cases the non-standard page 'features' were contracturally
required by Microsoft.  Back when MS drove others out of the market by
giving away IE (actually it was SpyGlass's revenue that was being
forgone, not MS's), they were allowing sites (such as AOL) to
distribute IE but contractuarally requiring that IE specific
non-standard extensions were coded into the site's web pages and then
suggesting that the user should download IE to overcome the problems
they were having.

Only sheep would not see how disingenuous that was.

Now with Longhorn MS will attempt to change yet again to some new sets
of 'standards'.  MS don't want to stop spam or viruses, yet, they want
to use these to get the sheep to upgrade to a new mechanism that will
keep the 'evil hackers' out by reinventing the internet with new
mechanisms that guarantee an all-MS world, protected by MS patents.

The problem for MS is that it is at least two years away and even
sheep get restless.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Market driven vs Standards (was: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T21:39:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N4fSb.2289$F23.554@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <163715e7.0401280455.5477cdf3@posting.google.com> <xNOdnZlbVoLXXIrdRVn-gw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0401281035.374fee1b@posting.google.com> <bvb5h3$fls$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0401291317.310ea108@posting.google.com>`

```
Is it just me, or does some of this remind anyone else of "IBM - in the 60's and
70's - and COBOL"?

It seems to me that GOBACK, COMP-3,  REDEFINES of previous not original data
items, etc all were "forced" upon a BUNCH <G> of vendors of COBOL - just to
"keep up".  (Not all vendors did this, but certainly some did)
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:N4fSb.2289$F23.554@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net...
> Is it just me, or does some of this remind anyone else of "IBM - in the
60's and
> 70's - and COBOL"?
>
> It seems to me that GOBACK, COMP-3,  REDEFINES of previous not original
data
> items, etc all were "forced" upon a BUNCH <G> of vendors of COBOL - just
to
> "keep up".  (Not all vendors did this, but certainly some did)
>

Yes, Bill. I agree that the idea that whoever dominates the Market sets the
"standard",  certainly came from the days before the Fall of the IBM Empire.
It was quite common for non-IBM vendors extensions to be dismissed as
"dangerous, non-standard, extensions, that lock you in to a specific
platform", while the IBM extensions were "pre-releases of what will soon be
the standard...."

How naive we were in retrospect. There's no way the sophisticated IT people
of people today would fall for such a ruse... <G>.

There's no chance of MicroSoft repeating IBM history.....is there?

Pete.

<snip>
```

Some comments below...

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0401291317.310ea108@posting.google.com...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

>

I have recently been working on a Chinese Web site which has some pages in
English. They asked me to review their very poor English and I agreed. (The
things one does for money...<G>)

I FTPed the pages in question  down to my machine, and was appalled at the
intricate nesting of Layout tables, which was really quite unnecessary.
Dreamweaver flags them all and shows the "wheels within wheels" graphically
as well as in code.

I marvelled at how anyone could set up such rubbish, then I saw that the
page was generated by FrontPage... 'nuff said.

> This is intended to force non-MS software users to buy MS software,
> and MS software users with older software to upgrade.  Exactly the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
All too often people believe that WHATEVER MS says is the "standard". I have
encountered this on may sites and it is very diffcult to shift this idea
once it is in someone's head... All I can conclude is that MS Marketing is
very effective.#

> In some cases the non-standard page 'features' were contracturally
> required by Microsoft.  Back when MS drove others out of the market by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Is that "urban myth" or did they really get away with it?

> Only sheep would not see how disingenuous that was.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sheep get restless.

Yes, they certainly do. Sadly, they don't have a lot of choice in terms of
pasture...or shepherd, for that matter....

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

Richard wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> browsers other than the latest MS one.

Exactly! That's why we write software exclusively for Windows.

If it works with Windows, that's fine by us. If some other OS breaks, tough
shit. I am NOT going back to the days where we had to have an airplane
hanger full of printers and modems to make sure our stuff worked on every
concievable permutation of hardware and software.

It's far simplier for our customer to buy (or we'll give them) $250 for the
latest version of Windows than for our small programming staff to come up
with versions that work with Amigas, MACs, BSD, Linux, Unix, and all the
other weevils.

>
> This is intended to force non-MS software users to buy MS software,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'standards'.

Hmmm. If (big percentage) agree on something, doesn't that become the
standard (de facto, at least)?

>
> In some cases the non-standard page 'features' were contracturally
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> they were having.

"Drove others out of business?" Who? Meanwhile, millions - make that tens of
millions - didn't have to pay Netscape $50 (and that was back when $50 was a
lot of money).

>
> Only sheep would not see how disingenuous that was.
>

Bah! (or Baa!)

> Now with Longhorn MS will attempt to change yet again to some new sets
> of 'standards'.  MS don't want to stop spam or viruses, yet, they want
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> sheep get restless.

Yeah. They (or we) will either just mill around and cope, or try jumping
over the fence. Evidence shows, however, when sheep jump the fence all that
happens is that people go to sleep.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T11:09:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401301109.4cecea3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <163715e7.0401280455.5477cdf3@posting.google.com> <xNOdnZlbVoLXXIrdRVn-gw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0401281035.374fee1b@posting.google.com> <bvb5h3$fls$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0401291317.310ea108@posting.google.com> <ScydnXuieKG0PYTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> > It is only dumb sheep who think that this represents any form of
> > 'standards'.
> 
> Hmmm. If (big percentage) agree on something, doesn't that become the
> standard (de facto, at least)?

You confirm my assertion.    ;-)

Actually there is a large difference between 'agree' and blind passive
aquiescence.  With MS they have demonstrated that their
implementations are only 'standard's until others can interact with
them, and then they change it to ensure their customers need to spend
more money and that their competitors no longer work.

And now with, say, XML documents in Word, they have patents on certain
processes so that they can control who can and cannot interchange data
with them.
 
> "Drove others out of business?" Who? 

You must get youur news feed exclusively from MS and MSN for you to
have to ask.

> Meanwhile, millions - make that tens of
> millions - didn't have to pay Netscape $50 (and that was back when $50 was a
> lot of money).

Actually there used to be dozens of browser makers, Netscape was the
most visible.  I used the IBM one supplied with OS/2 (it was also
available for Windows) when Bill Gates 'The Road Ahead' edition 1 did
not even mention the Internet.

MS got Spyglass to write IE for them on a contract that specified that
they would be paid $5 for every copy _sold_.  MS never paid them
anything for creating this product for them, it was not 'sold'.  This
drove Spyglass out of business.  Being a friend of MS is just as
deadly as being an enemy.

Fortunately there are now several browsers that are far better than IE
and that don't have the security problems that MS has built into later
versions. I use Mozilla mostly.  Sometimes Konqueror or Galleon
because it is there.  My son uses Opera, without my prompting, because
it is so much better than IE.  Tabbed browsing, no pop-ups, much
faster, and it is much more secure:

"""------ from Slashdot --------
"In a recent Microsoft Knowledge Base article, they provide 'Steps
that you can take to help identify and to help protect yourself from
deceptive (spoofed) Web sites and malicious hyperlinks.' These steps
include always using SSL/TLS, typing 'JScript commands' in the address
bar, and typing in URLs instead of clicking links! I have a suggestion
that's not in the Knowledge Base: don't use IE!"
------------------- """
 
But the grand plan for IE was not just to drive Netscape broke, it was
also to _replace_ the Internet with MS's own network to replace AOL
and CmpuServe. If you recall in the early days of IE they _had_ to
connect via MSN and had only limited interaction with the Internet.

MS wanted to drive a new 'standard' that they controlled.  Fortunately
they never got the sheep masses that would have allowed them to
declare that the world had changed.

> Yeah. They (or we) will either just mill around and cope, or try jumping
> over the fence. Evidence shows, however, when sheep jump the fence all that
> happens is that people go to sleep.

That's OK, you can advocate and follow the Rip Van Winkle approach.
```

Richard wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> more money and that their competitors no longer work.

I agree that part of Micros~1's motivation may be to cripple competing
products. Therefore, we have the people who try to compete with Micros~1 to
blame for the ever-changing 'standards.' In other words, if there were no
proto-competitors, Micros~1 wouldn't have to change anything!

Seems like the real enemy to 'standardization,' then, are the would-be
competitors.

>
> And now with, say, XML documents in Word, they have patents on certain
> processes so that they can control who can and cannot interchange data
> with them.

Maybe we can get more stability, now, as a result. People who keep trying to
one-up Micros~1 can concentrate on something useful, like growing organic
peanuts.
>
>> "Drove others out of business?" Who?
>
> You must get youur news feed exclusively from MS and MSN for you to
> have to ask.

As a matter of fact, I do. What's that got to do with anything?

>
>> Meanwhile, millions - make that tens of
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> deadly as being an enemy.

Nah, going out of business because you are stupid is called "Economic
Darwinism."

>
> Fortunately there are now several browsers that are far better than IE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> faster, and it is much more secure:

Cool. And your security concerns make sense for you, but you promote a
fundamental error. Non-IE browsers are not inherently more secure - or at
least no one has proven that to be the case. It's much more likely the case
that virus writers don't bother with attacking Mozilla.

You really should be using IE, though. It's only a matter of time until
Micros~1's Front Page cripples all non-IE browsers. Just go to sleep. When
you wake, it will all be better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401311146.6c0e3015@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Y_ednZG_P_qwQ4vdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <163715e7.0401280455.5477cdf3@posting.google.com> <xNOdnZlbVoLXXIrdRVn-gw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0401281035.374fee1b@posting.google.com> <bvb5h3$fls$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0401291317.310ea108@posting.google.com> <ScydnXuieKG0PYTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0401301109.4cecea3@posting.google.com> <B_adncbKEaJQX4bdRVn-gw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> I agree that part of Micros~1's motivation may be to cripple competing
> products. Therefore, we have the people who try to compete with Micros~1 to
> blame for the ever-changing 'standards.' In other words, if there were no
> proto-competitors, Micros~1 wouldn't have to change anything!

No. Wrong.  They change the 'standards' so that everyone has to
upgrade to the new versions.
 
> Seems like the real enemy to 'standardization,' then, are the would-be
> competitors.

No. Wrong.  The real enemy to 'standardisation' is the sheep like
users who accept MS's constant change and breaking of the _actual_
standards.

> Maybe we can get more stability, now, as a result. 


> People who keep trying to
> one-up Micros~1 can concentrate on something useful, like growing organic
> peanuts.


> > You must get youur news feed exclusively from MS and MSN for you to
> > have to ask.
> 
> As a matter of fact, I do. What's that got to do with anything?

The Russians used to believe what they read in Pravda.


> Cool. And your security concerns make sense for you, but you promote a
> fundamental error. Non-IE browsers are not inherently more secure - or at
> least no one has proven that to be the case. It's much more likely the case
> that virus writers don't bother with attacking Mozilla.

Actually it has been proven.  'phishing' works best with IE because a
flaw that exists in IE that has yet to be fixed.  This flaw does not
exist in other browsers.  Clicking a link that, in IE, looks like it
goes to 'www.mybank.com' and this address shows up in the address bar
may actually be a link to www.mybank.com%00:secret@www.ripoffsite.ru
where it ask you to input your id and password.

With IE the site looks genuine, with others it is obviously not.

But then you only read MS approved news so you wouldn't know that.

> You really should be using IE, though. It's only a matter of time until
> Micros~1's Front Page cripples all non-IE browsers. 

Actually MS will also cripple all pre-6.x versions of IE and then all
versions of IE to attempt to force users to buy new versions of
Windows and, eventually, Longhorn.

Fortunately it is only crap home-pagers who use Front-Page so there is
no problem in simply ignoring those sites.  In any case they probably
give viruses to Windows users.

> Just go to sleep. When you wake, it will all be better.

You seem to have done that already.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T11:37:06+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> http://msn-cnet.com.com/2100-1046_3-5148148.html
>
Very interesting. Thanks for posting it, Hugh.

"Services" communicating by XML under a .NET-like umbrella, is all very
well, but we already have that capability.

The fact is that .NET has not had the uptake  MS would like, and this looks
to me like a fairly desperate effort to grab SOMETHING before the house of
cards falls to Unix and Java.

To blame OO for the failure of .NET is like blaming cats for the fact that
there are mice in the house.

Java, which they admit is so successful they really MUST do something about
it, is completely Object Oriented and was designed that way from day 1.

If MS can sow the seeds of doubt regarding the paradigm their perceived
competition is based on, they can maybe stem the tide a bit.

I'll discard OO when it is of no further use to me. I don't see that as
being in this lifetime, but then I have less remaining  lifetime than many
of you reading this...<G>

Pete
```

- **From:** "The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T02:15:12+00:00

Having an understand of OO, maybe someone here can explain it
to me in a nutshell. I have the background, and can swallow the
technical jargon. I just haven't ever been able to see the attraction.

But then I don't know it either. Got any site where I could get an
understanding of the concepts. Be aware, I'm probably just going
to gain the understanding and then laugh about it, because it is
some kind of technique done on a mainframe 30 years ago, which
has been given a fancy name.

Kinda' like the time I overheard two PC programmers discussing
a new topic one had been reading about, and how they thought it
would really help their development efforts. The topic discussed?

Overlays....

But, I don't recall them assigning any fancy name to it, as yet.

So, do you think OO is some revelation, or just past technology
with a new name, introduced as revolutionary?

Convince me, please.

Thanks - Gary Walker

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T13:07:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4019a0d0_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com...
>
> Having an understand of OO, maybe someone here can explain it
> to me in a nutshell. I have the background, and can swallow the
> technical jargon. I just haven't ever been able to see the attraction.
>

Some people feel that way about anal intercourse...

> But then I don't know it either. Got any site where I could get an
> understanding of the concepts. Be aware, I'm probably just going
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Having been deeply involved in programming mainframes (of numerous
descriptions) thirty years ago, I can assure you that this trite dismissal
of OO is completely inaccurate. It is a convenient way for people who don't
really want to know, to persuade themselves that they are not missing
anything and to remain smugly ensconced in the world of what they DO know.

Understanding OO requires effort and change.

Those two facts alone are usually enough to dissuade the casual enquirer,
but it is comforting to think that you're not missing anything, so just
decide it is "old concepts repackaged". (It really isn't, but I'm tired of
defending it here...)

> Kinda' like the time I overheard two PC programmers discussing
> a new topic one had been reading about, and how they thought it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> But, I don't recall them assigning any fancy name to it, as yet.

Never heard of "Virtual Storage" or "System Paging" <G>? (Same old ideas
just repackaged...)

>
> So, do you think OO is some revelation, or just past technology
> with a new name, introduced as revolutionary?
>

I think it is a revelation.

> Convince me, please.
>

Why?

> Thanks - Gary Walker
>
You're welcome...

Pete.
<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** "RH" <nospam@hotmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
It's hard to explain because there's too much information to absorb.  OO
theory by itself is just the beginning.  OO patterns theory is really where
it comes into its own.

But, here is something to try:

-spend a few months learning basic calculus and geometry.  This is not
really any different than spending time learning some equivalent business
theory.
-write a basic 3D rendering engine in C; go ahead and use APIs if you want.
-then rewrite it in C++ or Delphi or even Java (although it won't be fast).

By the time you are done you'll be thinking "Holy Crap, who the heck would
do something that complicated in C when you have OO language X."  Even John
Carmack now uses C++ instead of C; and he's a harcore coder.

I actually did this, believe it or not.  When I was done I had an
understanding of why I'd never go back to orthodox procedural coding -- 
under many circumstances --.  It still has its uses (ex: Perl).  But used
properly it "just makes sense" (tm).



"The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com...
>
> Having an understand of OO, maybe someone here can explain it
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> > to me like a fairly desperate effort to grab SOMETHING before the house
of
> > cards falls to Unix and Java.
> >
> > To blame OO for the failure of .NET is like blaming cats for the fact
that
> > there are mice in the house.
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > being in this lifetime, but then I have less remaining  lifetime than
many
> > of you reading this...<G>
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T05:59:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0401300559.e0dbdd9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12>`

```
"RH" <nospam@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12>...
> It's hard to explain because there's too much information to absorb.  OO
> theory by itself is just the beginning.  OO patterns theory is really where
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Let's return to the point of the original discussion. The conclusion,
then, is that XML is so complex that we need "OO" techniques to deal
with it - fine. And we will be using XML, partly because Microsoft is
marketing it.

Do we really need a self-describing interface for inter-process
communication? Consider how baroque COM already is. Look at what
happened to the originally simple HTML. See the crawling horrors of
HTML + DHTML + javascript on the web. I'm sure that someone will add a
programming language to XML as a method of specifying RPCs.

Here's a novel thought. Prefix data with the COBOL program that
created it. Transform and compile the program on the receiving end and
voila - instant data import! (grin).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T10:28:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401301028.623f28ac@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12>`

```
"RH" <nospam@hotmail.com> wrote

> -write a basic 3D rendering engine in C; go ahead and use APIs if you want.
> -then rewrite it in C++ or Delphi or even Java (although it won't be fast).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Carmack now uses C++ instead of C; and he's a harcore coder.

It is certain that C++ has some features that overcome the problems
and limitations of C.  It is also a given that for certain types of
applications (such as a 3D rendering engine) the facilities of C++
match the abstraction much more easily.  Basically, C++ allows the
programmer, via the OO facilities, to create a higher level
programming language that is more suitable for writing the program
than C ever would be.

The question is how does this apply to Cobol and business
applications.

Cobol is not C, it doesn't have the problems that C++ overcomes in C.

Cobol already is a business application programming language.  In many
cases it doesn't need the facility of being able to create a new
language specific to the problem set, it already is that.

The priciples of OO Design are very useful to apply to Cobol
programming, but arguing the deficiencies of C and the needs of a 3D
render do not translate directly into an argument supporting OO Cobol
for use on business applications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T22:59:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wlBSb.144115$6y6.2803761@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12>`

```

RH <nospam@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12...
> It's hard to explain because there's too much information to absorb.

Your statement almost obviates any further need for you to continue.
What you are saying to OO neophytes and wannabe proselytes alike
is that not only will they be faced with learning the business processes,
not only will they be faced with developing appopriate business solutions,
they will have to do so while struggling to learn a methodology
that cannot easily be rendered lucid by even experienced proponents.

That is a scathing indictment, period.  The issue of whether I or anyone else
is for or against procedural or OO technologies is rendered extraneous.

> OO theory by itself is just the beginning.  OO patterns theory is really where
> it comes into its own.

That adds clarity?

> But, here is something to try:
>
> -spend a few months learning basic calculus and geometry.

"Umm, excuse me, users.  I cannot give you that solution.
I have to pop off and spend a few months learning basic calculus and geometry.
Can you hang on?"

Duh!  I think not.

> This is not really any different than spending time
> learning some equivalent business theory.

I demur on that.  Most developers assimilate all the crash course theory
they need to solve most business problems PDQ.

Show me any company that is willing in this current business climate -
where they fire people right left and center and work the remainder
into the ground  and gloss it all over as a "productivity gain" -
to send people off on 3 month training sessions.  I challenge you.

> -write a basic 3D rendering engine in C; go ahead and use APIs if you want.
> -then rewrite it in C++ or Delphi or even Java (although it won't be fast).

COBOL  Common Business Oiented Language

I'm not going to enter into any long-drawn-out debate here
as to what does and doesn't constitute a business application,
so please just accept for whatever it is worth that I do not consider
a basic 3D rendering engine to be a business application
OR an application appropriate for a COBOL solution.

My point:  If you are asked in a COBOL group for a simplified OO
discourse or a treatise or an exposition, it is only common sense
to stay within the indicated framework of COBOL methodology.

That serves the dual purpose of helping to validate OO
as an appropriate approach to business solutions, doesn't it?

So, forget about 3D.  Take something fundamental and globally understood.
A payroll application, perhaps?  You have employees, timesheets/timecards,
timekeepers/payroll clerks, payroll master files, tax tables, paychecks,
reconciliation reports, W-2s, et cetera.  I may even recognize still recognize
those system components after you get done wrapping them in OO terminology,
but why not use a basic everyday application such as that, and provide a contrast
between the procedural and OO approaches?  In COBOL parlance, nat-chew-rally!

I know that I find comprehension of a given theory to be much more
readily attainable if and when I can walk through a real-life example.

"Too hard" doesn't cut it when you are talking about developing
and supporting mission-citical applications, and training your staff to do that.

> By the time you are done you'll be thinking "Holy Crap, who the heck would
> do something that complicated in C when you have OO language X."  Even John
> Carmack now uses C++ instead of C; and he's a harcore coder.

Even John Carmack!?  Really!?  John Carmack!!  Wow!!!!  Who?
>
> I actually did this, believe it or not.

I believe it.  Why?  How does this in any way relate to a discussion
of the application of OO techniques in a COBOL environment,
or to providing a clear, basic, comprehensible OO primer
that doesn't force your eyeballs through your skull by the 3rd sentence?.

> When I was done I had an
> understanding of why I'd never go back to orthodox procedural coding --
> under many circumstances --.  It still has its uses (ex: Perl).  But used
> properly it "just makes sense" (tm).
>
As does a well-founded, extensively-used common business oriented
language used procedurally.  You can even use it in sub-routines.

>
>
…[71 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T19:06:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0401301906.91125b6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <x0jSb.32838$P51.26823@clgrps12> <wlBSb.144115$6y6.2803761@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote 
 
> I'm not going to enter into any long-drawn-out debate here
> as to what does and doesn't constitute a business application,
> so please just accept for whatever it is worth that I do not consider
> a basic 3D rendering engine to be a business application
> OR an application appropriate for a COBOL solution.

As part of the Container Ship planning and operational system that I
developed I did do a redering of the hull shape described by the
bonjean curves in 3D as confirmation of the data that had been input. 
I also did a 3D render of the view over the containers from any
arbitrary point, usually the helm position.  All this in Cobol.  It
just ain't that hard and can be done iteritively.  ie it doesn't need
to have all the containers 'draw themselves'.

Not that I am saying that this is in any way a normal business
application.

However, with the current warehousing application it has been
discussed having a 3D view of the warehouse racks with pallets in
place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO? Oh oh!

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2004-01-31T21:26:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0401312126.5ca862f1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SOyRb.121471$6y6.2401423@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <4016e8da_6@news.athenanews.com> <Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net> wrote in message news:<Q0_Rb.502$zv7.75942111@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>...
> Having an understand of OO, maybe someone here can explain it
> to me in a nutshell. I have the background, and can swallow the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I know this wasn't directed to me, but....  This is a ODD thing, OO
"Understanding".  I submit that if you don not think it is
revolutionary then your first assertion "having an understand[SIC] of
OO..." just isn't true.

Until you have the "AHA" lightbulb goes on experiece, you just don't
get it.  Sorry - that's the way it is.  When you DO get it -
everything will fall into place and the SIMPLICITY and EASE of it will
become plain.  At it's core it IS simple, and that is it's elegence.

That said - where I think the FAILING is, is not in the concept of OO,
but in it's implementation by programmers.  If it is not properly
managed, one loses some of the advantages.  Properly managed, however,
it becomes a powerful paradigm.

Is it really NEW or is it "libraries" and such repackaged?  No - it is
really "NEW" - or as new as something over 30 years old can be.

I think OO is the only thing debated more than the death of COBOL.
```

## Thread: Micro Focus Cobol and Web Services Question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Micro Focus Cobol and Web Services Question

- **From:** agsantiago@snopud.com (Andy)
- **Date:** 2004-01-27T12:08:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1aec4564.0401271208.34447e10@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

First of all, I am not familiar with Micro Focus Cobol so please 
forgive the ignorance of my question.

Any rate, here is my situation.

We have a vendor package that was written using 3-tier architecture. 
The middle tier(business tier) is written in Micro Focus Cobol. It runs
is a Unix AIX server. The client is a windows application that passes
request to the business tier through Tuxedo. The business tier does all
the database service request to the Oracle backend.

My organization wants to expose the Micro Focus Cobol business tier as
web services that can be called by other applications(mostly .Net web
applications).

My question is this:
1. We would like to put a wrapper(Facade Pattern) around the legacy 
business tier. This wrapper will be exposed as web services. Could you
guide on how is it possible to do with Micro Focus Cobol? In other words,
does MF Cobol has the capability/tools? that would create a web service
application in Unix that calls a legacy Unix .dll? If so, can you share
samples/articles on how to do this?

2. Is this the best approach in exposing a legacy MF Cobol dll hosted in
Unix as web services? Please note that the dll(business tier) is a 
vendor software and we cannot modify it, only call it.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol and Web Services Question

- **From:** Vaclav Snajdr <vsn@snajdr.de>
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T08:11:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bv7mq0$t9i$03$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<1aec4564.0401271208.34447e10@posting.google.com>`

```
MF brings now the Server Express v4.0 for Unix/Linux - I think it
will do it.

Andy wrote:

> Hello,
> 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.
```

## Thread: Performance in perform loops — 14 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Performance in perform loops

- **From:** m6v100@web.de (Joern)
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T02:45:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello, I am looking for some tips in order to improve the performance
of the following COBOL code. Our investigation has shown, that the
section C032 is the most time consuming part of the whole program.
We spend more than 60% of the cpu time in the simple perform loops of
the 'C032-PRUEFE-BEDG' section.
  
The program works as designed and in my opinion all the coding is
needed for the correct performing. I am not a expert in COBOL
programming, so I am not sure if I can perform the code in a better
way. Maybe you can help me.
  



01  SWITCH-DIMSCHL-FOUND             PIC 9 COMP.
    88 SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND              VALUE 0.
    88 SW-DIMSCHL-FOUND                  VALUE 1.

01  WS-BED-HILFSFELDER.
    05  PS-TREFFER                   PIC S9(1) COMP.
        88 PS-TREFFER-NEIN           VALUE ZERO.
        88 PS-TREFFER-JA             VALUE 1.
    05  WS-FORMAT                    PIC X(1).
    05  WS-VFELD                     PIC X(10).
    ...
    05  ABG-END                      PIC S9(8) COMP.



01  MAX-FELDNR                       PIC 9(4) COMP VALUE 2000.
01  TB-FELD-TAB.
    03  TB-FELD-ELEMENT
        OCCURS 2000 INDEXED BY F-I1 F-I2.
        05  TB-FELDNR                PIC S9(9) COMP.
        05  TB-FELDPOS               PIC S9(9) COMP.
        05  TB-FELDLAENGE            PIC S9(9) COMP.
        05  TB-FELDFRM               PIC X.
        05  TB-SUCHFELD              PIC X(10).
        05  TB-SUCHFELD-N REDEFINES
                         TB-SUCHFELD PIC S9(18) COMP-3.


****************************************************************
  - TABELLE MIT DEN ABGRENZUNGEN
****************************************************************
01  MAX-ABG-ELEM                     PIC 9(9) COMP VALUE 400000.
01  TB-ABGRENZUNG.
    03  FILLER         OCCURS 400000 INDEXED BY ABG-IND.
        05  TB-ABG-FELD-INDEX         PIC S9(9) COMP.
        05  TB-ABG-FELDNR             PIC S9(9) COMP.
        05  TB-ABG-OPERATOR           PIC X.
        05  TB-ABG-ABGRVON            PIC X(10).
        05  TB-ABG-ABGRVON-N REDEFINES
            TB-ABG-ABGRVON            PIC S9(18) COMP-3.
        05  TB-ABG-ABGRBIS            PIC X(10).
        05  TB-ABG-ABGRBIS-N REDEFINES
            TB-ABG-ABGRBIS            PIC S9(18) COMP-3.



C032-PRUEFE-BEDG SECTION.
****************************************************************
    eine Bedingung aus TB-BED prï¿½fen (BED-IND):
****************************************************************
C032-BEG.

    SET  SW-DIMSCHL-FOUND TO TRUE
    MOVE ZERO TO WS-FELDNR-PREV
    SET  ABG-IND TO TB-BED-ANF (BED-IND)
    MOVE TB-BED-END (BED-IND) TO ABG-END

    PERFORM UNTIL ABG-IND > ABG-END
               OR SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND
       MOVE TB-ABG-FELDNR (ABG-IND) TO WS-FELDNR
       IF WS-FELDNR NOT = WS-FELDNR-PREV
          MOVE WS-FELDNR TO WS-FELDNR-PREV
          set  F-I1  TO TB-ABG-FELD-INDEX (ABG-IND)
          MOVE TB-FELDFRM  (F-I1)        TO WS-FORMAT
          MOVE TB-SUCHFELD (F-I1)        TO WS-VFELD
       END-IF

Bedingung prï¿½fen je Feld:
       PERFORM UNTIL TB-ABG-FELDNR (ABG-IND)
                     NOT = WS-FELDNR-PREV
                  OR ABG-IND > ABG-END
                  OR SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND
          IF TB-ABG-OPERATOR (ABG-IND) = 'A'
             IF WS-FORMAT = 'X'
                IF  WS-VFELD   NOT < TB-ABG-ABGRVON (ABG-IND)
                AND WS-VFELD   NOT > TB-ABG-ABGRBIS (ABG-IND)
                   SET SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE
                END-IF
             ELSE
                IF  WS-VFELD-N NOT < TB-ABG-ABGRVON-N(ABG-IND)
                AND WS-VFELD-N NOT > TB-ABG-ABGRBIS-N(ABG-IND)
                   SET SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE
                END-IF
             END-IF
             SET  ABG-IND UP BY 1
          ELSE
             SET PS-TREFFER-NEIN TO TRUE
             PERFORM UNTIL TB-ABG-FELDNR (ABG-IND)
                           NOT = WS-FELDNR-PREV
                        OR ABG-IND > ABG-END
                        OR PS-TREFFER-JA
                IF WS-FORMAT = 'X'
                   IF  WS-VFELD   NOT < TB-ABG-ABGRVON (ABG-IND)
                   AND WS-VFELD   NOT > TB-ABG-ABGRBIS (ABG-IND)
                      SET PS-TREFFER-JA TO TRUE
                   END-IF
                ELSE
                   IF  WS-VFELD-N NOT < TB-ABG-ABGRVON-N(ABG-IND)
                   AND WS-VFELD-N NOT > TB-ABG-ABGRBIS-N(ABG-IND)
                      SET PS-TREFFER-JA TO TRUE
                   END-IF
                END-IF
                SET  ABG-IND UP BY 1
             END-PERFORM
             IF PS-TREFFER-JA
                PERFORM UNTIL TB-ABG-FELDNR (ABG-IND)
                              NOT = WS-FELDNR-PREV
                            OR ABG-IND > ABG-END
                   SET  ABG-IND UP BY 1
                END-PERFORM
             ELSE
                SET SW-DIMSCHL-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE
             END-IF
          END-IF
       END-PERFORM

    END-PERFORM

    CONTINUE.
C032-EX.

As you see, the index abg-ind of the table is used.
Someone told me, that the comparisons of the index with the data item
abg-end (binary) in the "until" statements of the loops are very time
expensive. I don't have any idea, whether it is right and how I can
handle the comparison in a better way

Thanks in advance, Joern
```

Can you tell us what compiler and operating system you are using? (It often
makes a difference in "performance" questions).   Many compilers include a
"produce assembler listings" compiler options.  If you are trying to "micro
tune" code, this MAY provide useful information (assuming you have an assembler
resource person available).
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-01-28T14:36:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0401281436.3937ee7d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com>`

```
m6v100@web.de (Joern) wrote in message news:<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com>...
> Hello, I am looking for some tips in order to improve the performance
> of the following COBOL code. Our investigation has shown, that the
…[139 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance, Joern

Hello Joern

It is usually a good idea to indicate the operating system, hardware
and compiler that you are using, so that responders have more idea
what is good advice in your specific circumstances.  The advice that I
shall provide is appropriate for the IBM mainframe MVS COBOL compilers
that I have worked on.

I haven't gone into the fine detail of the logic and aim of the code,
but the following are some (what I think are) useful tips, mostly
taught to me by my first COBOL teacher, while I was learning on the
job over 30 years ago.

Try to remove as much as possible from the innermost loops, since
these are the most intensively executed and will show the greatest
benefit from any efficiency improvements.  In this case I can't see
any improvements of this nature that you can make to your code, but
you may know better.

Try to make sure that indexes and subscripts are in a binary format,
they are in this case, and that they are aligned on half-word,
full-word or double-word boundaries according to the sizes of the
field being used, in this case full word alignment would be
appropriate.  This is much less important than it used to be, but I
think it is still relevant.  This would mean that each table element
would need some filler bytes to force the appropriate alignment.  You
can obtain filler bytes either explicitly by specifying them directly
yourself, or by using the SYNCHRONIZED clause on the data definitions.
 I prefer to specify them explicitly, but that is my own preference. 
On older compilers you had to specify SYNCHRONIZED to avoid
inefficiency.  I suggest you refer to the programmers guide for your
compiler to see what that recommends.

You can use USAGE INDEX to define working-storage variables as index
data items for comparing with table indexes, there isn't much
inefficiency in using ordinary binary data items, but it avoids having
to make the conversions from index to subscript.  Indexes hold values
as displacements which have to be converted to equivalent subscript
values by multiplication by the size of the table element.  Index data
items have the same internal format as indexes so don't need this
multiplication step.

Make sure all fields being used as subscripts are signed, this makes
for faster comparisons by avoiding unnecessary checks and conversions.
 But if you convert the field to be compared with the indexes to index
data items, this won't be necessary.  Using signed numbers is
generally more efficient in all forms of arithmetic.

Try to avoid multiple resolutions of the same index value for
different data items in the same table element or multiple tests of
the same data item.  Resolving indexes and subscripts can involve
quite a high overhead, so should be minimised.  This can be done by
having a working-storage copy of a table element to which each table
element being processed is moved for further testing/processing and if
necessary moved back again into the table after any changes have been
made (you aren't making any changes to the table elements in this
example, so you don't need to do the reinstatements).  Obviously if
only one data item in a table item is being tested/processed this
isn't necessary, but your examples show cases of two and four being
tested.  Another way is to meke two or more associated items
subordinate to a group item and move that instead of doing two or more
independently subscripted separate moves.

Bear in mind that it is often more desirable to have easily readable
code than the fastest possible, it's usually faster to debug and amend
easily readable code, which is often more likely to be logically
correct.  Which is more important depends on your particular
circumstances.  However, it is not always the case that efficient code
is less easy to understand, so it is worth knowing what's efficient
and where and when to use it to best advantage.

Best of luck

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T07:14:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<naKdnYuz8smKmoTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com>`

```
Joern wrote:
> Hello, I am looking for some tips in order to improve the performance
> of the following COBOL code. Our investigation has shown, that the
> section C032 is the most time consuming part of the whole program.
> We spend more than 60% of the cpu time in the simple perform loops of
> the 'C032-PRUEFE-BEDG' section.

400,000 x 40 bytes = 16,000,000 byte array.

Are you sure your OS is not paging giant hunks of WS in and out of memory?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

- **From:** "The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T17:16:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qdbSb.12657$oN3.5870@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com> <naKdnYuz8smKmoTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com>`

```

Well, at least we know the hunks are always the same size, however
many. I'd seen the post from the OP, but I never just sit and look at
source submitted for approval, must be ADD. However, I think that
you've highlighted a valuable conclusion. If a serial search through a
400K entry table is performed frequently, it's going to be a busy
routine.

Perhaps the table is eligible for a binary search mechanism(search
all, in cobol parlance)? Or, maybe the table could be loaded into
a DBMS, draw a key on the search element, and find your entry
that way. At least you could shift all the load over to the DBMS,
and make you look clean<G>.


Gary

> The Family wrote:

> peppier processor.
Hello everybody, thank you very much for your replies. 

Of course it is important to indicate the operation system and
especially the used compiler. We work on a IBM mainframe system,
running under OS/390. The COBOL Compiler is the LE370 from IBM.
The compile options are :
 *PARMMVS PARM.COB=(RENT,RES,DATA(31),OPT,DYN,MAP,OFFSET, TRUNC(OPT))
 *PARMMVS PARM.LNK=(RENT,REUS,'AMODE=31,RMODE=ANY')

We know that we use very large tables, but we really need so much 
table elements. This Application has to handle a lot of rules, witch
are defined by the Controlling Department of a German bank. The
definition off all this rules are stored in DB2-Tables and at running
time in the table "TB-ABGRENZUNG". Of course, we already reached the
maximum table size, that Cobol can handle, but the OS can keep the WS
in memory.
Even though, the shown part of the listing is simple, we have to
handle a complex system.
The Table contains numeric and alphanumeric data and we can't perform
a binary search or such intelligent algorithms. We have to check more
accounting conditions (and / or combinations) for one rule. (From the
shown code snip, you can't recognize this)

For every call of section C032, we go through a small part of the
table.
 -> Begin on element:  SET ABG-IND TO TB-BED-ANF (BED-IND)   
 -> End at element:    MOVE TB-BED-END (BED-IND) TO ABG-END

regards Joern
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T13:25:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0401301325.275be436@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com> <naKdnYuz8smKmoTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com> <qdbSb.12657$oN3.5870@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <Iaednatqpp4xAYTdRVn-tA@giganews.com> <e224fa77.0401300657.69af4f36@posting.google.com>`

```
Bottom posting

> Hello everybody, thank you very much for your replies. 
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> regards Joern

Hello again, just some more thoughts!

Try to look at an assembler listing of the code your compiler produces
as it is to be run in production, i.e. with the optimiser, etc.  This
should show what optimisations have already been made by the compiler
and therefore don't need to be done manually.  If you don't know how
to get this listing, look at the compiler options for listing
facilities in the language reference manual and programmer's guide,
MAP and LIST are for the data and procedure divisions respectively,
you can put CONTROL MAP and NOMAP statements and CONTROL LIST and
NOLIST statements in your source code to limit the volume of the
listing.

Also check with the manuals that your compiler options are the most
appropriate that they could be, offhand they look OK already, but
another check wouldn't hurt.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T22:06:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BzASb.3777$F23.1956@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com> <naKdnYuz8smKmoTdRVn-uQ@giganews.com> <qdbSb.12657$oN3.5870@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <Iaednatqpp4xAYTdRVn-tA@giganews.com> <e224fa77.0401300657.69af4f36@posting.google.com>`

```
Can you tell me exactly WHAT your compiler listing shows at the top of each page
as the compiler?  You say that you are using
  " Compiler is the LE370 from IBM"

but you also say that you are using the RES compiler option.  VS COBOL II
(pre-LE - although it can run with the LE run-time library) was the last
compiler to support the RES compiler option.  There HAVE been many compiler
"performance enhancements" in the compilers after that, i.e.
   IBM SAA AD/Cycle COBOL/370
   IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
   IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
   IBM Enterprise COBOL for OS/390 & z/OS

The last of these compilers is the "most current" compiler *and* includes the
greatest number of performance improvements.

FYI -
  It might also be useful for us to see the entire "options in effect" section
of your listing - rather than just the JCL "overrides".  It is possible that you
have a "poor" option as the default.

Finally, if you ARE using the SEARCH statement (which I can't remember) and the
table is NOT already sorted so you can use SEARCH ALL (which will be MUCH more
efficient with a large table), then I suggest that you look at:

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.12.10.2

For an example of how you can use the SORT statement with input/output
procedures to SORT a table in Working-Storage.
```
