_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-01-31T08:18:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bvg9vg$5lv$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com> <Iaednatqpp4xAYTdRVn-tA@giganews.com> <8a2d426e.0401300702.63bc43b3@posting.google.com> <e224fa77.0401310333.17daf522@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <e224fa77.0401310333.17daf522@posting.google.com>,
Joern <m6v100@web.de> wrote:
>jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight) wrote in message news:<8a2d426e.0401300702.63bc43b3@posting.google.com>...
>> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<Iaednatqpp4xAYTdRVn-tA@giganews.com>...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As an introduction... it sounds like your processing needs - for whatever 
reason or reasons, be it increased business volume or increased user 
demands - have outgrown your original designs and specifications, causing 
people to seek 'the simple way out' instead of doing the messy work of 
redesign.  (This frequently happens to businesses that are seeking to 
incorporate the processing of a newly-purchased subsidiary under their old 
systems, not realising that the increased transaction flow exceeds their 
original design limits.)  What is most frequently applied under such 
conditions is a messy, ugly patch which will hold up long enough for the 
Idiot who sits in the corner office to get promoted up to another floor, 
dropping the ticking time-bomb of a mess into his successor's lap... who, 
of course, will promptly blame the 'stupid programmers' for creating such 
a situation.

That bit of 'social commentary' having been said... in most businesses a
smaller subset of all possible cases meets the greater majority of all
actual cases... the adage of '20% of your situations consume 80% of your
energy' gets reversed, becoming '80% of your situations consume 20% of
your energy'.  If you are doing table-lookups on combined conditions then 
it might be that certain of these combinations occur frequently enough to 
merit a smaller table, one which might be designed in a fashion which 
allows a SEARCH ALL, that is checked before the larger table is 
sequentially searched.

(This technique is similar to checking an incoming records key field 
against the last-key-read on an indexed file; if the keys are the same 
there is no need for a second read.)

A few runs against old data for analysis would show if this suggestion is 
feasible.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance in perform loops

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "The Family" <lgvwalk@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-31T19:33:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tqTSb.12121$7R.6281@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<e224fa77.0401280245.1fd41f48@posting.google.com> <Iaednatqpp4xAYTdRVn-tA@giganews.com> <8a2d426e.0401300702.63bc43b3@posting.google.com> <e224fa77.0401310333.17daf522@posting.google.com> <bvg9vg$5lv$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


Would I detect some degree of cynicism in them thar' comments<g>

But, you are probably right, both with the "patch" assessment, as well
as the "technique outlived the design".

However, your other suggestion of breaking the table into multiple
objects might be possible, perhaps with a direct indexing scheme
gained from the first to others. I have used this in the past, where
an initial table may contain a group subset of entries. Once the cor-
rect group is identified, an index to the group detail entries is extr-
acted, and used for the detail table reference.

Depending on the requirement(s), this really can be a simple change,
that can produce vast improvements in performance, where the per-
formance is an issue.

Of course, since you're dealing with absolutes, maintaining this look-
up technique can produce a whole new set of challenges. Probably
should be very well documented.

Thanks - Gary

two parents, three teenagers into the same VW Beetle which held the same 
two parents and two toddlers 15 years ago was not going to be a satisfying 
long-term solution I was told 'Ha ha, good point, yeah, right... now are 
you going to do what you said or will we have to steal your idea, fire you 
and bring in someone else to do the job?'

>
>Depending on the requirement(s), this really can be a simple change,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>should be very well documented.

Even better... the 'coding culture' which produced the present program 
seems to be confortable with table-searches so that a new search, while 
changing the flow of a Very Important Program slightly, changes it with a 
familiar technique.

DD

>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bvg9vg$5lv$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Add an Online Update feature for your software
http://lanssoft.com/updatenow.htm

Update Now! Control makes an easy way to add an Online Update feature
for your software. It was designed to be as programmer-friendly as
possible, by providing features such as no user input to run in the
background and event-driven notifications.

Feature highlights: 

Download now! is completely API compilation, you won't need additional
attachment Microsoft WinSock Control;
Supporting proxy server and its checking function; 
Supporting Resume broken downloads; 
Perfect status events, Is so convenient for you to write out more
friendly interface;
Supplying beautiful download progress bar; 
Download now! it is Asynchronous operation, namely you don't need to
wait for the completion of the download threads, you can deal with
other tasks at the same time, The control will process the download
job at the
```

<a id="t-5a3c43f8ec"></a>
## Thread: IBM Mainframe JCL Conversion Tools — 3 this month / 54 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM Mainframe JCL Conversion Tools

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2004-01-29T16:09:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ohhSb.561$b77.1266@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
Does anyone know of tools which will convert the horrible JCL construct 
COND= to IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... ENDIF?

We can't forbid the use of COND= in our JCL Standards Checker (which is 
written in COBOL, so that I'm not 100% off topic), because there is so 
much use of COND= in existing jobs.

If I could get all existing uses converted, then we could prohibit the 
keyword entirely.
Thanks,
Colin
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe JCL Conversion Tools

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@NoMoSpam.ORG>
- **Date:** 2004-01-30T21:15:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QcWdnRrW3Y5-gIbdRVn_vw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ohhSb.561$b77.1266@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
Just a question here. I love the ability to use IF
statements in JCL and do use them frequently. However,
I also routinely put COND=(0,NE) on just about every
step to prevent it from executing if a prior step
ended with a condition code greater than zero. I've
seen this in a lot of shops. It seems do me having
to enclose each step with an IF ENDIF sequence to
accomplish the same thing would be more confusing and
complicated than the COND parameter. Would you really
want to prevent COND from being used in that manner?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe JCL Conversion Tools

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-01-31T13:54:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3sOSb.6506$BA2.798@newssvr26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<ohhSb.561$b77.1266@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <QcWdnRrW3Y5-gIbdRVn_vw@giganews.com>`

```
"Ron" <NoSpam@NoMoSpam.ORG> wrote in message
news:QcWdnRrW3Y5-gIbdRVn_vw@giganews.com...
> Just a question here. I love the ability to use IF
> statements in JCL and do use them frequently. However,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> want to prevent COND from being used in that manner?

Not sure ... but it seems to me mixing IF..ELSE with COND= in a single job
might make maintenance a real 'challenge' for the next poor schlub.

MCM
```

<a id="t-5be405420b"></a>
## Thread: File size / record count — 1 this month / 5 total

### File size / record count

- **From:** "Henk" <hnkatmweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 2004-01-31T18:19:49+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<401bd5b0.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
Hi

I need to split a file in 2 I have looked at DFSORT but it will distribute
the file as evenly as it can between the 2 datasets.
I.E. I need to split the file into 2 without changing the order of the
records as it is already in sorted sequence when I split it.

Is there a way to get a dataset attributes specifically the size with COBOL
LE on Z/OS. If I can get the file size and divide it by the record length
wich is fixed block I can calculate exactly how many records it contain and
create sysin cards for idcams to split the file into 2.

I need to find out how many records the file contains without reading
through the file. The reason why I don't know the total records is because
before the split step all duplicates is removed by icetool so a new file is
created.

Any other ideas to split the file fast is welcome.

Thanks
Henk
```
